BCG X Agentic Coding Field Guide

If you use AI coding tools every day, you already know the speed-up is real. A task that used to take hours lands in minutes. You sketch an idea and working code comes back. That speed is impressive, but it creates a new challenge: code can arrive faster than your understanding of it. PRs get larger, reviews can take longer than the implementation, and you start merging things you would struggle to explain later. The speed is real, but so is the creeping loss of oversight.

At AI speed, the hard part is not creating code. It is giving agents enough structure and context to produce work you can trust.

Teams that do this well do not rely on what the assistant happens to remember from the current chat. They persist decisions in files the agent can return to later, give it well-scoped, agent-friendly context, and maintain traceability so requirements stay connected to plans, code, tests, and review. This field guide is built to support that way of working.

  • Support across tools: The same workflows run across Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, and GitHub Copilot via slash commands (/name) or skills ($name). See Setup Guide.

  • A spec-first way of working: Work begins with generating implementation-ready specs, not prompting code ad-hoc. Requirements, constraints, interfaces, and edge cases are labeled and testable. See Spec (LLD).

  • Traceability from intent to review: IDs across specs, requirements, tests, tasks, and other project artifacts propagate through plan steps, validations, and review, so both the agent and the reviewer can follow the work end to end. See Plan (Implementation) and PR Review.

  • Deliberate context management: .claude/context/ is structured for selective loading and token efficiency, with explicit maintenance workflows See Context.

  • Persisted artifacts over chat-only decisions: Specs, plans, blueprints, and ADRs are persisted under artifacts/ so decisions remain inspectable across sessions and tools. See Artifacts Overview.

The core loop is simple: Design → Plan → Implement → Review. That is how teams keep AI-assisted development fast without losing clarity or control.

Quick Start

Section titled “Quick Start”

Start here:

  • Setup Guide - Prerequisites and integrations (Jira, GitHub, project initialization)
  • Workflows - How to run Design → Plan → Implement → Review with the included commands

  • Tickets Guide - Ticket-level specs, plans, and research notes
  • Blueprints Guide - Initiative-level design and decomposition

Getting Started

Section titled “Getting Started”
  • Using the workflows: Start with the Setup Guide, then follow the Workflows guide
  • Contributing to this repository: See the Contributing Guide

Field Guide Overview

What this is

Section titled “What this is”

This repository is a field guide for running agentic coding workflows with modern AI-assisted development tools.

This guide provides repeatable ways to go from intent to verified code changes using durable artifacts and clear stage gates.

It supports teams working across Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, and GitHub Copilot (GHCP), and it focuses on the real bottlenecks that appear at AI speed: collaboration under compressed PR cycles and a lead’s evolving responsibility for QA at the level of architecture, trade-offs, and intent.

The Setup Guide includes a straightforward mechanism to add workflows and skills to your repo’s agent configuration, including both slash-command and $skill invocation styles.

Who it’s for

Section titled “Who it’s for”
  • Tech leads / architects driving design and decomposition (blueprints → tickets)
  • Individual contributors implementing tickets with tight feedback loops
  • Anyone who wants a repeatable way to go from intent to verified code changes

How to use it

Section titled “How to use it”

The core loop is:

This isn’t a single “one size fits all” workflow — it’s a repeatable loop you can apply at different levels of scope:

  • Ticket implementation (most common): run the loop to take one ticket from intent to a verified PR.
  • Higher-level planning: run the same loop to turn an initiative into a coherent plan and a backlog that multiple tickets can execute.

The stages map to durable artifacts and clear gates:

  1. Design: clarify what to build (requirements, constraints, approach).
  2. Plan: break the work into verifiable steps (acceptance criteria, test strategy, sequencing).
  3. Implement: execute the plan with tight feedback loops (tests + code).
  4. Review: reconcile outcomes with intent (PR review, acceptance criteria, follow-ups).

This field guide includes two workflows that apply this pattern at different scopes: the Lead Workflow (initiative-level design and decomposition: blueprints → epics/tickets) and the Developer Workflow (implement a single ticket end-to-end).

As you run the loop, produce artifacts (specs, plans, research notes) under artifacts/, verify continuously by keeping acceptance criteria close to code and tests, and use human review gates to reconcile outcomes with intent. Use Principles to keep context scoped and verification explicit.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”
  • Clear, durable artifacts (specs and plans you can pick up later)
  • Small, verifiable steps with explicit acceptance criteria
  • Context scoped to the task and stage (avoid “everything everywhere all at once”)
  • Automated checks where possible, with human judgment where it matters

Contributing

Section titled “Contributing”

If you’re evolving this repository itself, start with the Contributing Guide. Common contributions include:

  • Improving workflow skills in .claude/skills/ and lean command entrypoints in .claude/commands/
  • Refining templates in templates/
  • Tightening docs and examples under docs/

Related Documentation

Section titled “Related Documentation”
  • Setup Guide - Prerequisites and integrations
  • Workflows - SDLC overview and reading order
  • Commands Index - Compact list of commands
  • Artifacts Overview - Where specs, plans, and blueprints live
  • Tickets Guide - Ticket-level artifact conventions
  • Architecture Decisions (ADRs) - Record cross-cutting decisions

Principles

These principles apply across the whole field guide. They’re intentionally high-level so you can “zoom in” into the workflow and artifact guides when you need step-by-step detail.

1. Work in stages, keep outputs durable

Section titled “1. Work in stages, keep outputs durable”

The field guide is organized around:

The most important idea isn’t the names of the stages; it’s that each stage produces a durable artifact you can re-open later:

  • Design produces a clear spec or design notes.
  • Plan produces a step-by-step checklist with acceptance criteria.
  • Implement produces code and tests tied back to the plan.
  • Review reconciles the work against the spec, plan, and standards.

See Workflows for the stage-by-stage walkthroughs.

2. Scope context to the task

Section titled “2. Scope context to the task”

Agentic tools are most effective when context is deliberately scoped:

  • Prefer “just enough” codebase context for the step you’re on.
  • Use artifact files (spec.md, plan.md, ticket.md) as the primary handoff mechanism.
  • If a thread gets noisy or stuck, restart with a smaller prompt and explicit pointers.

See Context for how .claude/context/ is structured to be agent-navigable and token-efficient.

3. Make verification explicit

Section titled “3. Make verification explicit”

Treat verification as a first-class output:

  • Write acceptance criteria in the spec/plan, not in chat history.
  • Prefer tests and reproducible checks over “looks good”.
  • Use human review gates to prevent drift and catch mismatches early.

4. Iterate on purpose

Section titled “4. Iterate on purpose”

Retries are a feature, not a failure mode:

  • If output quality is low, change constraints (scope, inputs, examples) rather than editing prose endlessly.
  • Record decisions in artifacts so you don’t re-litigate them across sessions.

Setup Guide

This guide covers prerequisites and setup required to use the workflow entrypoints and skills in this field guide effectively.

How It Works

Section titled “How It Works”

The workflow commands connect your AI coding assistant to Jira and GitHub, enabling a seamless development workflow:

The AI agent orchestrates the development workflow by:

  • Pulling tickets from Jira to start work
  • Pushing tickets back to Jira (creating epics and tasks from blueprints)
  • Creating PRs on GitHub after implementation
  • Reviewing PRs with automated code review

Install the Workflows

Section titled “Install the Workflows”

These workflows are designed to work across Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, and GitHub Copilot (GHCP). The installer is the simplest way to add the entrypoints and skills to your own repository’s agent configuration, with optional platform-specific integration outputs where applicable.

Run the installer from the root of your repo. This installs the workflow commands and skills into .claude/, scaffolds the artifacts/ directory structure, and can optionally set up platform integrations (GitHub Copilot prompt files, Codex skill discovery).

Our repository is hosted on EMU, so you’ll need a GitHub token to download the install script.

If you already have GitHub CLI (gh) installed and authenticated, you can populate GITHUB_TOKEN from it:

If not, set GITHUB_TOKEN manually with a token that can read this repository, then continue with the installer. The full GitHub CLI setup steps are covered later in this guide.

Run:

You can rerun that same command later from the root of your repo to update to the latest version of the field guide workflows and skills. By default, the installer pulls from main, so rerunning it is the normal way to refresh to the newest version on that branch. Use --dry-run first if you want to preview the update, and use --ref <ref> only when you want to pin to a specific branch, tag, or commit instead of the latest main.

The installer keeps a small bill of materials (BOM) in .claude/.genx-bom to track the files it manages. On later runs, it uses that BOM to identify installer-managed files, update unchanged files, and remove stale installer-managed files that no longer exist upstream. Local changes are not silently discarded: if an installer-managed file needs to be replaced, the current version is backed up under .genx-backup/<timestamp>/ before overwrite. For generated platform files such as GitHub Copilot prompt wrappers, the installer only overwrites files it originally generated. If you already have your own file at a platform-managed path, such as .github/prompts/spec.prompt.md, the installer leaves it in place unless you pass --force. When --force replaces a conflicting regular file, the existing file is backed up first. The current BOM format also normalizes whitespace for managed text files, so pure reformatting changes such as different line wrapping are treated as whitespace-equivalent during hash comparison. If the canonical source bytes still differ, the installer rewrites the file from the source content and backs up the previous version first.

Optional flags (after the -- passed to bash -s):

--dry-run:

Prints what the installer would change, without writing files. Use this before rerunning the installer if you want to review an update first.

--ref <ref>:

Installs from a specific Git ref (branch, tag, or commit SHA) instead of main.

--platform <platform>:

Generates optional, platform-specific integration files in addition to the standard .claude/ install.

Codex skill discovery:

Creates .agents/skills/ symlinks pointing to .claude/skills/.

GitHub Copilot (GHCP) prompts:

Generates prompt files in .github/prompts/ (e.g. *.prompt.md) that reference .claude/commands/.

All supported integrations:

Runs all supported integration outputs.

--force:

Forces replacement of conflicting, non-generated platform files (for example, a prompt file you created yourself at a path the installer would otherwise generate). This is not required for normal updates.

How to Invoke Workflows by Tool

Section titled “How to Invoke Workflows by Tool”

All workflow behavior is defined in .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md.

Use the invocation style your tool supports:

Tool modeInvocation styleExample
Slash-command tools/name/spec PROJ-123
Skill-invocation tools (including Codex)$name$spec PROJ-123

Equivalent examples:

Command files in .claude/commands/ are lean wrappers that forward to same-name skills.

Integration Setup

Section titled “Integration Setup”

After installing the workflows, complete these steps to enable all workflow features.

1. Atlassian MCP Setup (for Jira Workflows)

Section titled “1. Atlassian MCP Setup (for Jira Workflows)”

The Jira workflow commands (/pull-ticket, /ticket-to-jira) require the Atlassian MCP (Model Context Protocol) server to be configured and authenticated.

Why MCP is Required

Section titled “Why MCP is Required”
  • Direct API access to Jira for reading and creating tickets
  • Secure authentication through the MCP infrastructure
  • No need to manage API tokens manually

Configuration Steps

Section titled “Configuration Steps”

  1. Open your IDE settings

    • In Cursor: Cursor Settings → Features → MCP Servers
    • In other IDEs: Check MCP server configuration in settings

  2. Add Atlassian MCP Server

    • Add the Atlassian MCP server
    • Follow the authentication prompts

  3. Authenticate with Atlassian

    • You’ll be redirected to Atlassian’s OAuth flow
    • Grant the necessary permissions for Jira access
    • Return to your IDE to complete setup

  4. Verify Connection

    • The Atlassian server should show “Connected” status
    • Test by running a simple Jira query in the agent

Troubleshooting

Section titled “Troubleshooting”

If MCP is not available:

Common issues:

  • Authentication expired: Re-authenticate through your IDE settings
  • Permission denied: Ensure your Atlassian account has access to the Jira project
  • Server not connected: Restart your IDE and check MCP server status

2. GitHub CLI Setup (for PR Workflows)

Section titled “2. GitHub CLI Setup (for PR Workflows)”

The PR workflow command (/pr-review) requires GitHub CLI (gh) to be installed and authenticated.

macOS:

Windows:

Linux:

Section titled “Authentication”

Verify Authentication

Section titled “Verify Authentication”

Required Token Scopes

Section titled “Required Token Scopes”

If using a Personal Access Token instead of browser auth:

ScopePurpose
repoFull control of private repositories
read:orgRead org and team membership
workflowUpdate GitHub Action workflows

Generate a token at: https://github.com/settings/tokens

Project Initialization

Section titled “Project Initialization”

After installing the workflows and configuring the required integrations, initialize your repo-specific agent context.

Using /generate-agents or $generate-agents

Section titled “Using /generate-agents or $generate-agents”

The /generate-agents command creates a customized AGENTS.md file from the template, which provides project context to the AI agent.

Step 1: Fill Out the Template

Section titled “Step 1: Fill Out the Template”

Before running the command, edit .claude/skills/generate-agents/AGENTS-template.md with your project information:

Key parameters to fill:

ParameterExampleDescription
PROJECT_NAMEretail-data-demoYour project name
PROJECT_KEYRETAILYour Jira project key
TEAM_NAMEEngineeringYour team name
ATLASSIAN_ORGyourcompanyYour Atlassian organization
ATLASSIAN_CLOUD_IDabc123-def456Your Atlassian Cloud ID
DEFAULT_BRANCHmainDefault git branch

Step 2: Run the Workflow

Section titled “Step 2: Run the Workflow”

The command will:

  1. Read .claude/skills/generate-agents/AGENTS-template.md
  2. Extract parameter values
  3. Replace all placeholders throughout the template
  4. Generate AGENTS.md with your project configuration

Step 3: Verify Output

Section titled “Step 3: Verify Output”

Check the generated AGENTS.md file:

  • Project-specific Jira configuration
  • Correct repository URLs
  • Team and author information
  • Default branch settings

Verify Your Setup

Section titled “Verify Your Setup”

Run these checks to confirm the install, integrations, and generated AGENTS.md are working together:

CheckCommandExpected Result
AGENTS.md existsls AGENTS.mdFile exists with your project config
Jira connection/pull-ticket {TICKET-ID} or $pull-ticket {TICKET-ID}Creates artifacts/tickets/{TICKET-ID}/ticket.md (may prompt to transition status)
GitHub CLIgh auth statusShows “Logged in” status

Test Project Configuration

Section titled “Test Project Configuration”

Verify AGENTS.md was generated correctly:

  1. Check that AGENTS.md exists in your project root
  2. Verify Jira project key is correct
  3. Confirm repository URL matches your repo

Test Jira Connection

Section titled “Test Jira Connection”

Run a simple ticket query to verify Jira access:

Expected: Should pull that ticket from Jira and create artifacts/tickets/{TICKET-ID}/ticket.md.

If it fails: Check Atlassian MCP configuration in your IDE settings.

Test GitHub Connection

Section titled “Test GitHub Connection”

Verify GitHub CLI is working:

If it fails: Run gh auth login to re-authenticate.

Contributing Guide

This guide covers how to work with and contribute to this repository. For using the workflow commands in your own projects, see the Workflows guide.

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”

Before contributing, ensure you have:

  • Git installed and configured
  • Pre-commit installed (pip install pre-commit)
  • Access to the repository

See the Setup Guide for complete environment setup including Jira and GitHub integration.

Git Hooks (Pre-commit)

Section titled “Git Hooks (Pre-commit)”

This project uses pre-commit for git hooks to automatically run quality checks before commits:

The pre-commit hooks will automatically:

  • Fix trailing whitespace
  • Ensure files end with newlines
  • Check YAML files
  • Check for large files
  • Check for merge conflicts

Mermaid Diagrams

Section titled “Mermaid Diagrams”

This docs site supports Mermaid diagrams via fenced code blocks:

Diagrams render client-side and automatically follow the current Starlight light/dark theme. Mermaid is loaded client-side (see docs/src/scripts/mermaid.client.js). By default, it tries a local /vendor/mermaid.esm.min.mjs first and falls back to public CDNs.

Using the Scaffolding

Section titled “Using the Scaffolding”

With Slash-Command Tools

Section titled “With Slash-Command Tools”

Slash entrypoints are available:

  • /implement - Execute plan step-by-step with verification

With Skill-Invocation Tools (Including Codex)

Section titled “With Skill-Invocation Tools (Including Codex)”

Use $skill-name invocation with skills in .claude/skills/ (or .agents/skills/ symlinks when installed for Codex).

With Cursor IDE

Section titled “With Cursor IDE”

Rules are in .cursor/rules. Lean command entrypoints are in .claude/commands/, and workflow behavior is in .claude/skills/.

Working on Changes in This Repo

Section titled “Working on Changes in This Repo”

This page focuses on contributing mechanics (tooling, quality checks, and docs site development).

For workflow usage and artifact conventions, see:

  • Workflows Overview — SDLC and how to navigate the workflow docs
  • Commands Index — workflow entrypoint reference (/ and $)
  • Artifacts Overview — where specs, plans, blueprints, and ADRs live
  • Tickets Guide — ticket-level specs, plans, and research notes
  • Architecture Decisions (ADRs) — when and how to record decisions

Docs Site Development

Section titled “Docs Site Development”

The documentation in the docs/ folder is built with Astro/Starlight and deployed to GitHub Pages. To preview it locally:

Prerequisites:

  • Node.js 22.12.0+ (install via nvm or brew install node)
  • npm or pnpm

Run the docs site locally from the docs/ directory:

Workflows Overview

This field guide ships two complementary workflows—one for leads who plan features and one for developers who implement tickets. Both follow the same four-stage lifecycle, share the same artifact conventions, and enforce human review gates between stages.

This page explains the model. The role-specific pages (Developer, Lead) walk you through each workflow step by step.

The core idea: Design → Plan → Implement → Review

Section titled “The core idea: Design → Plan → Implement → Review”

Every piece of work—whether it is a multi-epic initiative or a single bug fix—moves through four stages:

StageQuestion it answersKey outputs
DesignWhat should we build?PRD, blueprint, spec
PlanHow should we build it?Ticket decomposition, implementation plan
ImplementBuild itCode, tests, commits
ReviewIs it correct and complete?PR review, traceability check

The stages are sequential on purpose. Each one narrows scope and removes ambiguity for the next, so the agent (and the developer) can execute mechanically instead of making design decisions mid-flight.

Between stages, a human reviews. The spec review (design → plan) is a hard gate — the lead approves before planning begins. The plan review (plan → implement) is lighter: if the spec review was thorough, the developer can proceed directly. The agent never silently advances — you decide when to proceed.

Two workflows, one lifecycle

Section titled “Two workflows, one lifecycle”

The lifecycle splits into two workflows based on the granularity of work:

  • Lead Workflow — takes an initiative from requirements through architecture to a structured backlog of epics and tickets.
  • Developer Workflow — takes a single ticket from spec through plan to merged code.
  • Overarching Commands — cross-cutting commands (ADR, Update Context) that can be used at any stage by any role.

The lead workflow feeds the developer workflow: blueprints decompose into tickets, which developers pull and implement. But neither workflow requires the other—you can use /spec → /plan → /implement on any ticket, whether it came from a blueprint or was created manually in Jira.

Traceability: the thread that connects everything

Section titled “Traceability: the thread that connects everything”

A recurring theme across both workflows is traceability—the ability to follow a requirement from its origin through design, plan, code, and test:

  1. Spec labels every requirement with a unique ID (e.g. F001-AUTH-TOKEN, E002-TIMEOUT).
  2. Plan references those IDs in each implementation step, so you know why a step exists.
  3. Tests reference spec IDs, so you can verify that every requirement has coverage.
  4. PR review walks the chain end to end: intent → spec → plan → code → tests.

This is not bureaucracy for its own sake. When the lead reviews a PR, traceability lets them answer “did we build what we agreed to build?” mechanically—by checking IDs—rather than re-reading the entire ticket and guessing.

How to get started

Section titled “How to get started”

If you are a developer picking up a ticket:

  1. Read the Developer Workflow overview
  2. Run /pull-ticket {TICKET-ID} to begin
  3. Follow the steps: /spec → lead review → /plan → /implement → /pr-review

If you are a lead starting a new initiative:

  1. Read the Lead Workflow overview
  2. Run /prd (optional) and /blueprint to design the architecture
  3. Run /blueprint-to-tickets to create the backlog, then /ticket-to-jira to export

For a quick reference of every command:

  • See the Commands Index

Workflow principles

Section titled “Workflow principles”

These ideas show up repeatedly across both workflows:

  • Separate what from how. Design (spec) decides what to build. Planning decides how. Implementation just follows the plan. Mixing these leads to scope drift and rework.
  • One task at a time. /implement works through plan steps sequentially, validating each one before moving on. This keeps the agent focused and makes progress visible.
  • Make verification explicit. Every plan step has a concrete validation (a test command, a type check, a build). “Looks good” is not a validation.
  • Keep context scoped. Each stage loads only the artifacts it needs. See Principles for practical guidance on managing context.
  • Automate the repeatable, gate the consequential. The agent handles mechanical work (file generation, test execution, Jira sync). Humans approve at design boundaries; plan review is optional when the spec was thorough.
  • Review promptly. Developers are blocked at review gates. Slow reviews negate the efficiency the workflow provides.

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”

Some commands need external integrations:

  • Atlassian MCP — required for /pull-ticket and /ticket-to-jira
  • GitHub CLI (gh) — required for /pr-review and automated PR creation via /implement

See the Setup Guide for configuration instructions. Commands that need these tools will tell you if they are missing.

Overarching commands

Section titled “Overarching commands”

Some commands are not tied to a single workflow stage—they apply across the entire lifecycle:

  • ADR — capture architecture decisions as Architecture Decision Records under artifacts/architecture/.
  • Update Context — maintain and extend the .claude/context structure so agents always have up-to-date project context.

These can be run at any point by any role.

Reading order

Section titled “Reading order”
OrderPageWhat you will learn
1Developer WorkflowHow a single ticket gets implemented end to end
2Lead WorkflowHow to go from initiative to Jira epics and tickets
3Overarching CommandsCross-cutting commands (ADR, Update Context)
4Commands IndexQuick reference for all workflow entrypoints

Developer Workflow

What this workflow is for

Section titled “What this workflow is for”

The developer workflow is how a single ticket becomes working, reviewed code. You use it whenever you pick up a ticket—whether it came from a lead’s blueprint or was created manually in Jira.

The workflow enforces a simple discipline: decide what to build (spec), decide how to build it (plan), then build it (implement). These are deliberately separate steps because mixing them is where most rework comes from. When a developer starts coding before the design is agreed, they discover mid-implementation that the scope was wrong, a dependency was missed, or the approach doesn’t fit the codebase. The three-step split prevents that.

Use either invocation style depending on your tool:

  • Slash-command tools: /pull-ticket, /spec, /plan, /implement, /pr-review
  • Skill-invocation tools (including Codex): $pull-ticket, $spec, $plan, $implement, $pr-review

Walking through the workflow

Section titled “Walking through the workflow”

Step 1: Pull the ticket — /pull-ticket {TICKET-ID}

Section titled “Step 1: Pull the ticket — /pull-ticket {TICKET-ID}”

Everything starts with context. /pull-ticket fetches the Jira ticket, writes it to artifacts/tickets/{ticket-id}/ticket.md, and creates a feature branch. This gives you and the agent a shared, durable reference for what was asked — no more re-reading Jira mid-conversation.

Before you move on, skim the ticket and any linked PRD or blueprint. The agent will read these too, but your job throughout this workflow is to supply context the agent can’t see: verbal decisions from standup, constraints from other teams, or domain knowledge that isn’t written down.

  • Output: artifacts/tickets/{ticket-id}/ticket.md, feature branch
  • Prerequisites: Atlassian MCP (only needed for /pull-ticket; without it, skip straight to /spec)
  • Deep dive: Pull Ticket — prerequisites, branch naming, and handoff

Step 2: Write the spec — /spec

Section titled “Step 2: Write the spec — /spec”

The spec is the contract between “what we agreed to build” and “what actually gets built.” It labels every requirement with a unique ID (like F001-PARSE-CSV or E003-TIMEOUT) so that nothing is ambiguous and everything is traceable downstream.

The agent produces the spec from the ticket context and codebase. Your review is what makes it valuable: Does the scope match your understanding? Are the edge cases complete? Are the design decisions (interfaces, data models) sound for this codebase?

After you’re satisfied, submit the spec for lead review. The lead checks alignment with broader project goals. Once approved, you move to planning.

  • Output: artifacts/tickets/{ticket-id}/spec.md — labeled requirements, interfaces, test strategy
  • Deep dive: Spec (LLD) — labels, traceability, and review guidance

Step 3: Create the plan — /plan

Section titled “Step 3: Create the plan — /plan”

The plan eliminates decision-making during implementation. It pre-decides which files change, in what order, and how each step is validated — so that /implement can execute mechanically.

Each plan step (P###) cites the spec IDs it implements and includes a runnable validation command. This is the traceability thread in action: a reviewer can walk from requirement to plan step to test and verify that nothing was lost.

When reviewing the plan, focus on judgment calls the agent can’t make well: Is this the right approach for the codebase? Are there simpler alternatives or existing patterns it should reuse? Is the step granularity reasonable — each step should be completable and verifiable in one sitting.

You always run /plan — /implement needs plan.md as input. But the lead review of the plan is optional. If the spec was already approved and you’re confident in the approach, proceed straight to /implement. If the ticket involves unfamiliar territory or risky architectural choices, ask your lead to review the plan before you start implementing.

  • Output: artifacts/tickets/{ticket-id}/plan.md — P-steps with validations, W-entries for files, K-checkpoints
  • Deep dive: Plan (Implementation) — P/W/K/X structure, traceability, and review guidance

Step 4: Build it — /implement

Section titled “Step 4: Build it — /implement”

Now the agent works through the plan step by step, implementing changes and adding tests, then running each step’s validation before moving to the next. You watch progress in the plan’s checkboxes. If a validation fails, the agent fixes the issue before advancing — no “I’ll come back to it later.”

This is where the earlier discipline pays off. Because the spec locked down what to build and the plan locked down how, implementation is mechanical execution, not design work. Scope drift and mid-flight architecture decisions become rare.

When tools are available, /implement can also commit and push your changes, automatically open a PR (if gh is configured), and move the Jira ticket to “In Review” (if Atlassian MCP is configured). Without gh, you open the PR manually.

  • Output: Code, tests, updated plan with progress, PR
  • Deep dive: Implement (Execution) — step loop, validation, and progress tracking

Step 5: Review — /pr-review

Section titled “Step 5: Review — /pr-review”

Before handing off to your lead, run /pr-review in preflight mode. It checks that spec requirements trace through to plan steps, code, and tests — catching gaps before the lead sees them.

The lead then runs /pr-review in gate mode, which walks the same traceability chain and posts inline GitHub comments. This isn’t bureaucracy; it’s the verification that the spec contract was fulfilled. Because everything is labeled with IDs, the lead can check coverage mechanically rather than re-reading the entire ticket.

  • Output: artifacts/tickets/{ticket-id}/review.md, optional GitHub review comments
  • Deep dive: PR Review (Preflight) — preflight flow, checks, and handoff

Workflow diagram

Section titled “Workflow diagram”

Review gates

Section titled “Review gates”

Review gates are where leads and developers align. They exist not to slow you down, but to catch misunderstandings early — when fixing them is cheap.

After spec: the lead verifies that scope matches intent and the design fits the broader architecture. Catching a wrong interface here saves a full rewrite later.

After plan (optional): if the spec review was thorough and the implementation is straightforward, go ahead and build. If you’re venturing into unfamiliar territory or the approach feels risky, ask your lead to glance at the plan before you start coding.

The spec gate is fast if the artifacts are well-structured. Labeled IDs make review scannable — the lead can read the S/F/N/E headings in 30 seconds and know what the ticket delivers.

Tips

Section titled “Tips”
  1. Keep context scoped. Load only what the current step needs. See Principles.
  2. One task at a time. Complete and verify each plan step before starting the next.
  3. Make verification concrete. Every plan step should have a runnable check — a test command, a type check, a build. “Looks good” is not a validation.
  4. Surface what the agent can’t see. The agent reads code and tickets. You provide verbal decisions, team conventions, and domain knowledge.
  5. Don’t skip the preflight. Running /pr-review before handoff catches traceability gaps and saves a review round-trip.

Related pages

Section titled “Related pages”
  • Lead Workflow — how leads create the tickets you implement
  • Workflows Overview — the shared lifecycle model and how the two workflows connect
  • Setup Guide — prerequisites and environment configuration
  • Tickets Guide — working with ticket artifacts

Pull Ticket

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

Pull-ticket bridges Jira (where tickets live) and your local development environment (where work happens). It creates a structured ticket.md artifact, a feature branch, and optionally transitions the ticket status.

Without it, developers copy-paste ticket descriptions, create branches with inconsistent names, and forget to update Jira status. Pull-ticket makes the handoff from project management to development explicit and repeatable.

How to pull a ticket

Section titled “How to pull a ticket”

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”

Atlassian MCP must be configured and authenticated. For developers, this is the only step in the Developer Workflow that strictly requires Jira access. See the Setup Guide if you haven’t configured it yet.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

A ticket ID — either the full key (PROJ-123) or just the number (123, which expands using the default project from AGENTS.md). If you’re unsure of the key, check your Jira board.

Run the command:

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”
  1. Fetches ticket details from Jira — summary, description, acceptance criteria, and links.
  2. Checks for blockers — if the ticket is blocked, it warns you but doesn’t auto-switch to a different ticket. That’s your call.
  3. Writes artifacts/tickets/PROJ-123/ticket.md — a structured markdown version of the ticket with all relevant fields preserved.
  4. Creates a feature branch from the base branch. The branch name format is ft/proj-123-ticket-name-slug. The prefix varies by work type: ft/ for features, rf/ for refactoring, doc/ for docs, etc.
  5. Asks whether to transition the ticket to “In Progress” — it won’t do this automatically.

How to review the output

Section titled “How to review the output”

Skim the generated ticket.md. Does it capture what you expect to build? If the Jira ticket was vague, this is the moment to notice — before you invest time in spec and plan.

Also verify the branch name looks right. If the auto-generated slug is misleading or too long, rename the branch now rather than later.

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Run /spec to create a detailed specification from the ticket context.

Working without a ticket

Section titled “Working without a ticket”

Pull-ticket is not required. If you’re working on an ad-hoc task — a refactoring, a tech-debt fix, or an idea you’re exploring — skip this step and go straight to /spec. The spec step supports working without a ticket; see Spec (LLD) for details.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

A good pull-ticket run gives you:

  • A ticket.md that reads as a self-contained description of the work, not just raw Jira fields
  • A branch name that tells collaborators what the work is about at a glance
  • Jira status updated so the team knows the ticket is actively being worked on

Anti-patterns:

  • Pulling a ticket and immediately jumping to code without reading ticket.md
  • Ignoring blocker warnings and discovering the dependency mid-implementation

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • Output file: artifacts/tickets/{TICKET-ID}/ticket.md
  • Generated by: /pull-ticket or $pull-ticket
  • Behavior source of truth: .claude/skills/pull-ticket/SKILL.md

Canonical sources (open in new tab):

  • .claude/commands/pull-ticket.md
  • .claude/skills/pull-ticket/SKILL.md

Spec (LLD)

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

The spec is the contract between “what the team agreed to build” and “what actually gets built.” Without it, requirements live in chat threads, vague Jira descriptions, or someone’s head. The spec makes them durable, reviewable, and traceable.

The spec is deliberately separate from the plan. Deciding what to build and how to build it are different cognitive tasks. Mixing them leads to premature implementation detail in the requirements or under-specified requirements hidden behind code decisions. The spec pins down intent; the plan pins down execution.

How to create a spec

Section titled “How to create a spec”

When to run it

Section titled “When to run it”

There are two paths into /spec:

  1. From a ticket: After /pull-ticket. You have a ticket.md and a feature branch. Before running /spec, skim the ticket and any linked PRD or blueprint so you can judge the output.
  2. Ad-hoc (no ticket): You can run /spec for a task you defined yourself — a refactoring, a tech-debt fix, or an idea you’re exploring. Describe the task and goals directly in the conversation. The agent produces the same structured spec, just without a ticket reference.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

The agent reads ticket context (if any) and the codebase automatically. Your job is to provide context the agent can’t see: verbal decisions from standup, constraints from other teams, related ADRs, or domain knowledge that isn’t in the code.

If the ticket is vague, clarify intent with your lead before running /spec — the spec can only be as good as the input.

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”

A spec.md with:

  • Labeled requirements — functional (F###), non-functional (N###), edge cases (E###), success criteria (S###)
  • Interface designs — components (C###), APIs (A###), models (M###), validation rules (V###)
  • Test strategy — unit scenarios (U###), integration scenarios (I###), test data needs (T###)
  • Coding standards references — guidelines (Y###) and anti-patterns (Z###)

Each requirement gets an ID like F001-PARSE-CSV. These IDs flow into the plan and tests for traceability. You label requirements with IDs so that reviewers can reference them by name and plans can trace back to them — “that CSV requirement” is ambiguous in review; F001-PARSE-CSV is not.

How to review the output

Section titled “How to review the output”

This is where your judgment matters most. The agent structures the spec, but only you know whether the content is right.

Do you agree with the scope? Did the agent include something you didn’t intend, or miss something you assumed was obvious? The agent works from what it can see — if you haven’t mentioned a constraint, it won’t know.

Are the design decisions sound? The agent makes choices about interfaces (A###), data models (M###), and component boundaries (C###). Do these match your understanding of the codebase and team conventions? Are there existing patterns or utilities it should use instead?

Are edge cases complete? You know the domain better than the agent. Think about failure scenarios, upstream dependencies, and real-world usage patterns that might be missing from E###.

Does the test strategy cover what matters? Not just “are there test scenarios” but “are these the right scenarios for this feature?”

Quick litmus test: can you scan the S/F/N/E headings in 30 seconds and know what the ticket delivers?

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Submit the spec for lead review. The lead checks alignment with broader project goals and architectural direction. After approval, run /plan.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

A well-specified requirement: E003-MALFORMED-INPUT: Return 400 with schema validation details including field path and constraint violated

A vague requirement: Handle errors appropriately

The 30-second scan test: a reviewer should be able to read just the S/F/N/E headings and understand the ticket’s scope without reading the body of each section.

Out of scope

Section titled “Out of scope”
  • Writing implementation steps (that is the Plan stage).
  • Writing code (that is the Implement stage).
  • Capturing cross-cutting architectural decisions inline (use ADRs for decisions, and reference them).

Artifact reference

Section titled “Artifact reference”

Spec structure

Section titled “Spec structure”
  1. Overview: problem statement and goals.
  2. Success Criteria (S###): concrete “done” statements.
  3. Requirements analysis:
    • Functional requirements (F###)
    • Non-functional requirements (N###)
    • Dependencies/constraints (D###)
    • Edge cases (E###)
    • Explicit out-of-scope exclusions
  4. Technical approach + detailed design:
    • Components (C###), APIs/contracts (A###), models (M###), validation rules (V###)
    • Related ADR references (if applicable)
  5. Testing strategy:
    • Unit scenarios (U###), integration scenarios (I###), test data needs (T###)
  6. Risks + open questions:
    • Risks (R###)
    • Questions/decisions (Q###) with recorded decisions when they block planning

Label format

Section titled “Label format”

LETTER + 3 digits + "-" + SHORT-NAME — e.g., F001-PARSE-CSV

ID categories:

  • S###: Success criteria
  • F###: Functional requirements
  • N###: Non-functional requirements
  • D###: Dependencies/constraints
  • E###: Edge cases
  • C###: Components/modules
  • A###: APIs/contracts
  • M###: Data models
  • V###: Validation rules
  • U### / I### / T###: Unit tests / integration tests / test data
  • R### / Q###: Risks / open questions
  • Y### / Z###: Coding guidelines / anti-patterns (plus language packs: PY###/PZ###, JY###/JZ###)

Traceability (how spec IDs flow forward)

Section titled “Traceability (how spec IDs flow forward)”
  • Plan steps: each P-step in plan.md cites the spec IDs it implements
  • Tests: test scenarios cite the requirements they validate (e.g., “validates F003”)
  • Review: leads review by walking the ID set and checking coverage

Coding standards reference

Section titled “Coding standards reference”

Specs should cite coding standards by ID so expectations are explicit and reviewable.

Coding standards live in the Context.

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • Output file: artifacts/tickets/{TICKET-ID}/spec.md
  • Generated by: /spec or $spec
  • Behavior source of truth: .claude/skills/spec/SKILL.md

Canonical sources (open in new tab):

  • .claude/commands/spec.md
  • .claude/skills/spec/SKILL.md
  • .claude/skills/spec/SPEC-TEMPLATE.md

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Missing success criteria (S###): planning becomes guesswork; add measurable “done” statements.
  • Unlabeled requirements: reviewers can’t reference or trace coverage; label every requirement.
  • Vague edge cases: list concrete error scenarios and boundary conditions (E###).
  • Implicit contracts: if an API/model changes, write the contract notes in A###/M###/V###.
  • Standards left as “follow best practices”: cite the relevant Y/Z (and PY/JY packs as needed).

Plan (Implementation)

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

The plan eliminates decision-making during implementation. It pre-decides file changes, ordering, and validation so that /implement can execute mechanically. This matters especially for AI agents, which perform better with explicit checklists than open-ended judgment calls.

Without a plan, the implementer (human or agent) makes design decisions on the fly that may conflict with the spec. The plan is the bridge between “what to build” (spec) and “build it” (implement).

How to create a plan

Section titled “How to create a plan”

When to run it

Section titled “When to run it”

After spec is approved by your lead. You need an approved spec.md — running /plan on an unapproved spec risks rework if the lead requests changes to scope or design.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

The agent reads spec.md and codebase context automatically. Provide any implementation preferences not captured in the spec: “use the existing validation middleware,” “this should follow the pattern in module X,” or sequencing preferences (“API first, then UI”).

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”

A plan.md with:

  • P-steps (execution units) — each cites the spec IDs it implements and includes a runnable validation command. P-steps are the checklist /implement executes.
  • W-entries (file touchpoints) — exactly which files are created, modified, or deleted, so “where will this happen?” is never ambiguous.
  • K-checkpoints (verification gates) — “stop and verify” points placed after risky changes like contract changes, integration points, or migrations.
  • X-points (commit milestones, optional) — align commits to meaningful milestones for easier review and rollback.

How to review the output

Section titled “How to review the output”

The agent handles structure and traceability. Your review should focus on judgment calls:

Does the implementation approach make sense? The agent chose an ordering and file structure. Does this match how you’d approach it? Are there simpler alternatives, or existing utilities and patterns it should reuse?

Is the scope right? Are there steps that feel unnecessary, or missing steps for things you know will be needed?

Are the file touchpoints (W###) correct? You know the codebase — does the agent target the right files, or is it missing something (or touching files it shouldn’t)?

Is the step granularity reasonable? Overly large steps are hard to verify; overly small steps create noise. Each should be completable and verifiable in one sitting.

Are there risks the agent didn’t consider? Migration concerns, backward compatibility, performance implications — things that require domain knowledge.

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Submit the plan for lead review. After approval, run /implement.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

A well-formed P-step:

A vague step: Implement validation — no spec IDs, no files, no validation command.

The difference: the first can be executed mechanically and verified independently. The second requires the implementer to make decisions that should have been made during planning.

Out of scope

Section titled “Out of scope”
  • Writing or revising the underlying design intent (that belongs in the spec/ADRs).
  • Implementing code changes (that is the Implement stage).
  • Leaving verification implicit (“we’ll test later”) without concrete commands.

Artifact reference

Section titled “Artifact reference”

Plan structure

Section titled “Plan structure”
  1. Progress Summary: current status, last updated, completed steps.
  2. Preconditions, open questions, and risks: what blocks safe execution.
  3. Implementation strategy: sequencing, critical path, and constraints.
  4. Requirements traceability: which spec IDs are covered, and where.
  5. Implementation steps (P###): executable steps with dependencies and validation.
  6. File change summary (W###): the concrete file touchpoints (including tests).
  7. Testing checkpoints (K###): de-risk gates with commands.
  8. Commit points (X###) (optional): milestones for review/rollback.
  9. Final validation & acceptance: how the work is verified against S/F/N/E.
  10. Rollback plan: safe stop points and revert guidance.

Traceability (Spec to Plan to Validation)

Section titled “Traceability (Spec to Plan to Validation)”

The plan should demonstrate coverage:

  • every S/F/N/E/C/A/M/V in the spec maps to one or more P-steps
  • every P-step lists concrete validation (tests/commands) tied to U/I scenarios

If a requirement can’t be mapped to a validation method, it’s usually not specified clearly enough.

Coding standards in plan steps

Section titled “Coding standards in plan steps”

Plan steps should cite the relevant coding standards IDs so reviewers can audit quality expectations without rereading a long “best practices” section.

Coding standards live in the Context.

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • Output file: artifacts/tickets/{TICKET-ID}/plan.md
  • Generated by: /plan or $plan (typically from spec.md)
  • Behavior source of truth: .claude/skills/plan/SKILL.md

Canonical sources (open in new tab):

  • .claude/commands/plan.md
  • .claude/skills/plan/SKILL.md
  • .claude/skills/plan/PLAN-TEMPLATE.md

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Steps without validation: always include a command or a deterministic check.
  • Missing file list: add W### entries (including test files).
  • No interface notes on contract changes: if you touch A###/M###/V###, include brief contract notes.
  • Overly large steps: split until each step can be completed and verified in one sitting.
  • Work Packages treated like execution IDs: Work Packages are headings only; execution is P-driven.

Implement (Execution)

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

Implementation is deliberately constrained to follow the plan as a checklist with TDD and validation at each step. This prevents the common failure mode of writing all code, testing at the end, and discovering fundamental issues late.

The plan gets updated as a durable progress record, so work survives across sessions and agents. If a session ends mid-implementation, the next session picks up from the last checked box — no context is lost.

How to run implementation

Section titled “How to run implementation”

When to run it

Section titled “When to run it”

After plan is approved by your lead. You need an approved plan.md with unchecked P-steps.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

Typically nothing — the agent reads the plan and executes the next unchecked P-step. You may need to intervene if the agent hits a blocker (missing dependency, unclear spec, failing test). Your role during implementation shifts to monitoring and unblocking.

What the agent does

Section titled “What the agent does”

For each P-step, the agent:

  1. Restates the step’s purpose, spec IDs, files (W###), and standards (Y/Z).
  2. Writes the test first (TDD) — writing the test first catches spec gaps before code is written. If the agent discovers the spec is unclear while writing a test, that’s a signal to pause and clarify, not push through.
  3. Implements the code to pass the test.
  4. Runs validation — the step’s commands and any relevant K-checkpoints.
  5. Updates the plan — checks the box, updates the Progress Summary, records deviations and validation results.

How to monitor progress

Section titled “How to monitor progress”

Check plan.md — checked boxes and the Progress Summary show what’s done. Deviation notes show where the agent diverged from the plan and why.

When to intervene

Section titled “When to intervene”

Many deviations: If the agent keeps deviating from the plan, the plan (or spec) may need revision. Deviations are normal in small doses, but a pattern suggests the upstream artifacts missed something.

You disagree with a design choice: The agent makes implementation decisions within each step. If a choice doesn’t fit your understanding of the codebase or team conventions, say so — the agent can adjust.

The agent is stuck: Missing dependencies, unclear spec sections, or environment issues. Your role is to unblock by providing context or clarifying intent.

Tests don’t test the right thing: The agent writes tests, but you know what actually matters. If a test validates syntax but misses the business logic, push back.

What happens at completion

Section titled “What happens at completion”

At configured automation points (typically X### commit checkpoints), the agent may create git commits, push to remote, open a PR, or transition a Jira ticket — but only when the necessary tooling (gh, MCP) is available. If no such automation is configured, /implement stops after updating plan.md, and you handle git, PR, and ticket operations yourself.

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Run /pr-review preflight before requesting lead review.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

A healthy implementation rhythm: each step is tested and validated before moving on. The plan shows a clean trail of checked boxes with brief validation results.

Anti-patterns:

  • “Implement everything then test” — defeats the purpose of step-by-step validation
  • “Skip validation because it looks right” — verification is part of the deliverable, not optional
  • Large uncommitted changes spanning multiple steps — use X-points for commit milestones

Out of scope

Section titled “Out of scope”
  • Re-deciding what the ticket should do (update the spec instead).
  • Replanning the work breakdown (update the plan instead).
  • “Finishing fast” by skipping validation (verification is part of the deliverable).

Artifact reference

Section titled “Artifact reference”

Implementation produces two durable outputs:

  1. Code + tests: the actual change set that satisfies the spec and plan.
  2. An updated plan: the durable execution record that makes progress auditable.

During /implement, plan.md is updated with:

  • checked P-step checkboxes (- [x])
  • updated Progress Summary (status, last updated, completed steps count)
  • notes on gaps vs spec, deviations, and validation results

Optional automation (tooling-dependent)

Section titled “Optional automation (tooling-dependent)”

Some automation may occur only when tools are available and configured:

  • commits at X### points
  • PR creation via gh
  • Jira transitions via MCP

If tooling is missing, the workflow still completes the core goal: verified code + updated plan.

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • Input file: artifacts/tickets/{TICKET-ID}/plan.md
  • Output: working code + tests + an updated plan (checkboxes + notes)
  • Run with: /implement or $implement
  • Behavior source of truth: .claude/skills/implement/SKILL.md

Canonical sources (open in new tab):

  • .claude/commands/implement.md
  • .claude/skills/implement/SKILL.md
  • .claude/skills/implement/WORKTREE-SETUP.md
  • .claude/skills/implement/VALIDATION-REFERENCE.md

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Skipping TDD: Writing code first then retrofitting tests misses the feedback loop that catches spec gaps early.
  • Ignoring deviations: If the agent deviates from the plan, record why. Unrecorded deviations make review and debugging harder.
  • Not checking the plan: The plan is your progress dashboard. If you don’t check it, you lose visibility into what’s done and what’s left.

PR Review (Preflight)

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

Preflight gives you a fast, private feedback loop before your work reaches a lead. Without it, the first traceability check happens during gate review, where feedback loops are slow and expensive.

Preflight also produces review.md as structured evidence — you’re not just saying “it’s ready,” you’re providing proof. Chat verdicts are ephemeral, but review.md survives across sessions and serves as auditable evidence for the lead.

How to run a preflight review

Section titled “How to run a preflight review”

When to run it

Section titled “When to run it”
  • Before requesting lead approval on a PR
  • After significant commit updates (to refresh review.md)
  • Before PR creation (local branch-diff mode) for early feedback

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

Optionally a PR number:

If omitted, the workflow auto-detects the PR from your current branch. If no PR exists yet, it reviews the branch diff against the effective base branch (origin/<base>...HEAD).

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”
  • artifacts/tickets/{ticket-id}/review.md — a rolling review artifact with structured findings
  • A chat verdict — one of APPROVED, CHANGES_REQUESTED, BLOCKED, or COMMENT

The review tracks freshness by commit SHA — new commits invalidate the previous review, making staleness visible. This prevents the common problem of a “green” review that doesn’t reflect the latest changes.

How to act on findings

Section titled “How to act on findings”

Read the verdict and findings. Fix issues, update tests and code, then re-run preflight. The iterative loop is: preflight, fix, re-run, until clean.

Focus on findings that reference spec IDs — these are traceability gaps the lead will also catch. Fixing them now saves a round-trip.

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Request lead gate review with a fresh review.md. The lead uses a different review pass focused on merge-readiness and architectural alignment. This two-pass model means each role focuses on what matters to them: you validate completeness, the lead validates intent and fit.

For the lead’s gate pass, see PR Review (Lead Gate).

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

Ideal flow: preflight, resolve findings, re-run clean, hand off with a fresh review.md that matches the current PR HEAD SHA.

Anti-patterns:

  • Push a PR and hope the lead catches issues — wastes the lead’s time on things you could fix
  • Submit with a stale review.md from three commits ago — the lead can’t trust it
  • Ignore CHANGES_REQUESTED findings and request review anyway

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • .claude/skills/pr-review/SKILL.md
  • .claude/commands/pr-review.md

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Stale reviews: Always re-run after pushing new commits. The SHA-based freshness tracking exists for a reason.
  • Ignoring traceability findings: If the review flags a spec ID without test coverage, that’s a real gap — not a false positive.
  • Skipping preflight entirely: The lead gate review is not a substitute for self-review. Two passes catch different things.

Lead Workflow

What this workflow is for

Section titled “What this workflow is for”

The lead workflow takes a fuzzy initiative — a feature idea, a product brief, a stakeholder request — and progressively refines it into a structured backlog of epics and tickets that developers can implement mechanically.

Your goal as a lead is to remove ambiguity before developers start working. Every decision you defer to a developer is a decision that might be made differently (and wrongly) across tickets. The lead workflow front-loads those decisions: requirements become a PRD, architecture becomes a blueprint, and work decomposition becomes a structured ticket set.

Use either invocation style depending on your tool:

  • Slash-command tools: /prd, /blueprint, /blueprint-to-tickets, /ticket-to-jira, /pr-review
  • Skill-invocation tools (including Codex): $prd, $blueprint, $blueprint-to-tickets, $ticket-to-jira, $pr-review

Walking through the workflow

Section titled “Walking through the workflow”

Step 1: Capture requirements — /prd (optional)

Section titled “Step 1: Capture requirements — /prd (optional)”

If the initiative doesn’t already have clear, written requirements, start here. /prd produces a product requirements document that structures business goals, user stories, and success criteria into a durable artifact.

This step is optional because many initiatives arrive with requirements already documented — in a product brief, a client deliverable, or a stakeholder deck. If the requirements exist and are clear enough to design against, skip /prd and feed them directly into /blueprint.

  • Output: artifacts/requirements/{feature-name}-prd.md
  • Deep dive: PRD (Product Requirements) — purpose, structure, and templates

Step 2: Design the architecture — /blueprint

Section titled “Step 2: Design the architecture — /blueprint”

The blueprint is where technical decisions get made. It defines the architecture, major components, integration points, and high-level design — scoped to the initiative, not the entire system.

Feed the agent your PRD (or equivalent requirements), any existing HLD or mockups, and your own architectural judgment. The agent produces the structure; your review ensures it fits the codebase, team capabilities, and non-functional constraints that aren’t written down.

What to look for in review: Does the component decomposition make sense? Are the integration points realistic given what exists today? Are there architectural patterns from the codebase it should follow — or constraints from other teams it doesn’t know about?

  • Output: artifacts/blueprints/{id}--{name}/blueprint.md
  • Deep dive: Blueprint (HLD) — purpose, structure, and templates

Step 3: Decompose into tickets — /blueprint-to-tickets

Section titled “Step 3: Decompose into tickets — /blueprint-to-tickets”

This is where architecture becomes work. /blueprint-to-tickets takes the blueprint and breaks it into epics and implementation tickets, each scoped to be independently implementable.

The agent handles the decomposition, but you decide whether it’s right. Good tickets are independently valuable, clearly scoped, and ordered so that dependencies flow naturally. Watch for tickets that are too large (they’ll need re-planning), too vague (developers will fill the gaps with guesswork), or that have hidden dependencies on each other.

This is also where you set up traceability. Each ticket traces back to blueprint components, so when a developer runs /spec later, they have architectural context for their design decisions.

  • Output: artifacts/blueprints/{id}--{name}/tickets/ — epics and tickets in markdown
  • Deep dive: Blueprint → Tickets — artifact structure and ticket guidance

Step 4: Export to Jira — /ticket-to-jira (optional)

Section titled “Step 4: Export to Jira — /ticket-to-jira (optional)”

Once you’re satisfied with the ticket set, /ticket-to-jira pushes them to Jira with proper epic/ticket hierarchy. This is optional — some teams manage work directly from the ticket files, and the developer workflow works either way.

The command includes safety checks: it validates the project, confirms the epic structure, and lets you review before creating anything. Jira operations are hard to undo, so the workflow is deliberately cautious.

  • Output: Epics and tickets created in Jira
  • Prerequisites: Atlassian MCP must be configured and authenticated
  • Deep dive: Ticket → Jira — inputs, safety checks, and MCP prerequisites

Step 5: Review developer work — /pr-review

Section titled “Step 5: Review developer work — /pr-review”

As developers implement tickets, your role shifts to reviewing their specs, plans, and PRs. The field guide’s review gates give you structured checkpoints:

Spec review (after /spec): Does the developer’s design match your blueprint intent? Are requirements complete and labeled? Are edge cases covered? This is where you catch misalignment before any code is written.

Plan review (after /plan, optional): Developers don’t always need a second review loop here. If the spec review was thorough, they can move straight to implementation. But if they’re unsure about the approach — unfamiliar domain, risky changes, complex ordering — encourage them to share the plan for a quick sanity check before coding.

PR review (after /implement): Run /pr-review in gate mode to walk the traceability chain: intent → spec → plan → code → tests. Because everything uses labeled IDs, you can verify coverage mechanically — check that every F/N/E requirement has a corresponding plan step, code change, and test.

  • Output: artifacts/tickets/{ticket-id}/review.md, optional GitHub review comments
  • Prerequisites: GitHub CLI (gh) must be installed and authenticated
  • Deep dive: PR Review — review objectives and outputs

Workflow diagram

Section titled “Workflow diagram”

How the lead workflow feeds the developer workflow

Section titled “How the lead workflow feeds the developer workflow”

The two workflows connect at the ticket boundary. Your blueprints decompose into tickets; developers pull those tickets and run /spec → /plan → /implement. The quality of your upstream work directly affects how mechanical their execution can be:

  • Clear tickets → specs that match intent on the first draft
  • Good architectural decisions → plans that follow existing patterns naturally
  • Well-scoped decomposition → tickets that can be implemented independently without cross-ticket coordination

Neither workflow requires the other — developers can use /spec → /plan → /implement on any ticket, even one created manually. But the lead workflow makes the developer workflow significantly more effective by front-loading decisions and providing architectural context.

Tips

Section titled “Tips”
  1. Write for the developer who hasn’t been in the room. Blueprints and tickets should carry enough context that someone outside the planning conversations can spec and implement correctly.
  2. Review specs and plans promptly. Developers are blocked at review gates. Slow reviews negate the efficiency the workflow provides.
  3. Use review gates to teach. When you see a spec or plan that misses something, explain why — this builds the developer’s judgment for next time.
  4. Don’t over-decompose. Tickets that are too small create coordination overhead. Each ticket should deliver something independently testable.
  5. Keep blueprints focused. One initiative, one blueprint. Cross-cutting concerns belong in ADRs, not embedded in a feature blueprint.

Related pages

Section titled “Related pages”
  • Developer Workflow — how developers implement the tickets you create
  • Workflows Overview — the shared lifecycle model and how the two workflows connect
  • Setup Guide — prerequisites and environment configuration
  • Principles — shared guidance on context and verification
  • Blueprints Guide — creating and managing blueprints

PRD (Product Requirements)

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

The PRD captures product intent before anyone thinks about architecture. Without it, technical decisions get made in a vacuum and scope creeps because nobody wrote down what was out of scope.

The PRD is deliberately separate from the blueprint. Product intent and technical architecture are different concerns often owned by different people (PM vs tech lead). Mixing them means the architect makes product decisions implicitly, or the PM gets pulled into technical debates that should happen later.

How to create a PRD

Section titled “How to create a PRD”

When to run it

Section titled “When to run it”

At the start of a new feature or initiative, before any technical planning. The PRD is optional if strong requirements already exist elsewhere (e.g., a detailed stakeholder brief), but even then, structuring them into a PRD makes them easier to reference from downstream artifacts.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

Business goals, user stories, stakeholder context, constraints, and any existing research. The more context you feed, the better the output. The agent can’t invent business requirements — it structures what you give it.

Think about:

  • What problem are we solving and for whom?
  • How will we measure success?
  • What is explicitly not in scope?
  • What assumptions are we making?
  • What external dependencies or constraints exist?

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”

A PRD with problem statement, users and workflows, success criteria, scope and non-goals, risks, and open questions. Output goes to artifacts/requirements/{feature-name}-prd.md.

How to review the output

Section titled “How to review the output”

The agent structures your input into a PRD format. Your review is about completeness and accuracy of intent, not format.

Is the problem statement what you actually mean? The agent paraphrases your input — make sure it captured the real problem, not a surface-level restatement.

Are non-goals explicit enough? Think about what stakeholders might assume is in scope that isn’t. Scope expands by default; non-goals are your pressure valve.

Are there assumptions you haven’t stated? The agent can’t know what you haven’t told it. Surface constraints, dependencies, and political realities.

The blueprint author test: can a tech lead write a blueprint from this PRD alone, without needing to ask you clarifying questions? If not, the PRD is missing context.

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Use the PRD as input to /blueprint. Later artifacts (blueprints, specs) should reference the PRD for traceability.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

A PRD that passes the blueprint author test — a tech lead can read it and start architectural planning without ambiguity about what to build or what’s out of scope.

Anti-patterns:

  • A PRD that forces the architect to make product decisions (“the PRD says ‘improve performance’ but doesn’t say which workflows or what targets”)
  • Non-goals that are too vague to enforce (“we won’t do anything unnecessary”)
  • Missing success criteria — how will anyone know the feature succeeded?

Out of scope

Section titled “Out of scope”
  • Define implementation details (that belongs in the blueprint/spec).
  • Decide architecture (that belongs in the blueprint + ADRs).
  • Decompose into tickets (that belongs in /blueprint-to-tickets).

Artifact reference

Section titled “Artifact reference”
  • Output file: artifacts/requirements/{feature-name}-prd.md

A PRD should make it easy for a reader to answer:

  • what success means (measures, targets, timelines)
  • what is in/out of scope (to prevent drift)
  • what user workflows matter and why
  • what risks and prerequisites exist

Traceability forward

Section titled “Traceability forward”

Later artifacts should reference the PRD:

  • Blueprints should link the related PRD in metadata/refs.
  • Ticket specs should reference the blueprint/PRD when relevant for intent.

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • .claude/skills/prd/SKILL.md
  • .claude/commands/prd.md
  • templates/prd_template.md

Run this workflow as /prd or $prd.

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Jumping to architecture: If you find yourself writing component names or API shapes, you’re in blueprint territory. Keep the PRD focused on what and why, not how.
  • Vague success criteria: “Users are satisfied” isn’t measurable. Quantify where possible.
  • Missing non-goals: Unstated non-goals become assumed scope. Be explicit about what you’re not building.

Blueprint (HLD)

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

The blueprint translates product intent into technical shape. Without it, ticket decomposition is ad-hoc — tickets don’t fit together, integration points are discovered late, and architecture decisions are inconsistent across tickets.

The blueprint is the artifact that lets decomposition be mechanical rather than creative. If the blueprint fully specifies component boundaries, interfaces, and phasing, then /blueprint-to-tickets can break the work down without requiring additional architectural judgment.

How to create a blueprint

Section titled “How to create a blueprint”

When to run it

Section titled “When to run it”

After the PRD (or equivalent requirements) is ready. If there’s no PRD, you’re implicitly making product decisions in the blueprint — be explicit about this with stakeholders.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

The PRD, any existing technical context (codebase patterns, infrastructure constraints, team capabilities), and your architectural preferences. The agent produces a first draft, but architecture is a human-judgment-heavy activity — expect to iterate.

Think about:

  • What existing patterns and infrastructure should this build on?
  • What are the team’s skills and capacity constraints?
  • Are there decisions that should be recorded as ADRs?
  • How should the work be phased for parallel execution?

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”

A blueprint.md with architecture overview, major components, interfaces, key decisions, non-functional requirements, and phasing guidance. Output goes to artifacts/blueprints/{id}--{name}/blueprint.md.

How to review the output

Section titled “How to review the output”

The agent drafts architecture, but you own the technical decisions.

Do you agree with the architecture? The agent proposes component boundaries, integration points, and technology choices. Do these match your vision, team skills, and infrastructure reality?

Are there decisions you’d make differently? Push back on choices that don’t fit. The agent works from codebase and PRD context — you know things it doesn’t (team capacity, org constraints, tech debt priorities).

Are cross-cutting decisions captured as ADRs? Decisions buried in a blueprint are hard to find later. If a decision affects multiple future tickets, it needs its own ADR under artifacts/architecture/.

Does the phasing make sense? Phasing directly feeds decomposition. Think about team parallelism and risk ordering — do high-risk components come first (to de-risk early) or last (to build confidence)?

Litmus test: does this resolve the “how” concretely, or just restate the PRD with technical jargon?

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Run /blueprint-to-tickets to decompose into epics and tickets.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

A blueprint where decomposition is mechanical — you can read the components and phasing and immediately see where the ticket boundaries are.

Anti-patterns:

  • Integration seams left undefined (“these components will communicate somehow”)
  • Architecture that restates PRD goals without resolving technical questions
  • Decisions that should be ADRs buried in prose

Out of scope

Section titled “Out of scope”
  • Ticket-level implementation detail (that belongs in spec.md per ticket).
  • A full executable implementation plan (that belongs in the Developer Workflow plan).
  • Recording cross-cutting decisions only in prose (use ADRs for decisions that should live beyond a single blueprint, and reference them).

Artifact reference

Section titled “Artifact reference”
  • Output file: artifacts/blueprints/{blueprint-id}--{name}/blueprint.md

Blueprints should be structured so a reviewer can quickly understand:

  • context (business + technical)
  • goals/non-goals
  • architecture overview and major components
  • key decisions and implications
  • security/performance/reliability considerations
  • how the work should be phased or decomposed

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • .claude/skills/blueprint/SKILL.md
  • .claude/commands/blueprint.md
  • templates/blueprint-template.md

Run this workflow as /blueprint or $blueprint.

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Skipping the PRD: Building architecture without product intent means the architect fills in gaps with assumptions. Those assumptions become implicit requirements nobody agreed to.
  • Monolithic phasing: A single “phase 1: build everything” defeats the purpose of phasing. Break work into independently deliverable increments.
  • Decision hygiene: If you make decisions that affect multiple future tickets or set precedent for the codebase, record them as ADRs — not just blueprint prose.

Blueprint → Tickets

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

Decomposition is where architecture meets execution. Without a deliberate decomposition step, tickets are too large, too small, or have hidden dependencies that block progress mid-sprint.

Good decomposition means each ticket is self-contained: a developer can pick it up, run /spec and /plan, and execute without needing to make product or architecture decisions that should have been captured earlier.

How to decompose a blueprint into tickets

Section titled “How to decompose a blueprint into tickets”

When to run it

Section titled “When to run it”

After the blueprint is reviewed and stable. Decomposing a moving target wastes effort — if the blueprint is still being iterated, wait until the architecture is agreed upon.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

The blueprint path. Optionally, sequencing preferences or team allocation constraints (“team A owns the API, team B owns the UI”).

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”

A two-level output:

  • Top-level summary ({id}-tickets.md) — epic breakdown with dependencies and sequencing
  • Per-epic ticket files in a subfolder ({id}-tickets/) — detailed tickets with acceptance criteria

The two levels serve different audiences: leads need the overview for sprint planning, developers need ticket detail for execution.

How to review the output

Section titled “How to review the output”

The agent decomposes mechanically from the blueprint. Your review catches what the blueprint didn’t make explicit:

Are there missing tickets? Think about cross-cutting concerns the agent might not infer: migration scripts, documentation updates, config changes, feature flags.

Do the dependencies reflect reality? The agent infers dependencies from the blueprint, but you know about external dependencies — other teams, infrastructure changes, third-party timelines.

Is the sizing right for your team? Each ticket should be narrow enough that a developer can spec it without making product or architecture decisions. Too broad means decisions get deferred; too narrow means coordination overhead.

Do any tickets overlap? Overlapping scope causes merge conflicts and duplicated work.

Review before anyone starts executing — bad decomposition discovered mid-implementation is expensive to fix.

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Optionally export to Jira with /ticket-to-jira. Or use the ticket files directly as input to the developer workflow.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

Each ticket is self-contained and executable. A developer reading a ticket can understand what to build without consulting other tickets (though dependencies are explicit).

Anti-patterns:

  • “Implement the API” with no endpoints specified — too broad
  • Circular dependencies between tickets
  • Tickets that require the developer to resolve architecture questions

Out of scope

Section titled “Out of scope”
  • Implement any of the tickets.
  • Write ticket-level spec.md and plan.md (those belong in the Developer Workflow per ticket).
  • Skip review: the decomposition should be reviewed and iterated before execution begins.

Artifact reference

Section titled “Artifact reference”
