Decomposition outputs live next to the blueprint:

  • Blueprint: artifacts/blueprints/{blueprint-id}--{name}/blueprint.md
  • Tickets output folder: artifacts/blueprints/{blueprint-id}--{name}/tickets/
    • Top-level tickets file: {blueprint-id}-tickets.md
    • Per-epic ticket files folder: {blueprint-id}-tickets/

This structure supports a two-level review:

  • a high-level epic breakdown in the top-level file
  • detailed tickets in per-epic files

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • .claude/skills/blueprint-to-tickets/SKILL.md
  • .claude/commands/blueprint-to-tickets.md
  • .claude/skills/managing-jira/SKILL.md

Run this workflow as /blueprint-to-tickets or $blueprint-to-tickets.

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Decomposing before the blueprint is stable: Rework cascades — changing architecture means re-decomposing, which means re-speccing tickets already in progress.
  • Missing cross-cutting tickets: Infrastructure, CI/CD, documentation, and observability tickets are easy to forget. Check for them explicitly.
  • Implicit dependencies: If ticket B can’t start until ticket A is done, state that. Implicit dependencies surface as blocked sprints.

Ticket → Jira

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

This step bridges artifact-based planning (markdown in the repo) and project management (Jira boards, sprints, reporting). Without it, the carefully decomposed tickets are invisible to PMs and sprint planning.

The export preserves the structure and detail from /blueprint-to-tickets so that Jira reflects the agreed decomposition rather than a manual re-entry that inevitably diverges.

How to export tickets to Jira

Section titled “How to export tickets to Jira”

Prerequisites

Section titled “Prerequisites”

Atlassian MCP must be configured and authenticated. This workflow is MCP-only — there is no manual fallback. See the Setup Guide if you haven’t configured it yet.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

The ticket files path from /blueprint-to-tickets output. The workflow will prompt you to confirm:

  • Atlassian cloud/site
  • Jira project
  • Required issue types (Epic/Task)

This confirmation is a deliberate safety measure — accidentally creating 30 tickets in the wrong project is costly and hard to undo.

What the agent does

Section titled “What the agent does”
  1. Creates epics and tickets in Jira matching the markdown hierarchy, with labels and acceptance criteria preserved.
  2. Updates the source markdown by checking off exported items — this prevents double-creation on re-runs and provides an audit trail.

How to verify the export

Section titled “How to verify the export”

Check two things after export:

In Jira: Verify that the hierarchy matches the decomposition. Are all epics present? Do tickets have the right parent epics? Are acceptance criteria and labels populated?

In the source markdown: Look for checkmarks confirming what was exported. If some items weren’t created (auth issues, missing issue types), the unchecked items tell you exactly what to retry.

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

Tickets are now visible for sprint planning. Developers can pull them with /pull-ticket to start the developer workflow.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

Jira hierarchy matches the decomposition exactly. Source markdown shows what was exported with checkmarks. A re-run creates nothing new (idempotent via checkmarks).

Anti-patterns:

  • Partial exports with no record of what succeeded
  • Manual Jira creation that diverges from the agreed decomposition
  • Skipping the confirmation prompts by pre-filling wrong values

Out of scope

Section titled “Out of scope”
  • Replace Jira permissions/admin work (projects and issue types must exist).
  • Provide a non-MCP fallback (this workflow assumes Atlassian MCP).
  • Decide the decomposition structure (that should already be agreed in the ticket set).

Artifact reference

Section titled “Artifact reference”

This workflow expects to export from:

  • artifacts/blueprints/{blueprint-id}--{name}/tickets/{blueprint-id}-tickets.md
  • artifacts/blueprints/{blueprint-id}--{name}/tickets/{blueprint-id}-tickets/EPIC-XX-*.md

During export, the source markdown files are updated by checking off created items.

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • .claude/skills/ticket-to-jira/SKILL.md
  • .claude/commands/ticket-to-jira.md
  • .claude/skills/managing-jira/SKILL.md

Run this workflow as /ticket-to-jira or $ticket-to-jira.

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Wrong project selection: Double-check the Jira project before confirming. Cleaning up mis-created tickets is tedious.
  • Re-running without checking source markdown: The checkmark system prevents duplicates, but only if the source files are up to date. If you manually edited them, verify state first.
  • Missing issue types: If Jira doesn’t have Epic or Task issue types configured, the export will fail. Resolve this with your Jira admin before running.

PR Review (Lead Gate)

Why this step exists

Section titled “Why this step exists”

The gate review is the final quality check before merge. It differs from developer preflight in scope and authority — the lead checks merge-readiness against architectural intent, not just spec coverage.

Without this gate, only CI checks quality (syntax and tests, not whether the change matches what was intended). A clean, well-tested diff can still solve the wrong problem. The gate review catches that.

How to run a gate review

Section titled “How to run a gate review”

When to run it

Section titled “When to run it”

After the developer has submitted a PR with a fresh preflight review.md. Use the preflight evidence as your starting point, not a blank slate — the developer has already validated traceability and resolved basic issues.

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

A PR number, or let it auto-detect from the current branch. The agent resolves ticket context from the PR diff — changed file paths like artifacts/tickets/*/spec.md identify which ticket directory to review.

Base branch and Jira project defaults from AGENTS.md are optional. The skill resolves fallbacks from user input, environment variables, git remote defaults, and PR metadata when needed.

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”
  • An updated review.md with structured findings and a verdict (APPROVED, CHANGES_REQUESTED, BLOCKED, COMMENT)
  • Optionally, GitHub inline comments when the tooling supports it (typically in gate mode)

How to review effectively

Section titled “How to review effectively”

Walk the traceability chain: ticket intent, then spec, then plan, then code, then tests. The diff tells you what changed but not whether it matches intent. Structure findings around spec IDs (“F003-VALIDATE-INPUT not covered by test”) rather than vague line references (“looks wrong on line 42”). This makes findings actionable for the developer.

Check freshness: Verify review.md HEAD SHA matches current PR HEAD. A review of commit abc123 is useless if def456 was pushed afterward. If the review is stale, ask the developer to re-run preflight.

Focus on what preflight can’t catch: Preflight validates structure and traceability. Your gate review adds judgment: does the approach fit the broader architecture? Are there cross-ticket implications? Does this set the right precedent for the codebase?

What to do next

Section titled “What to do next”

If approved, merge. If changes requested, the developer iterates and re-runs preflight before requesting another gate review.

What “good” looks like

Section titled “What “good” looks like”

Every code change traces to a spec requirement. Every requirement traces to a test. The review artifact has a current SHA and clean findings.

Anti-patterns:

  • Reviewing a diff in isolation without spec context — you can’t validate intent from code alone
  • Approving with a stale review.md — freshness matters
  • Line-level nitpicks without traceability — “rename this variable” is style; “F003 has no test coverage” is substance

Out of scope

Section titled “Out of scope”
  • Replace CI: reviews complement automated checks; they don’t replace them.
  • Re-implement the work: if major rework is needed, prefer returning it to the developer workflow.

Artifact reference

Section titled “Artifact reference”

This workflow typically produces:

  • artifacts/tickets/{ticket-id}/review.md (tracked artifact, rolling latest review)
  • a structured review summary (what’s correct, what’s missing, what to change)
  • (optionally) GitHub inline comments when the tooling supports it

Context resolution from PR diff

Section titled “Context resolution from PR diff”

When the PR includes ticket artifacts, use changed paths to locate context:

  • artifacts/tickets/*/spec.md
  • artifacts/tickets/*/plan.md
  • artifacts/tickets/*/ticket.md
  • artifacts/tickets/*/review.md

This is the primary signal for which ticket directory to review.

If you are a developer preparing handoff, start with PR Review (Preflight).

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • .claude/skills/pr-review/SKILL.md
  • .claude/commands/pr-review.md
  • .claude/skills/gh-cli/SKILL.md

Run this workflow as /pr-review or $pr-review.

Common pitfalls

Section titled “Common pitfalls”
  • Reviewing without the spec: The diff shows what changed. The spec tells you what should have changed. Without both, you can’t validate completeness.
  • Stale reviews: If new commits were pushed after the last review.md, the review is stale. Ask the developer to re-run preflight.
  • Approving to unblock: If there are real issues, CHANGES_REQUESTED is the right verdict. Approving with caveats (“fix this after merge”) creates tech debt and breaks traceability.

ADR (Architecture Decision Record)

Why this command exists

Section titled “Why this command exists”

Architecture decisions affect multiple tickets, components, or teams. Without a record, the reasoning behind a choice lives in someone’s head or buried in a PR comment thread. When a new developer asks “why did we choose X?” six months later, there’s no answer.

ADRs give decisions a durable, reviewable home. They are short by design — context, decision, rationale, consequences — and live alongside the code they govern.

When to use /adr

Section titled “When to use /adr”

Run /adr when a decision:

  • affects more than one ticket or component
  • chooses a major library, framework, or platform dependency
  • changes how a cross-cutting concern works (auth, data model, deployment, observability)
  • introduces a pattern that other tickets will follow
  • is hard to reverse later

Don’t use /adr for routine implementation details that only matter inside a single ticket. Keep those in the ticket’s spec.md.

How to create an ADR

Section titled “How to create an ADR”

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

Describe the decision context: what problem you’re solving, what constraints exist, and what alternatives you considered. The agent structures the rest.

What the agent produces

Section titled “What the agent produces”

An ADR file at artifacts/architecture/ADR-<NNN>-<short-title>.md with:

  • Status — Proposed, Accepted, Deprecated, or Superseded
  • Context — the problem or situation that led to the decision
  • Decision — the choice in clear terms
  • Rationale — why this over alternatives
  • Consequences — positive, negative, and neutral impacts
  • Related guidelines — Y### / Z### coding standards, if applicable

The ADR index

Section titled “The ADR index”

After creating or updating an ADR, the skill automatically rebuilds artifacts/architecture/README.md with an index of all ADRs. This index is the single entry point for discovering existing decisions.

The index is a flat list — one line per ADR with filename, status, and date:

The rebuild is always a full scan of all ADR-<NNN>-*.md files, so the index self-heals if it drifts out of sync.

How other commands use the index

Section titled “How other commands use the index”
  • /spec reads the index early in its workflow to identify ADRs relevant to the ticket being specified. It pre-populates the “Related ADRs” section with candidates.
  • /plan reads the index when generating the implementation strategy section, linking relevant ADRs in the related documentation.

Both commands fall back to scanning the directory directly if the index is missing.

How to review the output

Section titled “How to review the output”

Check that the ADR captures the why, not just the what. A good ADR lets someone who wasn’t in the room understand the trade-offs. If the consequences section is empty, the decision probably needs more thought.

Naming conventions

Section titled “Naming conventions”
  • File: ADR-<NNN>-<kebab-case-title>.md
  • Number: 3-digit sequential (001, 002, …)
  • Title: short and specific (what decision was made)

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • Output file: artifacts/architecture/ADR-<NNN>-<short-title>.md
  • Index file: artifacts/architecture/README.md
  • Generated by: /adr or $adr
  • Behavior source of truth: .claude/skills/adr/SKILL.md

Canonical sources (open in new tab):

  • .claude/commands/adr.md
  • .claude/skills/adr/SKILL.md
  • .claude/skills/adr/ADR-TEMPLATE.md

Related documentation

Section titled “Related documentation”
  • Architecture Decisions (ADRs) — when and how to record cross-cutting technical decisions
  • Artifacts Overview — how ADRs fit into the overall artifacts system

Update Context

Why this command exists

Section titled “Why this command exists”

The .claude/context/ directory is the shared knowledge base that agents load selectively when they need coding standards, project rules, or domain-specific guidance. If files grow too large, agents waste context window on irrelevant content. If indexes drift, agents can’t find the right rules.

/update-context keeps this structure healthy: files stay short, indexes stay accurate, and validation catches structural problems before they reach agents.

When to use /update-context

Section titled “When to use /update-context”

Run /update-context when you:

  • add, rename, or remove coding standards (Y/Z, PY/PZ, JY/JZ, TY/TZ)
  • restructure .claude/context/ files or directories
  • notice an agent loading stale or missing rules
  • want to verify that the context structure is valid after manual edits

You don’t need to run it for routine code changes — only when the context files themselves change.

How it works

Section titled “How it works”

What to provide

Section titled “What to provide”

Point the agent at the area you want to update. For example: “update the Python coding standards index” or “add a new rule to the TypeScript hundreds.” If you don’t specify, it reviews the full .claude/context/ tree.

What the agent does

Section titled “What the agent does”
  1. Locates entrypoints — root index, domain overviews, and topic indexes
  2. Applies size/split rules — targets <=80 lines per file; splits if needed
  3. Refreshes index summaries — each entry gets a 1-line “when to load” description
  4. Regenerates catalogs — runs generate_catalog.py if coding standards changed
  5. Formats and validates — make format-context and make validate-context must pass

File format

Section titled “File format”

Most files under .claude/context/ are XML (manifests and rulesets). A few entrypoints are Markdown (README.md, coding-standards.md, overview.md). The agent follows the schema at .claude/context/schema/context.xsd and the format rules in .claude/context/AGENTS.md.

How to review the output

Section titled “How to review the output”
  • Check that indexes match reality: every rule file should have a corresponding index entry, and vice versa
  • Check file sizes: no file should exceed ~80 lines
  • Run validation yourself if you want to double-check: make validate-context

Guardrails

Section titled “Guardrails”
  • Files stay short and minimal — no large appendices
  • Splitting is preferred over expanding single files
  • Standards are never duplicated in commands or skills — they reference canonical files
  • Filenames and IDs stay stable unless a structural change requires renaming

Canonical sources

Section titled “Canonical sources”
  • Generated by: /update-context or $update-context
  • Behavior source of truth: .claude/skills/update-context/SKILL.md

Canonical sources (open in new tab):

  • .claude/commands/update-context.md
  • .claude/skills/update-context/SKILL.md

Commands Index

This page is a compact index of all workflow entrypoints in this repository.

Source of Truth

Section titled “Source of Truth”
  • Behavior source of truth: .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md
  • Entrypoint wrappers: .claude/commands/<name>.md

Commands are intentionally lean and forward to same-name skills.

Use by Tool

Section titled “Use by Tool”

Use the invocation style your coding assistant supports:

  • Slash-command tools: /name
  • Skill-invocation tools (including Codex): $name

Both forms invoke the same workflow behavior.

Index

Section titled “Index”
WorkflowSlashSkillPrimary userSummary
Generate AGENTS/generate-agents$generate-agentsSetupGenerate AGENTS.md from .claude/skills/generate-agents/AGENTS-template.md
Pull Ticket/pull-ticket$pull-ticketDeveloperPull a user-specified Jira ticket into local context
Spec/spec$specDeveloperCreate ticket-level low-level design (spec.md)
Plan/plan$planDeveloperConvert a spec into a task-by-task plan (plan.md)
Implement/implement$implementDeveloperExecute a plan step-by-step (TDD by default)
PRD/prd$prdLeadCreate a PRD for an initiative/feature
Blueprint/blueprint$blueprintLeadCreate a project-level blueprint (blueprint.md)
Blueprint to Tickets/blueprint-to-tickets$blueprint-to-ticketsLeadDecompose a blueprint into epics/ticket files
Ticket to Jira/ticket-to-jira$ticket-to-jiraLeadExport generated epics/tickets to Jira (via MCP)
PR Review/pr-review$pr-reviewDeveloper + LeadTwo-pass review with review.md, traceability checks, and optional GitHub comments
ADR/adr$adrOverarchingCapture architecture decisions as ADRs
Update Context/update-context$update-contextOverarchingMaintain .claude/context structure and indexes

Detailed Workflow Guidance

Section titled “Detailed Workflow Guidance”

Detailed guidance lives in:

  • Developer Workflow
  • Pull Ticket
  • Spec (LLD)
  • Plan (Implementation)
  • Implement (Execution)
  • Lead Workflow
  • PRD (Product Requirements)
  • Blueprint (HLD)
  • Blueprint → Tickets
  • Ticket → Jira
  • PR Review (Preflight)
  • PR Review (Lead Gate)
  • ADR (Architecture Decision Record)
  • Update Context

Prerequisites: Some workflows require integrations. See Setup Guide.

Artifacts Overview

This directory contains all design artifacts, low-level designs, plans, and architectural documentation for the project.

Directory Structure

Section titled “Directory Structure”

What goes where

Section titled “What goes where”
  • Requirements (artifacts/requirements/): product intent and constraints captured as PRDs. Leads reference PRDs when producing blueprints. See Lead Workflow.
  • Tickets (artifacts/tickets/): the unit of delivery. Use this for specs, plans, and research tied to a single ticket or work item. See Tickets Guide.
  • Blueprints (artifacts/blueprints/): initiative-level architecture that spans multiple tickets and gets decomposed into ticket files. See Blueprints Guide.
  • Architecture decisions (artifacts/architecture/): cross-cutting decisions recorded as ADRs. See Architecture Decisions (ADRs).

How artifacts relate to workflows

Section titled “How artifacts relate to workflows”
  • The Lead workflow typically produces blueprints (and sometimes ADRs), then decomposes into tickets. See Lead Workflow.
  • The Developer workflow produces ticket-level spec.md and plan.md, then code + tests. See Developer Workflow.

Naming conventions (overview)

Section titled “Naming conventions (overview)”
  • Tickets: artifacts/tickets/{TICKET-ID}/ or artifacts/tickets/$NNN-{description}/ (details in Tickets Guide)
  • Blueprints: artifacts/blueprints/{id}--{name}/ (details in Blueprints Guide)
  • ADRs: artifacts/architecture/ADR-{NNN}-{title}.md (details in Architecture Decisions (ADRs))

Related documentation

Section titled “Related documentation”
  • Workflows - SDLC overview and workflow reading order
  • Commands Index - Slash-command reference table
  • Setup Guide - Prerequisites and integrations
  • Field Guide Overview - What this repository is and how to use it

Artifacts directory structure for BCG X Agentic Coding Field Guide

Tickets Guide

Ticket-level and ad-hoc work item artifacts live under artifacts/tickets/, including specs (LLDs), implementation plans, reviews, and research notes.

Directory Structure

Section titled “Directory Structure”

Quick Start

Section titled “Quick Start”

Working on a Ticket

Section titled “Working on a Ticket”

If you have a ticket ID (e.g., from Jira, GitHub, Linear):

Working Without a Ticket

Section titled “Working Without a Ticket”

If you’re doing internal work without a formal ticket:

File Descriptions

Section titled “File Descriptions”

spec.md (Ticket Spec)

Section titled “spec.md (Ticket Spec)”

Purpose: Define what needs to be built for this ticket at a detailed, implementation-ready level.

When to create:

  • Ticket requirements are unclear or complex
  • Need to agree on approach before implementation
  • Multiple team members will work on this
  • Ticket will be reviewed before implementation

What to include:

  • Ticket context and business value
  • Scope (in/out of scope)
  • Functional requirements and workflows
  • Edge cases and error handling
  • Technical approach and detailed design
  • Acceptance criteria
  • Test scenarios

Template: Use /spec command to create from template. (Source template: .claude/skills/spec/SPEC-TEMPLATE.md; templates/design-template.md points to it.)

Deep dive: See Spec (LLD) for labels, traceability, and templates.

Canonical template (open in new tab): .claude/skills/spec/SPEC-TEMPLATE.md

Key distinction from Blueprint:

  • Blueprint (HLD) - High-level architecture spanning multiple tickets
  • Spec (LLD) - Labeled requirements, interfaces, and implementation-ready details for a single ticket

plan.md (Implementation Plan)

Section titled “plan.md (Implementation Plan)”

Purpose: Break down how to implement this ticket into actionable tasks.

When to create:

  • Always (required for every ticket/work item)
  • Before starting implementation
  • To track progress across multiple sessions

What to include:

  • Overview and success criteria
  • Task breakdown with checkboxes
  • Implementation details (code structure, interfaces)
  • Acceptance criteria with validation methods
  • Testing requirements
  • Dependencies between tasks

Template: Use /plan command to create from template or design

Deep dive: See Plan (Implementation) for P/W/K/X structure, traceability, and validations.

Canonical template (open in new tab): .claude/skills/plan/PLAN-TEMPLATE.md

research/ (Investigation Notes)

Section titled “research/ (Investigation Notes)”

Purpose: Document research, options analysis, and decisions made during planning.

When to create:

  • Need to investigate codebase or external dependencies
  • Evaluating multiple approaches
  • Making significant technical decisions

Files:

  • investigation.md - Research findings, what you learned
  • options.md - Different approaches considered, pros/cons
  • decision.md - Final approach chosen and rationale

notes.md (Additional Context)

Section titled “notes.md (Additional Context)”

Purpose: Miscellaneous notes that don’t fit elsewhere.

When to create:

  • Have additional context that doesn’t fit in design or plan
  • Want to record challenges or gotchas
  • Need to document workarounds or temporary solutions

Naming Conventions

Section titled “Naming Conventions”

With Ticket ID

Section titled “With Ticket ID”

Use the exact ticket ID from your project management system:

SystemTicket ID ExampleDirectory Name
JiraPROJ-123artifacts/tickets/PROJ-123/
GitHubIssue 456artifacts/tickets/GH-456/
GitLab!789artifacts/tickets/GL-789/
LinearTEAM-567artifacts/tickets/TEAM-567/
TrelloCard abc123artifacts/tickets/TR-abc123/

Key rules:

  • Use the ticket ID exactly as it appears in your system
  • Remove special characters like #, ! (convert to prefix)
  • For GitHub: issue 123 becomes GH-123
  • For GitLab: !456 becomes GL-456

Without Ticket ID (Internal Work)

Section titled “Without Ticket ID (Internal Work)”

Use the $NNN-{description} format:

Format: $NNN-{description}

  • {NNN} - Three-digit number, auto-incremented (001, 002, 003…)
  • {description} - Short description (2-4 words, kebab-case)

Examples:

  • $001-auth-refactor - Refactoring authentication module
  • $002-docker-setup - Setting up Docker development environment
  • $003-api-migration - Migrating API to new version

How to determine the next number:

Workflow Patterns

Section titled “Workflow Patterns”

Pattern 1: Spec Plan Implement

Section titled “Pattern 1: Spec Plan Implement”

Best for: Complex tickets with unclear requirements

Result:

Pattern 2: Plan Implement

Section titled “Pattern 2: Plan Implement”

Best for: Clear tickets with well-defined requirements

Result:

Pattern 3: Explore Research Spec Plan

Section titled “Pattern 3: Explore Research Spec Plan”

Best for: Tickets requiring significant investigation

Result:

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. One Directory Per Ticket

Section titled “1. One Directory Per Ticket”

DO:

  • Create one directory per ticket/work item
  • Use ticket ID as directory name when available
  • Keep all related files together

DON’T:

  • Don’t create multiple directories for the same ticket
  • Don’t split related files across directories
  • Don’t reuse directories for different tickets

2. Use Fixed Filenames

Section titled “2. Use Fixed Filenames”

DO:

  • Always name spec as spec.md
  • Always name plan as plan.md
  • Use standard names for research files

DON’T:

  • Don’t use custom filenames like my-spec.md
  • Don’t create multiple specs (spec-v1.md, spec-v2.md)
  • Don’t use dates in filenames

3. Update, Don’t Duplicate

Section titled “3. Update, Don’t Duplicate”

DO:

  • Update existing spec.md or plan.md if requirements change
  • Add notes to existing files
  • Keep one source of truth

DON’T:

  • Don’t create spec-v2.md or plan-new.md
  • Don’t keep outdated versions
  • Don’t duplicate information across files

4. Create research/ for Investigations

Section titled “4. Create research/ for Investigations”

DO:

  • Create research/ subdirectory for investigation work
  • Document what you learned in investigation.md
  • Document decision rationale in decision.md

DON’T:

  • Don’t put research notes in spec.md or plan.md
  • Don’t create research files at the root of the ticket directory
  • Don’t skip documenting decisions

5. When you need a blueprint (HLD)

Section titled “5. When you need a blueprint (HLD)”

Tickets are the unit of delivery. If you need initiative-level design that spans multiple tickets, use a blueprint and decompose it into tickets.

See Blueprints Guide for blueprint conventions and how they relate to ticket specs.

Integration with Workflows

Section titled “Integration with Workflows”

Slash Commands

Section titled “Slash Commands”

The tickets directory integrates with these commands:

CommandWhat It DoesOutput
/pull-ticketPull a user-specified ticketartifacts/tickets/{ID}/ticket.md
/specCreate ticket specartifacts/tickets/{ID}/spec.md
/planCreate implementation planartifacts/tickets/{ID}/plan.md
/implementExecute plan step-by-stepUpdates plan checkboxes
/pr-reviewReview PR with ticket contextartifacts/tickets/{ID}/review.md

Typical Workflow

Section titled “Typical Workflow”

See Workflows for the complete four-stage SDLC and all available commands.

Summary

Section titled “Summary”

Key takeaways:

  1. One directory per ticket/work item
  2. Use {TICKET-ID}/ or $NNN-{description}/
  3. Fixed filenames: spec.md, plan.md, review.md (plus optional notes.md)
  4. Research goes in research/ subdirectory
  5. Update existing files, don’t duplicate
  6. Keep completed work for reference
  7. Understand HLD (blueprints) vs LLD (specs)

Quick reference:

  • Formal ticket? artifacts/tickets/PROJ-123/
  • No ticket? artifacts/tickets/$001-description/
  • Complex? Create spec.md then plan.md
  • Before merge? Run /pr-review to update review.md
  • Simple? Create just plan.md
  • Investigation? Create research/ first
  • HLD needed? Use /blueprint (not /spec)

Tickets directory guide for BCG X Agentic Coding Field Guide Last updated: 2026-02-20

Blueprints Guide

This directory contains project-level technical blueprints (high-level designs) that span multiple tickets and define the architecture for significant features or projects.

What is a Blueprint?

Section titled “What is a Blueprint?”

A blueprint is a high-level design (HLD) that provides architectural guidance for a project or major feature. Blueprints are typically created by Tech Leads or Architects and serve as the foundation for multiple implementation tickets.

Key characteristics:

  • Scope: Project-level, spanning multiple tickets/epics
  • Audience: Architects, tech leads, senior engineers
  • Purpose: Define architecture, major components, and technical decisions
  • Created by: Tech Leads, Architects (Lead Workflow)
  • Decomposed into: Multiple tickets and epics using /blueprint-to-tickets

Blueprint vs Spec

Section titled “Blueprint vs Spec”
AspectBlueprint (HLD)Spec (LLD)
ScopeProject/feature with multiple ticketsSingle ticket
LevelHigh-level architectureLow-level implementation
Created byTech Leads/ArchitectsIndividual Contributors
Locationartifacts/blueprints/artifacts/tickets/{ID}/spec.md
Command/blueprint/spec
Decomposition/blueprint-to-tickets Epics + TicketsN/A (already ticket-level)

Quick Start

Section titled “Quick Start”

Creating a Blueprint

Section titled “Creating a Blueprint”

Use the /blueprint command:

This creates a new blueprint in this directory following the template structure.

Decomposing a Blueprint into Tickets

Section titled “Decomposing a Blueprint into Tickets”

Once the blueprint is complete, use /blueprint-to-tickets to decompose it:

This analyzes the blueprint and creates:

  • Epics: High-level work packages
  • Tickets: Actionable implementation tasks

Directory Structure

Section titled “Directory Structure”

Naming Convention

Section titled “Naming Convention”

Format: artifacts/blueprints/{blueprint-id}--{name}/blueprint.md

Examples:

  • artifacts/blueprints/001--progress-tracking/blueprint.md
  • artifacts/blueprints/proj-123--api-gateway/blueprint.md
  • artifacts/blueprints/team-456--user-authentication/blueprint.md

Key rules:

  • Use a stable blueprint-id (project/epic ID or other stable identifier)
  • Use kebab-case for name
  • Keep name concise (2-4 words)
  • Set id in blueprint.md frontmatter; tooling should treat this as canonical
  • If frontmatter id is missing, tooling may fall back to parsing folder names only when using the -- delimiter between {blueprint-id} and {name}
  • Do not parse IDs by splitting on single hyphens in folder names because IDs may contain hyphens

When to Create a Blueprint

Section titled “When to Create a Blueprint”

Create a blueprint when:

  • Starting a new project or major feature
  • Building something that spans multiple tickets
  • Need to define architecture before implementation
  • Multiple teams or engineers will work on related tickets
  • Making significant technical decisions that affect multiple components

Don’t create a blueprint for:

  • Single ticket work (use /spec instead)
  • Small bug fixes or enhancements
  • Work that doesn’t require architectural planning

Blueprint Lifecycle

Section titled “Blueprint Lifecycle”
  1. Draft: Initial blueprint created, under review
  2. Approved: Blueprint reviewed and ready for decomposition
  3. In Progress: Tickets being implemented
  4. Completed: All related tickets implemented

What to Include

Section titled “What to Include”

A complete blueprint should include:

1. Context

Section titled “1. Context”
  • Business problem and value
  • Technical context and constraints
  • Goals and non-goals

2. Architecture

Section titled “2. Architecture”
  • High-level design overview
  • Major components and their responsibilities
  • System interactions and data flow

3. Technical Decisions

Section titled “3. Technical Decisions”
  • Key architectural decisions
  • Alternatives considered
  • Rationale and implications

4. Implementation Plan

Section titled “4. Implementation Plan”
  • Phases and milestones
  • Epics and estimated tickets
  • Dependencies and risks

5. Quality Attributes

Section titled “5. Quality Attributes”
  • Security considerations
  • Performance requirements
  • Scalability strategy
  • Testing approach

See templates/blueprint-template.md for the complete template structure, or use the /blueprint command which uses this template.

Where it fits

Section titled “Where it fits”

Blueprints are part of the Lead workflow: they describe initiative-level architecture that later gets decomposed into tickets.

See Lead Workflow for the end-to-end process and Commands Index for the full command list.

Best Practices

Section titled “Best Practices”

1. Focus on Architecture

Section titled “1. Focus on Architecture”

DO:

  • Define major components and their boundaries
  • Document key technical decisions
  • Describe system interactions
  • Consider scalability and performance

DON’T:

  • Include ticket-level implementation details
  • Write code or pseudocode
  • Get too detailed on specific functions

2. Make Decisions Explicit

Section titled “2. Make Decisions Explicit”

DO:

  • Document why decisions were made
  • List alternatives considered
  • Explain trade-offs
  • Note implications

DON’T:

  • Leave decisions implicit
  • Skip rationale
  • Ignore alternatives

3. Plan for Implementation

Section titled “3. Plan for Implementation”

DO:

  • Break down into logical phases
  • Identify dependencies
  • Estimate complexity
  • Plan for incremental delivery

DON’T:

  • Create one massive blueprint for everything
  • Ignore dependencies between components
  • Skip risk assessment

4. Keep It Updated

Section titled “4. Keep It Updated”

DO:

  • Update blueprint if architecture changes
  • Document deviations from original plan
  • Record lessons learned

DON’T:

  • Let blueprint become stale
  • Create multiple versions
  • Forget to update after major changes

Integration with Tickets

Section titled “Integration with Tickets”

Blueprints should reference related tickets, and tickets should reference their parent blueprint:

In blueprint:

In ticket design:

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Good Blueprint Names

Section titled “Good Blueprint Names”
  • blueprint-GENX-001-progress-tracking.md
  • blueprint-AUTH-001-user-authentication.md
  • blueprint-API-002-rate-limiting.md

Good Blueprint Structure

Section titled “Good Blueprint Structure”
  • Clear context and problem statement
  • High-level architecture diagram
  • Major components with clear responsibilities
  • Explicit technical decisions with rationale
  • Phased implementation plan
  • Security and performance considerations

Resources

Section titled “Resources”
  • Template: templates/blueprint-template.md - Complete blueprint template
  • Command: /blueprint - Create new blueprint
  • Command: /blueprint-to-tickets - Decompose into tickets
  • Workflows - SDLC overview and workflow reading order
  • Commands Index - Slash-command reference table
  • Setup Guide - Prerequisites for Jira integration

Blueprints directory guide for BCG X Agentic Coding Field Guide Last updated: 2025-11-11

Architecture Decisions (ADRs)

Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) capture important technical decisions that affect more than one ticket or component, along with context and trade-offs.

They’re designed to be short, durable, and reviewable in a PR.

When to write an ADR

Section titled “When to write an ADR”

Write an ADR when you need to record a decision such as:

  • choosing a major library, framework, or platform dependency
  • changing how a cross-cutting concern works (auth, data model, deployment, observability)
  • introducing a new architecture pattern that will be reused

Don’t write an ADR for routine implementation details that only matter inside a single ticket; keep those in the ticket’s spec.md and plan.md.

Where ADRs live

Section titled “Where ADRs live”

Store ADRs under:

Naming conventions

Section titled “Naming conventions”
  • Use ADR-{NNN}-{kebab-case-title}.md
  • Keep the title short and specific (what decision was made)
  • Increment {NNN} sequentially within the repo

How to create one

Section titled “How to create one”

  1. Copy the template:

  2. Fill in context, decision, rationale, and consequences.

  3. Link the ADR from the relevant ticket (artifacts/tickets/{ID}/spec.md) or blueprint.

  4. Submit in the same PR as the change (or immediately before it).

Related documentation

Section titled “Related documentation”
  • Artifacts Overview — how ADRs fit into the overall artifacts system
  • Tickets Guide — ticket-level specs and plans
  • Blueprints Guide — initiative-level architecture and decomposition
