View raw XML source

py125 enforce immutability via types

PY125-ENFORCE-IMMUTABILITY-VIA-TYPES

Section titled “PY125-ENFORCE-IMMUTABILITY-VIA-TYPES”

Enforce immutability through type hints (e.g., Sequence, Mapping, AbstractSet) rather than creating immutable copies of collections.

When To Use

Section titled “When To Use”
  • Return types: Use Sequence[T] instead of list[T] when callers shouldn’t modify
  • Return types: Use Mapping[K, V] instead of dict[K, V] when callers shouldn’t modify
  • Return types: Use AbstractSet[T] instead of set[T] when callers shouldn’t modify
  • Parameters: Use Sequence[T] for flexible input (accepts list, tuple, etc.)
  • Parameters: Use Mapping[K, V] for flexible input (accepts dict, etc.)
  • Parameters: Use AbstractSet[T] for flexible input (accepts set, frozenset, etc.)

Rationale

Section titled “Rationale”

Type hints provide compile-time immutability guarantees without runtime overhead. Creating immutable copies (e.g., tuple(my_list), frozenset(my_set)) adds unnecessary performance costs. Static type checking via mypy enforces immutability contracts.

Tool Support

Section titled “Tool Support”

mypy enforces method availability based on type. Sequence, Mapping, and AbstractSet don’t have mutation methods.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Good Example 2

Section titled “Good Example 2”

Good Example 3

Section titled “Good Example 3”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Bad Example 2

Section titled “Bad Example 2”

Bad Example 3

Section titled “Bad Example 3”

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY101-PRECISE-TYPES
  • PY106-COLLECTION-TYPES
  • PY300-IMMUTABILITY

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Import Note: Use from collections.abc import Set as AbstractSet to avoid conflicts with the built-in set type.

View raw XML source

py126 use metaclass abcmeta

PY126-USE-METACLASS-ABCMETA

Section titled “PY126-USE-METACLASS-ABCMETA”

When using abstract base classes, use metaclass=ABCMeta with @abstractmethod decorator and ellipsis body.

When To Use

Section titled “When To Use”
  • Defining abstract base classes with methods subclasses must implement
  • Creating interfaces with semantic inheritance (use Protocol for structural typing)
  • Combining with @property, @classmethod, @staticmethod for various abstract members

Rationale

Section titled “Rationale”
  • metaclass=ABCMeta keeps MRO simpler than inheriting from ABC
  • @abstractmethod decorator provides compile-time checking that subclasses implement methods
  • Ellipsis (...) is the modern, clean way to indicate “no implementation”
  • raise NotImplementedError is runtime checking; @abstractmethod is compile-time

Tool Support

Section titled “Tool Support”
  • mypy enforces @abstractmethod at type-check time
  • Python raises TypeError if abstract class is instantiated
  • @abstractmethod works with @property, @classmethod, @staticmethod

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Bad Example 2

Section titled “Bad Example 2”

Bad Example 3

Section titled “Bad Example 3”

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY102-USE-PROTOCOLS
  • PY117-SELF-TYPE
  • PY127-DEFINE-CONTRACTS-IN-CODE

View raw XML source

py127 define contracts in code

PY127-DEFINE-CONTRACTS-IN-CODE

Section titled “PY127-DEFINE-CONTRACTS-IN-CODE”

Define contracts explicitly in code (class attributes with __init__ methods, abstract properties) instead of documenting them in docstrings.

When To Use

Section titled “When To Use”
  • Base classes expecting subclasses to define attributes
  • Protocols that require specific attributes
  • Classes with mandatory initialization parameters
  • Any case where you’d write # type: ignore[attr-defined]

Rationale

Section titled “Rationale”

Code-based contracts are enforced by type checkers and provide better IDE support. Documentation-only contracts require # type: ignore comments and can drift from actual usage. Defining both attributes AND initialization ensures proper contract enforcement.

Tool Support

Section titled “Tool Support”

mypy catches missing attributes in subclasses. Dataclasses work seamlessly (fields override class attributes).

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY100-USE-TYPE-HINTS
  • PY102-USE-PROTOCOLS
  • PZ102-IGNORING-TYPE-ERRORS

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Good patterns: - Declare attributes at class level: id: str - Provide __init__ method that initializes them: self.id = id - Abstract properties with @property @abstractmethod for computed values - Type stubs (.pyi files) for third-party libraries

View raw XML source

py128 no export typevars

PY128-NO-EXPORT-TYPEVARS

Section titled “PY128-NO-EXPORT-TYPEVARS”

Never export TypeVar definitions in module all; TypeVars are implementation details, not public API.

Rationale

Section titled “Rationale”

TypeVars are internal type machinery used for generic definitions. Exporting them pollutes the module’s public API and suggests they should be used by callers, which is incorrect. With Python 3.12+ syntax, TypeVars become even more internal as they’re declared inline.

Tool Support

Section titled “Tool Support”

ruff can check all consistency with PLE0604.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Good Example 2

Section titled “Good Example 2”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY103-USE-GENERICS
  • PY201-PUBLIC-API

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Python 3.12+ Migration: When migrating to Python 3.12+ syntax, remove all exported TypeVars as they’re no longer defined separately.
  • When TypeVars appear in signatures: Even if a TypeVar appears in public function signatures, don’t export it. Users should reference the generic class/function, not the TypeVar directly.

View raw XML source

py129 prefer public attributes

PY129-PREFER-PUBLIC-ATTRIBUTES

Section titled “PY129-PREFER-PUBLIC-ATTRIBUTES”

Prefer public attributes for simple stored values over private attributes with @property decorators.

Rationale

Section titled “Rationale”
  • Type hints provide compile-time encapsulation without runtime overhead (PY125-ENFORCE-IMMUTABILITY-VIA-TYPES)
  • Properties should be reserved for computed values, validation, or lazy initialization (PZ205-PROPERTY-WITH-SIDE-EFFECTS)
  • Python’s Uniform Access Principle allows refactoring public attributes to properties later without breaking callers
  • Avoid premature abstraction (YAGNI) - don’t add indirection until needed

Tool Support

Section titled “Tool Support”
  • mypy enforces type hints regardless of public/private
  • ruff can detect unnecessary properties (consider adding custom rule)

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY125-ENFORCE-IMMUTABILITY-VIA-TYPES
  • PY301-FROZEN-DATACLASSES
  • PZ205-PROPERTY-WITH-SIDE-EFFECTS
  • Y206-LAZY-INITIALIZATION

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Example (Bad - Unnecessary Property): python class Config: """Over-engineered configuration.""" def __init__(self, port: int) -&gt; None: self._port = port # Private backing field @property def port(self) -&gt; int: """Unnecessary property - just returns stored value.""" return self._port # No computation, validation, or logic! # BAD: Every access pays function call overhead for no benefit print(config.port) # Slower than direct attribute access
  • Example (Good - Property for Computed Value): python class Circle: """Circle with radius and computed area.""" def __init__(self, radius: float) -&gt; None: self.radius = radius # Public - simple stored value @property def area(self) -&gt; float: """Computed value - appropriate use of @property.""" return 3.14159 * self.radius ** 2
  • Example (Good - Property for Validation): python class Server: """Server with port validation.""" def __init__(self, port: int) -&gt; None: self.port = port # Triggers setter validation @property def port(self) -&gt; int: return self._port @port.setter def port(self, value: int) -&gt; None: """Validate port range.""" if not (1024 &lt;= value &lt;= 65535): raise ValueError(f"Port must be 1024-65535, got {value}") self._port = value
  • Example (Good - Public Attributes): python from dataclasses import dataclass @dataclass(frozen=True) class Config: """Simple configuration with public immutable attributes.""" host: str port: int timeout_seconds: float = 30.0 # Usage - direct access, zero overhead config = Config(host="localhost", port=8080) print(config.port) # Direct attribute access
  • Refactoring Path: Public attributes can be refactored to properties later without breaking callers: python # Version 1: Simple public attribute class User: def __init__(self, email: str) -&gt; None: self.email = email # Version 2: Refactored to property with validation (callers unchanged!) class User: def __init__(self, email: str) -&gt; None: self.email = email # Still works - triggers setter @property def email(self) -&gt; str: return self._email @email.setter def email(self, value: str) -&gt; None: if "@" not in value: raise ValueError("Invalid email") self._email = value
  • When to Use @property: - Computed values calculated on access - Validation required on setting (with @property.setter) - Lazy initialization (use @cached_property from functools) - Mutable state requiring controlled access - Complex logic or side effects on access
  • When to Use Public Attributes: - Simple stored values (not computed on access) - Immutable after initialization - No validation or transformation needed - Frequently accessed (zero overhead matters)

View raw XML source

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Public API and InterfacesExports, privacy, package contracts../py20x-public-api.xml
Imports and PackagingImport style, ordering, packaging rules../py21x-imports-and-packaging.xml

View raw XML source

Public API and Interfaces

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY200-PACKAGE-INITUse __init__.py to expose public API using from ... import *.
PY201-PUBLIC-APIDefine __all__ in each module (not in __init__.py) to control the public API.
PY202-EXPLICIT-EXPORTSBe explicit when importing from other modules.
PY203-PRIVATE-MODULESUse _module.py prefix for internal/private modules.
PY204-INIT-DEFINES-BEHAVIORConstructor parameters define instance behavior and configuration; method parameters are call-specific data that varies per invocation.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY200-PACKAGE-INIT

Section titled “PY200-PACKAGE-INIT”

Use __init__.py to expose public API using from ... import *.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Don’t explicitly import and list names in __init__.py - use wildcard imports instead.

Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”

Each private module defines its own __all__ to control what gets exported.

  • Related: PY201-PUBLIC-API

Back to Rule Catalog

PY201-PUBLIC-API

Section titled “PY201-PUBLIC-API”

Define __all__ in each module (not in __init__.py) to control the public API.

  • Tool Support: ruff checks __all__ consistency.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

The package __init__.py uses from ._parser import * to re-export these names.

  • Related: PY200-PACKAGE-INIT

Back to Rule Catalog

PY202-EXPLICIT-EXPORTS

Section titled “PY202-EXPLICIT-EXPORTS”

Be explicit when importing from other modules. Avoid import * in application code.

  • Exceptions: Wildcard imports are appropriate in __init__.py files for re-exporting public APIs (see PY200-PACKAGE-INIT).

  • Tool Support: ruff F403 warns on wildcard imports (except in __init__.py).

  • Good Example: Import specific names: from orders.models import Order, OrderItem not from orders.models import *.

  • Related: PY200-PACKAGE-INIT, PY201-PUBLIC-API

Back to Rule Catalog

PY203-PRIVATE-MODULES

Section titled “PY203-PRIVATE-MODULES”

Use _module.py prefix for internal/private modules.

  • Good Example: _internal.py, _helpers.py signal “don’t import externally”.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY204-INIT-DEFINES-BEHAVIOR

Section titled “PY204-INIT-DEFINES-BEHAVIOR”

Constructor parameters define instance behavior and configuration; method parameters are call-specific data that varies per invocation.

  • Good Example: TransactionValidator(min_amount=0) configures behavior in __init__, then validate(transaction) accepts data to process.

  • Related: Y100-SINGLE-RESPONSIBILITY, Y103-DEPENDENCY-INJECTION, Z114-TEMPORARY-FIELD

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Imports and Packaging

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY210-IMPORT-STYLEPrefer absolute imports for public modules; use relative imports for private (underscore-prefixed) modules within packages.
PY211-IMPORT-ORDERStandard library, third-party, local.
PY212-CIRCULAR-IMPORTSAvoid circular dependencies.
PY213-LAZY-IMPORTSImport at module level by default.
PY214-TYPE-STUBSFor untyped third-party libs, use stub files or py.typed.
PY215-NAMESPACE-PACKAGESAvoid namespace packages unless distributing plugins.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY210-IMPORT-STYLE

Section titled “PY210-IMPORT-STYLE”

Prefer absolute imports for public modules; use relative imports for private (underscore-prefixed) modules within packages.

When To Use

Section titled “When To Use”

  • Relative imports: importing _module.py from another module in same package

  • Relative imports: private cross-imports within package implementation

  • Absolute imports: importing public modules from other packages

  • Avoid relative imports in top-level scripts outside packages

  • Rationale: Relative imports for private modules emphasize package-internal structure and make refactoring easier. They signal that these imports are implementation details, not public dependencies. Absolute imports for public modules make code more portable and explicit.

  • Tool Support: ruff I252 can enforce relative import rules. ruff I001-I005 enforce general import style.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Good Example 2
Section titled “Good Example 2”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
Bad Example 2
Section titled “Bad Example 2”
  • Related: PY201-PUBLIC-API, PY203-PRIVATE-MODULES, PY212-CIRCULAR-IMPORTS

Back to Rule Catalog

PY211-IMPORT-ORDER

Section titled “PY211-IMPORT-ORDER”

Standard library, third-party, local. Alphabetical within groups.

  • Exceptions: Pre-commit hooks auto-format this.
  • Tool Support: ruff I001 enforces isort-compatible ordering. Back to Rule Catalog

PY212-CIRCULAR-IMPORTS

Section titled “PY212-CIRCULAR-IMPORTS”

Avoid circular dependencies. Use protocols or TYPE_CHECKING.

  • Good Example: See PY120-TYPE-CHECKING for avoiding circular imports.

  • Related: PY120-TYPE-CHECKING, Z101-CIRCULAR-DEPS

Back to Rule Catalog

PY213-LAZY-IMPORTS

Section titled “PY213-LAZY-IMPORTS”

Import at module level by default. Use local imports only for optional dependencies or circular imports.

  • Good Example: if TYPE_CHECKING: from expensive_module import Heavy to avoid runtime cost.

  • Related: PY120-TYPE-CHECKING

Back to Rule Catalog

PY214-TYPE-STUBS

Section titled “PY214-TYPE-STUBS”

For untyped third-party libs, use stub files or py.typed.

  • Good Example: pandas-stubs for pandas type hints.

  • Related: PY100-USE-TYPE-HINTS

Back to Rule Catalog

PY215-NAMESPACE-PACKAGES

Section titled “PY215-NAMESPACE-PACKAGES”

Avoid namespace packages unless distributing plugins.

  • Good Example: Use regular packages with __init__.py for standard projects.

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Rules

py300 immutability

PY300-IMMUTABILITY

Section titled “PY300-IMMUTABILITY”

Prefer immutable data structures. Use frozen=True for dataclasses.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

@dataclass(frozen=True) prevents modification after creation.

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY114-NAMEDTUPLE-TYPES
  • PY301-FROZEN-DATACLASSES

View raw XML source

py301 frozen dataclasses

PY301-FROZEN-DATACLASSES

Section titled “PY301-FROZEN-DATACLASSES”

Use frozen=True for dataclasses that shouldn’t change.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

@dataclass(frozen=True) class Transaction: ...

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY112-DATACLASS-TYPES
  • PY300-IMMUTABILITY

View raw XML source

py302 pure functions

PY302-PURE-FUNCTIONS

Section titled “PY302-PURE-FUNCTIONS”

Functions without side effects, same input → same output.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

def calculate_tax(amount: Decimal) -&gt; Decimal: return amount * TAX_RATE

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY303-AVOID-SIDE-EFFECTS

View raw XML source

py303 avoid side effects

PY303-AVOID-SIDE-EFFECTS

Section titled “PY303-AVOID-SIDE-EFFECTS”

Don’t modify arguments or global state. Return new values.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

def uppercase_merchant(txn: Transaction) -&gt; Transaction: return replace(txn, merchant=txn.merchant.upper())

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY302-PURE-FUNCTIONS
  • PZ200-MUTATING-ARGUMENTS

View raw XML source

py304 return new values

PY304-RETURN-NEW-VALUES

Section titled “PY304-RETURN-NEW-VALUES”

Return new objects instead of modifying existing ones.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Use dataclasses.replace() to create modified copy.

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY300-IMMUTABILITY

View raw XML source

py305 generators

PY305-GENERATORS

Section titled “PY305-GENERATORS”

Use generators with yield for lazy evaluation and memory efficiency. Generators compute values on-demand rather than creating entire sequences in memory.

When To Use

Section titled “When To Use”
  • Processing large datasets or files line-by-line
  • Transforming sequences where not all values may be consumed
  • Creating infinite sequences
  • Chaining operations that don’t need materialization

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Building lists unnecessarily:

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY306-LAZY-EVALUATION
  • PY602-GENERATOR-VS-LIST
  • Y224-ITERATOR-PATTERN

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Return Type: Annotate with Iterator[T] from collections.abc, not tuple or list.

View raw XML source

py306 lazy evaluation

PY306-LAZY-EVALUATION

Section titled “PY306-LAZY-EVALUATION”

Prefer lazy evaluation over eager computation. Compute values only when actually needed. This reduces memory usage, enables working with infinite sequences, and can improve performance by avoiding unnecessary computation.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Good Example 2

Section titled “Good Example 2”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY305-GENERATORS
  • PY310-ITERTOOLS
  • PY602-GENERATOR-VS-LIST

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Key Techniques: - Generators and generator expressions (not list comprehensions) - itertools for lazy transformations (chain, islice, takewhile, etc.) - Return Iterator[T] instead of list[T] or tuple[T] when possible - Let callers materialize with list() only if they need the full sequence
  • Performance Benefits: - Memory: O(1) vs O(n) for large sequences - Speed: Can start consuming immediately (no upfront delay) - Composability: Chain operations without intermediate lists
  • Trade-offs: - Can only iterate once (unless using itertools.tee()) - Can’t index or get length without consuming - Debugging harder (can’t inspect full sequence)
  • When to Materialize: - Need random access or indexing - Need length without consuming - Need to iterate multiple times - Returning from API where caller doesn’t control iteration

View raw XML source

py307 comprehensions

PY307-COMPREHENSIONS

Section titled “PY307-COMPREHENSIONS”

Use list/dict/set comprehensions for transformations.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

[txn.amount for txn in txns if txn.amount &gt; 100]

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY308-MAP-FILTER

View raw XML source

py308 map filter

PY308-MAP-FILTER

Section titled “PY308-MAP-FILTER”

Prefer comprehensions over map()/filter() for clarity.

Exceptions

Section titled “Exceptions”

Use map/filter when you already have a named function.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

[x*2 for x in nums] clearer than list(map(lambda x: x*2, nums)).

View raw XML source

py309 functools

PY309-FUNCTOOLS

Section titled “PY309-FUNCTOOLS”

Use functools for functional patterns: reduce, partial, lru_cache.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

from functools import lru_cache; @lru_cache; def expensive(...): ...

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY604-CACHING

View raw XML source

Functional Tooling

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY310-ITERTOOLSUse itertools for efficient iteration patterns.
PY311-OPERATOR-MODULEUse operator module instead of lambdas for common operations.
PY312-DATACLASS-REPLACEUse dataclasses.replace() for immutable updates.
PY313-AVOID-MUTABLE-DEFAULTSNever use mutable default arguments.
PY314-CLOSURESUse closures for encapsulation and factory functions.
PY315-FIRST-CLASS-FUNCTIONSPass functions as arguments, return from functions.
PY316-HIGHER-ORDER-FUNCTIONSFunctions that take or return functions.
PY317-RECURSIONUse recursion for naturally recursive problems (trees, etc.).
PY318-TAIL-RECURSIONPython doesn’t optimize tail recursion.
PY319-IMMUTABLE-COLLECTIONSUse tuple, frozenset for immutable collections.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY310-ITERTOOLS

Section titled “PY310-ITERTOOLS”

Use itertools for efficient iteration patterns.

  • Good Example: itertools.chain(), groupby(), accumulate().

  • Related: PY305-GENERATORS

Back to Rule Catalog

PY311-OPERATOR-MODULE

Section titled “PY311-OPERATOR-MODULE”

Use operator module instead of lambdas for common operations.

  • Good Example: sorted(txns, key=operator.attrgetter('date')) not sorted(txns, key=lambda t: t.date).

Back to Rule Catalog

PY312-DATACLASS-REPLACE

Section titled “PY312-DATACLASS-REPLACE”

Use dataclasses.replace() for immutable updates.

  • Good Example: updated = replace(txn, amount=Decimal("100")).

  • Related: PY300-IMMUTABILITY

Back to Rule Catalog

PY313-AVOID-MUTABLE-DEFAULTS

Section titled “PY313-AVOID-MUTABLE-DEFAULTS”

Never use mutable default arguments.

  • Good Example: def func(items: list[int] | None = None) not def func(items: list[int] = []).

  • Related: PZ109-MUTABLE-DEFAULTS

Back to Rule Catalog

PY314-CLOSURES

Section titled “PY314-CLOSURES”

Use closures for encapsulation and factory functions.

  • Good Example: def make_adder(n): return lambda x: x + n.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY315-FIRST-CLASS-FUNCTIONS

Section titled “PY315-FIRST-CLASS-FUNCTIONS”

Pass functions as arguments, return from functions.

  • Good Example: def apply(f: Callable, x): return f(x).

  • Related: PY113-CALLABLE-TYPES

Back to Rule Catalog

PY316-HIGHER-ORDER-FUNCTIONS

Section titled “PY316-HIGHER-ORDER-FUNCTIONS”

Functions that take or return functions.

  • Good Example: Decorators, sorted(key=...), map(), filter().

  • Related: Y212-DECORATOR-PATTERN

Back to Rule Catalog

PY317-RECURSION

Section titled “PY317-RECURSION”

Use recursion for naturally recursive problems (trees, etc.).

  • Exceptions: Python has recursion limit (~1000). Use iteration for deep structures. Back to Rule Catalog

PY318-TAIL-RECURSION

Section titled “PY318-TAIL-RECURSION”

Python doesn’t optimize tail recursion. Use iteration instead.

  • Good Example: Use while loop, not tail-recursive function.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY319-IMMUTABLE-COLLECTIONS

Section titled “PY319-IMMUTABLE-COLLECTIONS”

Use tuple, frozenset for immutable collections.

  • Good Example: ALLOWED_CATEGORIES: Final = frozenset(["Food", "Transport"]).

  • Related: PY108-FINAL

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Advanced Techniques

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY320-AVOID-GLOBALSMinimize global mutable state.
PY321-REFERENTIAL-TRANSPARENCYFunction calls can be replaced with their return values.
PY322-PIPELINE-PATTERNChain transformations using function composition.
PY323-MONADSConsider Result/Option types for error handling (via libraries).
PY324-PARTIAL-APPLICATIONUse functools.partial to pre-fill function arguments.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY320-AVOID-GLOBALS

Section titled “PY320-AVOID-GLOBALS”

Minimize global mutable state. Use parameters/returns.

  • Good Example: Pass configuration as parameter, don’t use global config dict.

  • Related: PZ201-GLOBAL-STATE

Back to Rule Catalog

PY321-REFERENTIAL-TRANSPARENCY

Section titled “PY321-REFERENTIAL-TRANSPARENCY”

Function calls can be replaced with their return values.

  • Good Example: Pure functions are referentially transparent.

  • Related: PY302-PURE-FUNCTIONS

Back to Rule Catalog

PY322-PIPELINE-PATTERN

Section titled “PY322-PIPELINE-PATTERN”

Chain transformations using function composition.

  • Good Example: data |&gt; parse |&gt; validate |&gt; transform (via libraries or custom operator).

  • Related: PY305-GENERATORS

Back to Rule Catalog

PY323-MONADS

Section titled “PY323-MONADS”

Consider Result/Option types for error handling (via libraries).

  • Exceptions: Python idiom uses exceptions; monads less common.
  • Good Example: Result[T, Error] instead of exceptions for expected failures.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY324-PARTIAL-APPLICATION

Section titled “PY324-PARTIAL-APPLICATION”

Use functools.partial to pre-fill function arguments.

  • Good Example: from functools import partial; process_all = partial(map, process_one).

  • Related: PY309-FUNCTOOLS

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Docstrings and DocumentationDocstring format, coverage, layout../py40x-docstrings.xml
Formatting and StringsQuotes, f-strings, concatenation../py41x-formatting-and-strings.xml

View raw XML source

Docstrings and Documentation

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY400-RST-DOCSTRINGSUse reStructuredText format for docstrings.
PY401-DOCSTRING-COVERAGEDocument all public functions, classes, and modules.
PY402-MODULE-DOCSTRINGSEvery module has a docstring explaining its purpose.
PY403-ONE-LINE-SUMMARYFirst line of docstring is a brief summary, then a blank line, then details.
PY404-TYPE-HINTS-AS-DOCSType hints document expected types; don’t repeat in docstrings.
PY405-EXAMPLES-IN-DOCSTRINGSInclude usage examples in docstrings for complex functions.
PY406-LINE-LENGTHMax 88 characters per line (Black standard).
PY407-BLANK-LINESTwo blank lines between top-level definitions, one within classes.
PY408-INDENTATION4 spaces per indentation level.
PY409-CONTRACT-BASED-PRIVACYUse underscore prefix only for internal implementation details.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY400-RST-DOCSTRINGS

Section titled “PY400-RST-DOCSTRINGS”

Use reStructuredText format for docstrings.

  • Tool Support: Sphinx uses RST docstrings.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

PY401-DOCSTRING-COVERAGE

Section titled “PY401-DOCSTRING-COVERAGE”

Document all public functions, classes, and modules.

  • Tool Support: ruff D100-D107 (some relaxed in pyproject.toml).
  • Good Example: Every def in public API has a docstring.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY402-MODULE-DOCSTRINGS

Section titled “PY402-MODULE-DOCSTRINGS”

Every module has a docstring explaining its purpose.

  • Good Example: """CSV data parser module.""" at top of file.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY403-ONE-LINE-SUMMARY

Section titled “PY403-ONE-LINE-SUMMARY”

First line of docstring is a brief summary, then a blank line, then details.

  • Good Example: """Parse transaction.\n\nDetailed description here...""".

Back to Rule Catalog

PY404-TYPE-HINTS-AS-DOCS

Section titled “PY404-TYPE-HINTS-AS-DOCS”

Type hints document expected types; don’t repeat in docstrings.

  • Good Example: Don’t write :param text: str - the text when signature has text: str.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY405-EXAMPLES-IN-DOCSTRINGS

Section titled “PY405-EXAMPLES-IN-DOCSTRINGS”

Include usage examples in docstrings for complex functions.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

PY406-LINE-LENGTH

Section titled “PY406-LINE-LENGTH”

Max 88 characters per line (Black standard).

  • Tool Support: ruff E501 (relaxed for notebooks in pyproject.toml). Back to Rule Catalog

PY407-BLANK-LINES

Section titled “PY407-BLANK-LINES”

Two blank lines between top-level definitions, one within classes.

  • Tool Support: ruff E301-E306 enforce. Back to Rule Catalog

PY408-INDENTATION

Section titled “PY408-INDENTATION”

4 spaces per indentation level.

  • Tool Support: ruff formatter enforces. Back to Rule Catalog

PY409-CONTRACT-BASED-PRIVACY

Section titled “PY409-CONTRACT-BASED-PRIVACY”

Use underscore prefix only for internal implementation details. Attributes that represent observable state or configuration should be public with a read-only contract.

  • Related: PY112-DATACLASS-TYPES, PY300-IMMUTABILITY

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Make attributes PRIVATE (underscore prefix): 1. Helper methods implementing public methods 2. Internal caches and performance optimizations 3. Internal data structures for public APIs 4. Runtime guards and one-time flags
  • Make attributes PUBLIC (no underscore): 1. Configuration parameters passed to __init__ 2. Observable state external code needs to read 3. Computed properties already exposed via @property

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Formatting and Strings

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY410-DOUBLE-UNDERSCOREAvoid __name__ (dunder) unless implementing protocols.
PY411-STRING-QUOTESConsistent quote style.
PY412-F-STRINGSUse f-strings for formatting, not % or .format().
PY413-STRING-CONCATENATIONUse f-strings or str.join(), not + in loops.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY410-DOUBLE-UNDERSCORE

Section titled “PY410-DOUBLE-UNDERSCORE”

Avoid __name__ (dunder) unless implementing protocols.

  • Good Example: __init__, __str__ OK. Don’t use __my_var for privacy.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY411-STRING-QUOTES

Section titled “PY411-STRING-QUOTES”

Consistent quote style. Double quotes " preferred.

  • Tool Support: ruff formatter enforces consistent quotes. Back to Rule Catalog

PY412-F-STRINGS

Section titled “PY412-F-STRINGS”

Use f-strings for formatting, not % or .format().

  • Good Example: f"{name}: {amount}" not "%s: %s" % (name, amount).

Back to Rule Catalog

PY413-STRING-CONCATENATION

Section titled “PY413-STRING-CONCATENATION”

Use f-strings or str.join(), not + in loops.

  • Good Example: "".join(parts) not s = ""; for p in parts: s += p.

  • Related: PY605-STRING-PERFORMANCE

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Exception Types and BoundariesSpecific exceptions, hierarchy, context../py50x-exception-types.xml
Control Flow and MessagesLogging, assertions, validation, messages../py51x-control-flow-and-messages.xml

View raw XML source

Exception Types and Boundaries

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY500-SPECIFIC-EXCEPTIONSCatch specific exceptions, not bare except:.
PY501-EXCEPTION-HIERARCHYCreate exception hierarchy for your domain.
PY502-RAISE-FROMUse raise ... from ... to preserve exception chain.
PY503-CONTEXT-MANAGERSUse context managers (with) for resource management.
PY504-SUPPRESS-EXCEPTIONSUse contextlib.suppress() for expected exceptions to ignore.
PY505-FINALLY-CLEANUPUse finally for cleanup that must happen regardless of exceptions.
PY506-ELSE-CLAUSEUse else in try/except for code that runs only if no exception.
PY507-CUSTOM-EXCEPTIONSDefine custom exceptions for your domain errors.
PY508-EXCEPTION-ARGSPass useful information to exceptions.
PY509-DONT-CATCH-ALLDon’t catch BaseException or Exception at top level unless re-raising.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY500-SPECIFIC-EXCEPTIONS

Section titled “PY500-SPECIFIC-EXCEPTIONS”

Catch specific exceptions, not bare except:.

  • Tool Support: ruff E722 warns on bare except.
  • Good Example: except ValueError: not except:.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY501-EXCEPTION-HIERARCHY

Section titled “PY501-EXCEPTION-HIERARCHY”

Create exception hierarchy for your domain.

  • Good Example: class ParsingError(ValueError): ... then class CsvParsingError(ParsingError): ....

Back to Rule Catalog

PY502-RAISE-FROM

Section titled “PY502-RAISE-FROM”

Use raise ... from ... to preserve exception chain.

  • Good Example: raise ValidationError(msg) from exc preserves original exception.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY503-CONTEXT-MANAGERS

Section titled “PY503-CONTEXT-MANAGERS”

Use context managers (with) for resource management.

  • Tool Support: contextlib module for custom context managers.
  • Good Example: with open(file) as f: ensures file closes.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY504-SUPPRESS-EXCEPTIONS

Section titled “PY504-SUPPRESS-EXCEPTIONS”

Use contextlib.suppress() for expected exceptions to ignore.

  • Good Example: with suppress(FileNotFoundError): os.remove(path).

Back to Rule Catalog

PY505-FINALLY-CLEANUP

Section titled “PY505-FINALLY-CLEANUP”

Use finally for cleanup that must happen regardless of exceptions.

  • Good Example: try: ... finally: cleanup().

Back to Rule Catalog

PY506-ELSE-CLAUSE

Section titled “PY506-ELSE-CLAUSE”

Use else in try/except for code that runs only if no exception.

  • Good Example: try: data = parse() except: handle() else: process(data).

Back to Rule Catalog

PY507-CUSTOM-EXCEPTIONS

Section titled “PY507-CUSTOM-EXCEPTIONS”

Define custom exceptions for your domain errors.

  • Good Example: class InsufficientFundsError(Exception): ....

  • Related: PY501-EXCEPTION-HIERARCHY

Back to Rule Catalog

PY508-EXCEPTION-ARGS

Section titled “PY508-EXCEPTION-ARGS”

Pass useful information to exceptions.

  • Good Example: raise ValueError(f"Amount must be positive, got {amount}").

Back to Rule Catalog

PY509-DONT-CATCH-ALL

Section titled “PY509-DONT-CATCH-ALL”

Don’t catch BaseException or Exception at top level unless re-raising.

  • Good Example: Only catch specific expected exceptions.

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Control Flow and Messages

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY510-LOGGING-EXCEPTIONSLog exceptions with context before re-raising or handling.
PY511-ASSERT-FOR-BUGSUse assertions for impossible conditions (bugs), exceptions for expected errors.
PY512-VALIDATION-EXCEPTIONSValidate inputs and raise exceptions early.
PY513-ERROR-MESSAGESError messages should be clear, actionable, include context.
PY514-EXITSTACKUse ExitStack for dynamic number of context managers.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY510-LOGGING-EXCEPTIONS

Section titled “PY510-LOGGING-EXCEPTIONS”

Log exceptions with context before re-raising or handling.

  • Good Example: logger.exception("Failed to parse transaction %s", txn_id).

Back to Rule Catalog

PY511-ASSERT-FOR-BUGS

Section titled “PY511-ASSERT-FOR-BUGS”

Use assertions for impossible conditions (bugs), exceptions for expected errors.

  • Exceptions: S101 ignored in tests.
  • Good Example: assert parser is not None, "Parser must be set" for internal invariants.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY512-VALIDATION-EXCEPTIONS

Section titled “PY512-VALIDATION-EXCEPTIONS”

Validate inputs and raise exceptions early.

  • Good Example: if amount &lt; 0: raise ValueError("Amount must be non-negative").

Back to Rule Catalog

PY513-ERROR-MESSAGES

Section titled “PY513-ERROR-MESSAGES”

Error messages should be clear, actionable, include context.

  • Good Example: f"Failed to parse date '{date_str}' with format '{fmt}'" not "Parse error".

Back to Rule Catalog

PY514-EXITSTACK

Section titled “PY514-EXITSTACK”

Use ExitStack for dynamic number of context managers.

  • Good Example: with ExitStack() as stack: files = [stack.enter_context(open(f)) for f in paths].

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Profiling and StructureMeasure first, generators, caching, layout../py60x-profiling-and-structure/index.xml
Micro-optimizationsBuilt-ins, regex, sets, lazy imports../py61x-micro-optimizations.xml

index

Rules

Section titled “Rules”

Related context files:

  • py600-premature-optimization.xml
  • py601-profile-first.xml
  • py602-generator-vs-list.xml
  • py603-dict-lookups.xml
  • py604-caching.xml
  • py605-string-performance.xml
  • py606-slots.xml
  • py607-local-lookups.xml
  • py608-early-return.xml
  • py609-avoid-dots.xml

View raw XML source

py600 premature optimization

PY600-PREMATURE-OPTIMIZATION

Section titled “PY600-PREMATURE-OPTIMIZATION”

Don’t optimize until you measure. Clarity first.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Write clear code, profile to find bottlenecks, then optimize.

View raw XML source

py601 profile first

PY601-PROFILE-FIRST

Section titled “PY601-PROFILE-FIRST”

Use profiler (cProfile, line_profiler) to find actual bottlenecks.

Tool Support

Section titled “Tool Support”

python -m cProfile script.py.

View raw XML source

py602 generator vs list

PY602-GENERATOR-VS-LIST

Section titled “PY602-GENERATOR-VS-LIST”

Favor generators over lists by default. Use generator expressions for large datasets to save memory and enable lazy evaluation. Only materialize to lists when you need random access, length, or multiple iterations.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Good Example 2

Section titled “Good Example 2”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Bad Example 2

Section titled “Bad Example 2”

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY305-GENERATORS
  • PY306-LAZY-EVALUATION
  • Y224-ITERATOR-PATTERN

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Default Choice: Generator (lazy evaluation) - Use (x for x in items) generator expression - Return Iterator[T] from functions - Let caller decide when/if to materialize with list()
  • Memory Impact: - List: O(n) memory for n items (all stored) - Generator: O(1) memory (one item at a time) - For 1M transactions: ~800MB (list) vs ~80 bytes (generator)
  • Migration Pattern: When converting tuple/list returns to generators: 1. Change return type: tuple[T, ...] → Iterator[T] 2. Replace list building with yield 3. Update callers to use list() only where needed 4. Update tests to materialize before assertions
  • Performance Impact: - Generator: Immediate start, incremental results - List: Upfront delay to build entire list - Generator wins when: processing streams, early termination, partial consumption
  • When List is Better: - Need to iterate multiple times over same data - Need random access (result[3]) - Need length without consuming (len(result)) - Small datasets where memory isn’t a concern - Returning from public API where lazy evaluation complicates usage

View raw XML source

py603 dict lookups

PY603-DICT-LOOKUPS

Section titled “PY603-DICT-LOOKUPS”

Use dict lookups instead of long if/elif chains.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

PARSERS = {"csv": CsvParser, ...}; Parser = PARSERS[type].

View raw XML source

py604 caching

PY604-CACHING

Section titled “PY604-CACHING”

Cache expensive computations with @lru_cache or @cache.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

@lru_cache(maxsize=128); def expensive_func(): ....

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY309-FUNCTOOLS

View raw XML source

py605 string performance

PY605-STRING-PERFORMANCE

Section titled “PY605-STRING-PERFORMANCE”

Use str.join() for concatenating many strings.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

"".join(parts) faster than repeated + in loop.

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY413-STRING-CONCATENATION

View raw XML source

py606 slots

PY606-SLOTS

Section titled “PY606-SLOTS”

Use __slots__ for memory optimization in classes with many instances.

Exceptions

Section titled “Exceptions”

Only when profiling shows memory issues.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

class Point: __slots__ = ('x', 'y').

View raw XML source

py607 local lookups

PY607-LOCAL-LOOKUPS

Section titled “PY607-LOCAL-LOOKUPS”

Local variables faster than global/attribute lookups.

Exceptions

Section titled “Exceptions”

Only optimize hot loops after profiling.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

local_func = obj.method; for x in items: local_func(x).

View raw XML source

py608 early return

PY608-EARLY-RETURN

Section titled “PY608-EARLY-RETURN”

Return early from functions to reduce nesting.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

if error: return None; # rest of logic not if not error: # all logic.

View raw XML source

py609 avoid dots

PY609-AVOID-DOTS

Section titled “PY609-AVOID-DOTS”

In hot loops, minimize attribute access.

Exceptions

Section titled “Exceptions”

Only in performance-critical code.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Cache obj.attr in local variable before loop.

View raw XML source

Micro-optimizations

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PY610-BUILT-INSUse built-in functions (C-optimized) when possible.
PY611-NUMPY-PANDASFor numerical data, use numpy/pandas (vectorized operations).
PY612-COMPILE-REGEXCompile regexes used repeatedly.
PY613-SET-MEMBERSHIPUse sets for membership testing (in), not lists.
PY614-LAZY-IMPORTSImport expensive modules only when needed.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PY610-BUILT-INS

Section titled “PY610-BUILT-INS”

Use built-in functions (C-optimized) when possible.

  • Good Example: sum(), min(), max() faster than manual loops.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY611-NUMPY-PANDAS

Section titled “PY611-NUMPY-PANDAS”

For numerical data, use numpy/pandas (vectorized operations).

  • Good Example: df['new_col'] = df['col'] * 2 faster than row-by-row.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY612-COMPILE-REGEX

Section titled “PY612-COMPILE-REGEX”

Compile regexes used repeatedly.

  • Good Example: PATTERN = re.compile(r"..."); PATTERN.match(text) not re.match(r"...", text) in loop.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY613-SET-MEMBERSHIP

Section titled “PY613-SET-MEMBERSHIP”

Use sets for membership testing (in), not lists.

  • Good Example: ALLOWED = {"a", "b", "c"}; if x in ALLOWED faster than list.

Back to Rule Catalog

PY614-LAZY-IMPORTS

Section titled “PY614-LAZY-IMPORTS”

Import expensive modules only when needed.

  • Good Example: def plot(): import matplotlib; ... if matplotlib not always used.

  • Related: PY213-LAZY-IMPORTS

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Missing or Weak TypesAny overuse, missing hints, weak contracts../pz10x-missing-or-weak-types.xml
Type ViolationsVariance, runtime misuse, dataclass misuse../pz11x-type-violations/index.xml

View raw XML source

Missing or Weak Types

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PZ100-OVERUSE-ANYUsing Any everywhere instead of proper types.
PZ101-MISSING-RETURN-TYPEFunctions without return type annotations.
PZ102-IGNORING-TYPE-ERRORSUsing # type: ignore comments instead of fixing root causes.
PZ103-TYPE-CASTING-WITHOUT-VALIDATIONcast(Type, value) without runtime check.
PZ104-IMPLICIT-OPTIONALdef func(x: int = None) without &#124; None.
PZ105-MIXED-TYPES-COLLECTIONSList[Union[int, str, dict]] grab bag.
PZ106-UNTYPED-DICTSDict[str, Any] for structured data.
PZ107-MISSING-PARAMETER-TYPESFunction parameters without type hints.
PZ108-RETURN-MULTIPLE-TYPESReturning different types without union.
PZ109-MUTABLE-DEFAULTSdef func(items=[]) or def func(config={}).

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PZ100-OVERUSE-ANY

Section titled “PZ100-OVERUSE-ANY”

Using Any everywhere instead of proper types.

  • Better: PY103-USE-GENERICS PY109-AVOID-ANY
  • Why This Is Problematic: Defeats type checking, loses safety. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ101-MISSING-RETURN-TYPE

Section titled “PZ101-MISSING-RETURN-TYPE”

Functions without return type annotations.

  • Better: PY115-RETURN-TYPE-HINTS
  • Why This Is Problematic: Unclear contract, no type checking. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ102-IGNORING-TYPE-ERRORS

Section titled “PZ102-IGNORING-TYPE-ERRORS”

Using # type: ignore comments instead of fixing root causes.

  • Better: Fix the underlying type issue rather than suppressing the error. Define contracts in code, not documentation.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Hides real problems, prevents type checking benefits, causes maintenance issues.

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Common Causes: - Documenting contracts in docstrings instead of defining them in code (use PY127-DEFINE-CONTRACTS-IN-CODE) - Missing base class attributes (define them explicitly) - Imprecise types (use PY101-PRECISE-TYPES)

Back to Rule Catalog

PZ103-TYPE-CASTING-WITHOUT-VALIDATION

Section titled “PZ103-TYPE-CASTING-WITHOUT-VALIDATION”

cast(Type, value) without runtime check.

  • Better: Use PY111-TYPE-NARROWING with isinstance.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Unsafe, can crash at runtime. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ104-IMPLICIT-OPTIONAL

Section titled “PZ104-IMPLICIT-OPTIONAL”

def func(x: int = None) without | None.

  • Better: def func(x: int | None = None)
  • Why This Is Problematic: Type checker error, unclear intent. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ105-MIXED-TYPES-COLLECTIONS

Section titled “PZ105-MIXED-TYPES-COLLECTIONS”

List[Union[int, str, dict]] grab bag.

  • Better: Define proper types or use PY103-USE-GENERICS.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Hard to use safely, unclear purpose. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ106-UNTYPED-DICTS

Section titled “PZ106-UNTYPED-DICTS”

Dict[str, Any] for structured data.

  • Better: PY112-DATACLASS-TYPES, TypedDict
  • Why This Is Problematic: No validation, no IDE support. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ107-MISSING-PARAMETER-TYPES

Section titled “PZ107-MISSING-PARAMETER-TYPES”

Function parameters without type hints.

  • Better: PY100-USE-TYPE-HINTS
  • Why This Is Problematic: Unclear expectations, no checking. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ108-RETURN-MULTIPLE-TYPES

Section titled “PZ108-RETURN-MULTIPLE-TYPES”

Returning different types without union.

  • Better: PY110-UNION-TYPES or raise exception.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Unclear contract.
  • Bad Example: return "error" sometimes, return 0 other times.

Back to Rule Catalog

PZ109-MUTABLE-DEFAULTS

Section titled “PZ109-MUTABLE-DEFAULTS”

def func(items=[]) or def func(config={}).

  • Better: PY313-AVOID-MUTABLE-DEFAULTS
  • Why This Is Problematic: Shared mutable default across calls, classic Python gotcha. Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Rules

Section titled “Rules”

Related context files:

  • pz110-missing-protocol-methods.xml
  • pz111-wrong-variance.xml
  • pz112-isinstance-on-typing.xml
  • pz113-mixed-sync-async.xml
  • pz114-union-none-last.xml
  • pz115-dataclass-with-behavior.xml

View raw XML source

pz110 missing protocol methods

PZ110-MISSING-PROTOCOL-METHODS

Section titled “PZ110-MISSING-PROTOCOL-METHODS”

Class claims to implement protocol but missing methods.

Better

Section titled “Better”

Use PY102-USE-PROTOCOLS correctly, test compliance.

Why This Is Problematic

Section titled “Why This Is Problematic”

Runtime AttributeError.

View raw XML source

pz111 wrong variance

PZ111-WRONG-VARIANCE

Section titled “PZ111-WRONG-VARIANCE”

Covariant/contravariant used incorrectly.

Better

Section titled “Better”

Study variance rules, use TypeVar correctly.

Why This Is Problematic

Section titled “Why This Is Problematic”

Type safety violated.

View raw XML source

pz112 isinstance on typing

PZ112-ISINSTANCE-ON-TYPING

Section titled “PZ112-ISINSTANCE-ON-TYPING”

isinstance(x, List[int]) doesn’t work.

Better

Section titled “Better”

isinstance(x, list) and validate elements manually.

Why This Is Problematic

Section titled “Why This Is Problematic”

Runtime type info lost for generics.

View raw XML source

pz113 mixed sync async

PZ113-MIXED-SYNC-ASYNC

Section titled “PZ113-MIXED-SYNC-ASYNC”

Confusing sync/async in type hints.

Better

Section titled “Better”

Consistent async types, use Awaitable.

Why This Is Problematic

Section titled “Why This Is Problematic”

Runtime errors, unclear behavior.

View raw XML source

pz114 union none last

PZ114-UNION-NONE-LAST

Section titled “PZ114-UNION-NONE-LAST”

None | int instead of int | None.

Better

Section titled “Better”

int | None for consistency.

Why This Is Problematic

Section titled “Why This Is Problematic”

Convention is None last.

View raw XML source

pz115 dataclass with behavior

PZ115-DATACLASS-WITH-BEHAVIOR

Section titled “PZ115-DATACLASS-WITH-BEHAVIOR”

Using @dataclass decorator for classes with complex behavior, business logic, or methods beyond simple accessors.

Better

Section titled “Better”

Use regular classes with explicit __init__ for classes with behavior. Use __slots__ for memory efficiency when appropriate. Reserve dataclasses for pure data containers (PY112-DATACLASS-TYPES).

Why This Is Problematic

Section titled “Why This Is Problematic”

Dataclasses are designed for “dumb” data containers (DTOs, value objects) that primarily hold data. Using them for classes with behavior:

  • Confuses the purpose of the class (is it data or behavior?)
  • Encourages mixing data representation with business logic
  • Makes it harder to test (behavior mixed with data initialization)
  • Violates Y100-SINGLE-RESPONSIBILITY (data structure + behavior = two responsibilities)

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Bad Example 2

Section titled “Bad Example 2”

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Good Example 2

Section titled “Good Example 2”

Good Example 3

Section titled “Good Example 3”

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Example - Class with factory method: python # Bad - Factory method makes this behavior, not just data @dataclass(frozen=True) class QueryResult: _data: pd.DataFrame @classmethod def from_query(cls, sql: str, conn: Connection) -&gt; QueryResult: # Factory method = behavior! ... # Good - Regular class when factory methods are needed class QueryResult: """Result of a database query.""" __slots__ = ("_data",) def __init__(self, data: pd.DataFrame) -&gt; None: self._data = data @classmethod def from_query(cls, sql: str, conn: Connection) -&gt; QueryResult: data = pd.read_sql(sql, conn) return cls(data)
  • Example - Collection with initialization logic: python # Bad - Collection that sorts/transforms input as dataclass @dataclass(frozen=True) class EventSchedule: events: tuple[Event, ...] _by_date: dict[date, list[Event]] = field(init=False) # Awkward! def __post_init__(self) -&gt; None: # Complex initialization doesn't belong in __post_init__ self._by_date = self._build_index() # Good - Regular class for collections with initialization logic class EventSchedule: """Collection of events stored in sorted order.""" __slots__ = ("_events",) def __init__(self, events: Iterable[Event]) -&gt; None: self._events: tuple[Event, ...] = tuple(sorted(events)) @property def events(self) -&gt; Sequence[Event]: return self._events
  • Rule of thumb: If the class has a @classmethod factory method, non-trivial __init__ logic, or methods beyond simple property access, it’s not a “dumb” data container and shouldn’t be a dataclass.
  • When NOT to use dataclasses: - Strategy implementations (Y220-STRATEGY-PATTERN) - Service classes with business logic - Controllers/Managers with orchestration logic - Parsers/Processors with complex algorithms - Collection/container classes with factory methods or complex initialization - Any class where behavior > data
  • When to use dataclasses: - DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) between layers - Value objects (immutable data with no behavior) - Configuration containers (just data fields) - Action parameters (Command pattern parameters) - Test fixtures (simple data for tests)

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Shared State IssuesGlobals, shared mutable state, caching../pz20x-shared-state.xml
Mutation HazardsIn-place ops, loop mutation, frozen violations../pz21x-mutation-hazards.xml

View raw XML source

Shared State Issues

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PZ200-MUTATING-ARGUMENTSFunction modifies its arguments.
PZ201-GLOBAL-STATEFunctions read/write global variables.
PZ202-MUTABLE-CLASS-ATTRIBUTESMutable class variable shared across instances.
PZ203-SHARED-MUTABLE-REFSMultiple references to same mutable object.
PZ204-MUTATING-DURING-ITERATIONModifying collection while iterating.
PZ205-PROPERTY-WITH-SIDE-EFFECTSProperty getter that modifies state or does I/O.
PZ206-MUTABLE-RETURN-INTERNALReturning mutable internal state.
PZ207-MODIFYING-CONSTANTSChanging values marked as constants.
PZ208-SINGLETON-MUTABLE-STATESingleton with mutable state.
PZ209-CACHING-MUTABLECaching mutable objects.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PZ200-MUTATING-ARGUMENTS

Section titled “PZ200-MUTATING-ARGUMENTS”

Function modifies its arguments.

  • Better: PY303-AVOID-SIDE-EFFECTS PY304-RETURN-NEW-VALUES
  • Why This Is Problematic: Unexpected side effects, hard to reason about. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ201-GLOBAL-STATE

Section titled “PZ201-GLOBAL-STATE”

Functions read/write global variables.

  • Better: PY320-AVOID-GLOBALS Y103-DEPENDENCY-INJECTION
  • Why This Is Problematic: Hard to test, order-dependent, not thread-safe. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ202-MUTABLE-CLASS-ATTRIBUTES

Section titled “PZ202-MUTABLE-CLASS-ATTRIBUTES”

Mutable class variable shared across instances.

  • Better: Initialize in __init__.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Instances unexpectedly share state.
  • Bad Example: class Foo: items = [] # shared!

Back to Rule Catalog

PZ203-SHARED-MUTABLE-REFS

Section titled “PZ203-SHARED-MUTABLE-REFS”

Multiple references to same mutable object.

  • Better: PY300-IMMUTABILITY, copy when needed.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Modifications affect all references, hard to track. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ204-MUTATING-DURING-ITERATION

Section titled “PZ204-MUTATING-DURING-ITERATION”

Modifying collection while iterating.

  • Better: Iterate over copy or collect items to remove.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Runtime error or undefined behavior.
  • Bad Example: for x in items: items.remove(x)

Back to Rule Catalog

PZ205-PROPERTY-WITH-SIDE-EFFECTS

Section titled “PZ205-PROPERTY-WITH-SIDE-EFFECTS”

Property getter that modifies state or does I/O.

  • Better: Use method, not property.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Violates expectations (properties should be cheap, pure). Back to Rule Catalog

PZ206-MUTABLE-RETURN-INTERNAL

Section titled “PZ206-MUTABLE-RETURN-INTERNAL”

Returning mutable internal state.

  • Better: Return copy or immutable view.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Caller can modify your internal state.
  • Bad Example: return self._items (list)

Back to Rule Catalog

PZ207-MODIFYING-CONSTANTS

Section titled “PZ207-MODIFYING-CONSTANTS”

Changing values marked as constants.

  • Better: PY108-FINAL enforces immutability.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Breaks expectations, hard to debug. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ208-SINGLETON-MUTABLE-STATE

Section titled “PZ208-SINGLETON-MUTABLE-STATE”

Singleton with mutable state.

  • Better: Y203-SINGLETON-PATTERN used carefully or avoid.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Global mutable state, testing nightmare. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ209-CACHING-MUTABLE

Section titled “PZ209-CACHING-MUTABLE”

Caching mutable objects.

  • Better: Cache immutable data or deep copy.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Cached object modified, stale cache. Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Mutation Hazards

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
PZ210-MUTATING-LOOP-VARIABLEModifying loop variable inside loop.
PZ211-DEFAULT-DICT-MUTATIONMutating default dict values.
PZ212-IN-PLACE-OPERATIONSlist.sort() instead of sorted(list).
PZ213-FROZEN-VIOLATIONWorking around frozen dataclass.
PZ214-MUTABLE-DATACLASSDataclass without frozen=True when should be immutable.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

PZ210-MUTATING-LOOP-VARIABLE

Section titled “PZ210-MUTATING-LOOP-VARIABLE”

Modifying loop variable inside loop.

  • Better: Use different variable.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Confusing, doesn’t affect loop.
  • Bad Example: for i in range(10): i += 1 # doesn’t work as expected

Back to Rule Catalog

PZ211-DEFAULT-DICT-MUTATION

Section titled “PZ211-DEFAULT-DICT-MUTATION”

Mutating default dict values.

  • Better: PY313-AVOID-MUTABLE-DEFAULTS
  • Why This Is Problematic: Unexpected sharing. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ212-IN-PLACE-OPERATIONS

Section titled “PZ212-IN-PLACE-OPERATIONS”

list.sort() instead of sorted(list).

  • Better: Use non-mutating versions when possible.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Mutates original, can’t chain.
  • Exceptions: In-place OK for performance when intentional. Back to Rule Catalog

PZ213-FROZEN-VIOLATION

Section titled “PZ213-FROZEN-VIOLATION”

Working around frozen dataclass.

  • Better: Create new instance with PY312-DATACLASS-REPLACE.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Defeats immutability guarantees.
  • Bad Example: object.__setattr__(frozen_obj, 'x', value)

Back to Rule Catalog

PZ214-MUTABLE-DATACLASS

Section titled “PZ214-MUTABLE-DATACLASS”

Dataclass without frozen=True when should be immutable.

  • Better: PY301-FROZEN-DATACLASSES
  • Why This Is Problematic: Accidental mutations, harder to reason about. Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Source

Section titled “Source”

Related context files:

  • pz3xx-miscellaneous.xml

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
MiscellaneousOther Python-specific issues../pz3xx-miscellaneous.xml

View raw XML source

Miscellaneous

PZ300-FILE-LEVEL-NOQA

Section titled “PZ300-FILE-LEVEL-NOQA”

Using file-level # noqa comments to suppress linter warnings across entire modules.

Better

Section titled “Better”

Fix underlying issues incrementally. Use targeted inline # noqa comments only when genuinely needed.

Why This Is Problematic

Section titled “Why This Is Problematic”

File-level suppressions hide all issues in a file, prevent incremental fixes, and create technical debt.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Bad Example 1

Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Good Example 1

Section titled “Good Example 1”

Related Rules

Section titled “Related Rules”
  • PY118-STRICT-MODE
  • PZ102-IGNORING-TYPE-ERRORS
  • Z404-COMMENTED-OUT-CODE

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • When suppressions are acceptable: - Inline only: specific line/function needs exception with justification - Temporary: during migration with TODO to remove - Third-party compat: use # type: ignore per-line

View raw XML source

index catalog

Catalog Overview

Section titled “Catalog Overview”
  • Guidelines
    • TY1xx — Type System & Type Safety
      • TY10x — Type System & Type Safety — Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.
    • TY2xx — Code Organization & Modules
      • TY20x — Code Organization & Modules — Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.
    • TY3xx — Immutability & Functional Patterns
      • TY30x — Immutability & Functional Patterns — Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.
    • TY4xx — Error Handling
      • TY40x — Error Handling — Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.
    • TY5xx — Async & Promises
      • TY50x — Async & Promises — Async/await, Promise patterns, concurrency, cancellation.
    • TY6xx — Testing & Tooling
      • TY60x — Testing & Tooling — Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.
  • Anti-Patterns
    • TZ1xx — Type Safety Violations
      • TZ10x — Type Safety Violations — Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.
    • TZ2xx — Module & Import Problems
      • TZ20x — Module & Import Problems — Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems.
    • TZ3xx — Async & Runtime Hazards
      • TZ30x — Async & Runtime Hazards — Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.

Guidelines

Section titled “Guidelines”

Normative rules to follow.

TY1xx — Type System & Type Safety

Section titled “TY1xx — Type System & Type Safety”

Strict mode, annotations, generics, utility types.

TY10x — Type System & Type Safety

Section titled “TY10x — Type System & Type Safety”

Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.

RuleSummarySource
TY100-STRICT-MODEEnable strict: true in tsconfig.json for all projects. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY101-EXPLICIT-RETURN-TYPESAnnotate return types on exported functions and public methods. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY102-INTERFACES-VS-TYPESPrefer interface for object shapes that may be extended; use type for unions, intersections, mapped types, and primitives. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY103-GENERICSUse generics for reusable components that work with multiple types. Constrain type parameters with extends when possible. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY104-UTILITY-TYPESUse built-in utility types (Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, Readonly) instead of manual type construction. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONSTUse literal types and as const assertions for values that must be specific constants. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONSUse discriminated unions with a shared literal property for type-safe variants. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY107-BRANDED-TYPESUse branded types to distinguish semantically different values that share a primitive representation. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY108-TEMPLATE-LITERAL-TYPESUse template literal types for string patterns that follow a known format. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml
TY109-AVOID-ENUMSPrefer union types or as const objects over TypeScript enums. Use enums only when numeric reverse mapping is required. Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.ty1xx/ty1xx-type-system.xml

TY2xx — Code Organization & Modules

Section titled “TY2xx — Code Organization & Modules”

Modules, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.

TY20x — Code Organization & Modules

Section titled “TY20x — Code Organization & Modules”

Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.

RuleSummarySource
TY200-ES-MODULESUse ES modules (import/export) exclusively. Avoid CommonJS require/module.exports. Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.ty2xx/ty2xx-modules.xml
TY201-EXPLICIT-EXPORTSExport only what consumers need. Use named exports over default exports. Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.ty2xx/ty2xx-modules.xml
TY202-BARREL-FILESUse barrel files (index.ts) sparingly for package boundaries. Avoid deep barrel nesting. Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.ty2xx/ty2xx-modules.xml
TY203-TYPE-ONLY-IMPORTSUse import type for imports used only in type positions. Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.ty2xx/ty2xx-modules.xml
TY204-DEPENDENCY-DIRECTIONDependencies flow inward: infrastructure depends on domain, never the reverse. Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.ty2xx/ty2xx-modules.xml
TY205-PATH-ALIASESUse path aliases in tsconfig.json for cross-cutting imports instead of deep relative paths. Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.ty2xx/ty2xx-modules.xml

TY3xx — Immutability & Functional Patterns

Section titled “TY3xx — Immutability & Functional Patterns”

Readonly, const assertions, pure functions.

TY30x — Immutability & Functional Patterns

Section titled “TY30x — Immutability & Functional Patterns”

Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.

RuleSummarySource
TY300-READONLY-BY-DEFAULTMark properties, parameters, and arrays as readonly by default. Remove readonly only when mutation is intentional. Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.ty3xx/ty3xx-immutability.xml
TY301-CONST-ASSERTIONSUse as const for literal objects and arrays that should not change. Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.ty3xx/ty3xx-immutability.xml
TY302-PURE-FUNCTIONSPrefer pure functions: same input produces same output, no side effects. Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.ty3xx/ty3xx-immutability.xml
TY303-RETURN-NEW-VALUESReturn new objects or arrays instead of mutating inputs. Use spread syntax or utility methods. Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.ty3xx/ty3xx-immutability.xml
TY304-ARRAY-METHODS-OVER-LOOPSPrefer map, filter, reduce, and other array methods over imperative loops for data transformations. Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.ty3xx/ty3xx-immutability.xml
TY305-AVOID-MUTABLE-GLOBALSAvoid module-level mutable state. Use dependency injection or factory functions instead. Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.ty3xx/ty3xx-immutability.xml

TY4xx — Error Handling

Section titled “TY4xx — Error Handling”

Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types.

TY40x — Error Handling

Section titled “TY40x — Error Handling”

Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.

RuleSummarySource
TY400-EXHAUSTIVE-CHECKSUse exhaustive checks with never to ensure all union members are handled. Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.ty4xx/ty4xx-error-handling.xml
TY401-CUSTOM-ERROR-CLASSESExtend Error for domain-specific errors. Include structured context, not just message strings. Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.ty4xx/ty4xx-error-handling.xml
TY402-TYPE-SAFE-CATCHNarrow caught errors with instanceof or type guards. Never assume catch (e) is an Error. Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.ty4xx/ty4xx-error-handling.xml
TY403-RESULT-TYPEConsider Result/Either types for operations where failure is expected and should be handled by callers. Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.ty4xx/ty4xx-error-handling.xml
TY404-TYPE-GUARDSUse type guard functions (is return type) and assertion functions (asserts return type) for complex type narrowing. Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.ty4xx/ty4xx-error-handling.xml
TY405-VALIDATE-BOUNDARIESValidate and parse external data at system boundaries using runtime validation (e.g., Zod, io-ts, Valibot). Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.ty4xx/ty4xx-error-handling.xml

TY5xx — Async & Promises

Section titled “TY5xx — Async & Promises”

Async/await, Promises, concurrency patterns.

TY50x — Async & Promises

Section titled “TY50x — Async & Promises”

Async/await, Promise patterns, concurrency, cancellation.

RuleSummarySource
TY500-ASYNC-AWAITUse async/await over raw Promise chains (.then/.catch). Async/await, Promise patterns, concurrency, cancellation.ty5xx/ty5xx-async.xml
TY501-PROMISE-CONCURRENCYUse Promise.all for independent concurrent operations; use Promise.allSettled when partial failure is acceptable. Async/await, Promise patterns, concurrency, cancellation.ty5xx/ty5xx-async.xml
TY502-ABORT-SIGNALSAccept AbortSignal for cancellable async operations. Propagate signals through the call chain. Async/await, Promise patterns, concurrency, cancellation.ty5xx/ty5xx-async.xml
TY503-TYPED-EVENT-EMITTERSUse typed event emitters or observable patterns instead of untyped string-based events. Async/await, Promise patterns, concurrency, cancellation.ty5xx/ty5xx-async.xml
TY504-ASYNC-ITERATORSUse for await...of with async iterables for streaming data. Prefer async generators over manual iterator implementation. Async/await, Promise patterns, concurrency, cancellation.ty5xx/ty5xx-async.xml

TY6xx — Testing & Tooling

Section titled “TY6xx — Testing & Tooling”

Type testing, mocking, assertion patterns.

TY60x — Testing & Tooling

Section titled “TY60x — Testing & Tooling”

Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.

RuleSummarySource
TY600-TYPE-LEVEL-TESTSWrite type-level tests for complex generics and utility types using Expect/Equal helpers or tsd. Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.ty6xx/ty6xx-testing.xml
TY601-TYPED-MOCKSUse typed mocking utilities that preserve interface contracts. Avoid as any in test doubles. Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.ty6xx/ty6xx-testing.xml
TY602-AAA-STRUCTUREStructure tests with Arrange/Act/Assert (or Given/When/Then). One logical assertion group per test. Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.ty6xx/ty6xx-testing.xml
TY603-DESCRIPTIVE-TEST-NAMESUse descriptive test names that state the behavior and context, not the implementation. Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.ty6xx/ty6xx-testing.xml
TY604-MOCK-BOUNDARIESMock external systems (HTTP, DB, clock, filesystem). Do not mock value objects, utility functions, or the module under test. Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.ty6xx/ty6xx-testing.xml
TY605-PARAMETERIZED-TESTSUse it.each or describe.each for behavior with multiple inputs. Avoid copy-pasted test bodies. Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.ty6xx/ty6xx-testing.xml

Anti-Patterns

Section titled “Anti-Patterns”

Patterns to avoid.

TZ1xx — Type Safety Violations

Section titled “TZ1xx — Type Safety Violations”

Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak types.

TZ10x — Type Safety Violations

Section titled “TZ10x — Type Safety Violations”

Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.

RuleSummarySource
TZ100-OVERUSE-ANYUsing any to bypass type checking instead of proper types. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ101-TS-IGNOREUsing @ts-ignore or @ts-expect-error to suppress type errors instead of fixing them. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ102-NON-NULL-ASSERTIONUsing the non-null assertion operator (!) instead of proper null checks. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ103-UNSAFE-TYPE-ASSERTIONUsing as Type to force a type without runtime validation. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ104-OBJECT-AS-MAPUsing plain objects (Record&lt;string, T&gt;) as maps when Map&lt;K, V&gt; is more appropriate. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ105-IMPLICIT-ANY-RETURNFunctions with inferred any return type due to missing annotation or unsafe operations. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ106-STRING-TYPED-PARAMETERSUsing bare string for parameters that accept only specific values. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ107-INTERFACE-FOR-UNIONUsing a single interface with optional fields to model variants instead of a discriminated union. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ108-NAMESPACE-ABUSEUsing TypeScript namespace for code organization instead of ES modules. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml
TZ109-FUNCTION-OVERLOADS-ABUSEExcessive function overloads when a union parameter or generic would be clearer. Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.tz1xx/tz1xx-type-violations.xml

TZ2xx — Module & Import Problems

Section titled “TZ2xx — Module & Import Problems”

Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export issues.

TZ20x — Module & Import Problems

Section titled “TZ20x — Module & Import Problems”

Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems.

RuleSummarySource
TZ200-CIRCULAR-DEPSCircular module dependencies that cause runtime initialization errors or undefined imports. Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems.tz2xx/tz2xx-module-antipatterns.xml
TZ201-BARREL-BLOATBarrel files (index.ts) that re-export everything from every module, causing bundle bloat and slow IDE performance. Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems.tz2xx/tz2xx-module-antipatterns.xml
TZ202-SIDE-EFFECT-IMPORTSModules that execute side effects on import (register globals, modify prototypes, start servers). Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems.tz2xx/tz2xx-module-antipatterns.xml
TZ203-WILDCARD-REEXPORTSUsing export * from which leaks implementation details and creates name collisions. Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems.tz2xx/tz2xx-module-antipatterns.xml
TZ204-DEFAULT-EXPORT-MISMATCHDefault exports that lose their name binding, causing inconsistent names across imports. Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems.tz2xx/tz2xx-module-antipatterns.xml

TZ3xx — Async & Runtime Hazards

Section titled “TZ3xx — Async & Runtime Hazards”

Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.

TZ30x — Async & Runtime Hazards

Section titled “TZ30x — Async & Runtime Hazards”

Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.

RuleSummarySource
TZ300-UNHANDLED-PROMISESCalling an async function without await or an attached rejection handler, leaving the Promise to reject unobserved. Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.tz3xx/tz3xx-async-hazards.xml
TZ301-CALLBACK-PROMISE-MIXMixing callback-style and Promise-style APIs in the same flow. Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.tz3xx/tz3xx-async-hazards.xml
TZ302-ASYNC-VOID-EVENTSPassing async functions to event handlers or callbacks that expect synchronous void return. Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.tz3xx/tz3xx-async-hazards.xml
TZ303-SHARED-MUTABLE-STATESharing mutable state between async operations without coordination. Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.tz3xx/tz3xx-async-hazards.xml
TZ304-PROMISE-CONSTRUCTOR-ANTIPATTERNWrapping an existing Promise in new Promise() unnecessarily. Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.tz3xx/tz3xx-async-hazards.xml
TZ305-SEQUENTIAL-INDEPENDENT-AWAITSSequentially awaiting independent Promises instead of running them concurrently. Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.tz3xx/tz3xx-async-hazards.xml

View raw XML source
