Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Type System & Type SafetyStrict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types../ty1xx-type-system.xml

View raw XML source

Type System & Type Safety

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TY100-STRICT-MODEEnable strict: true in tsconfig.json for all projects.
TY101-EXPLICIT-RETURN-TYPESAnnotate return types on exported functions and public methods.
TY102-INTERFACES-VS-TYPESPrefer interface for object shapes that may be extended; use type for unions, intersections, mapped types, and primitives.
TY103-GENERICSUse generics for reusable components that work with multiple types.
TY104-UTILITY-TYPESUse built-in utility types (Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, Readonly) instead of manual type construction.
TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONSTUse literal types and as const assertions for values that must be specific constants.
TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONSUse discriminated unions with a shared literal property for type-safe variants.
TY107-BRANDED-TYPESUse branded types to distinguish semantically different values that share a primitive representation.
TY108-TEMPLATE-LITERAL-TYPESUse template literal types for string patterns that follow a known format.
TY109-AVOID-ENUMSPrefer union types or as const objects over TypeScript enums.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TY100-STRICT-MODE

Section titled “TY100-STRICT-MODE”

Enable strict: true in tsconfig.json for all projects.

  • Rationale: Strict mode enables strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, and other flags that catch entire classes of bugs at compile time.
  • Tool Support: tsc —strict. ESLint @typescript-eslint/strict-type-checked preset.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TZ100-OVERUSE-ANY, TZ101-TS-IGNORE

Back to Rule Catalog

TY101-EXPLICIT-RETURN-TYPES

Section titled “TY101-EXPLICIT-RETURN-TYPES”

Annotate return types on exported functions and public methods.

  • Rationale: Explicit return types prevent accidental API changes, improve documentation, and speed up type checking in large codebases.
  • Tool Support: ESLint @typescript-eslint/explicit-function-return-type, @typescript-eslint/explicit-module-boundary-types.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TZ100-OVERUSE-ANY

Back to Rule Catalog

TY102-INTERFACES-VS-TYPES

Section titled “TY102-INTERFACES-VS-TYPES”

Prefer interface for object shapes that may be extended; use type for unions, intersections, mapped types, and primitives.

  • Rationale: Interfaces support declaration merging and are more performant for object type checking. Types are required for unions and complex type algebra.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY103-GENERICS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY103-GENERICS

Section titled “TY103-GENERICS”

Use generics for reusable components that work with multiple types. Constrain type parameters with extends when possible.

  • Rationale: Generics provide type safety without sacrificing reusability. Constraints communicate intent and catch misuse.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY104-UTILITY-TYPES, TZ100-OVERUSE-ANY

Back to Rule Catalog

TY104-UTILITY-TYPES

Section titled “TY104-UTILITY-TYPES”

Use built-in utility types (Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, Readonly) instead of manual type construction.

  • Rationale: Utility types are well-tested, readable, and keep types DRY. They automatically track changes to the source type.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY102-INTERFACES-VS-TYPES

Back to Rule Catalog

TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONST

Section titled “TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONST”

Use literal types and as const assertions for values that must be specific constants.

  • Rationale: Literal types and const assertions narrow types at compile time, enabling exhaustive checking and preventing invalid values.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONS, TY300-READONLY-BY-DEFAULT

Back to Rule Catalog

TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONS

Section titled “TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONS”

Use discriminated unions with a shared literal property for type-safe variants.

  • Rationale: Discriminated unions enable exhaustive pattern matching, clear control flow, and compiler-enforced completeness.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONST, TY400-EXHAUSTIVE-CHECKS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY107-BRANDED-TYPES

Section titled “TY107-BRANDED-TYPES”

Use branded types to distinguish semantically different values that share a primitive representation.

  • Rationale: Branded types prevent mixing up IDs, currencies, units, and other domain values that are all strings or numbers at runtime.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY103-GENERICS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY108-TEMPLATE-LITERAL-TYPES

Section titled “TY108-TEMPLATE-LITERAL-TYPES”

Use template literal types for string patterns that follow a known format.

  • Rationale: Template literal types encode string structure in the type system, catching malformed strings at compile time.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONST

Back to Rule Catalog

TY109-AVOID-ENUMS

Section titled “TY109-AVOID-ENUMS”

Prefer union types or as const objects over TypeScript enums. Use enums only when numeric reverse mapping is required.

  • Rationale: Enums generate runtime code, have surprising behaviors (numeric enums are not type-safe, const enums are fragile across module boundaries), and union types are simpler and more predictable.
  • Tool Support: ESLint core no-restricted-syntax rule with TSEnumDeclaration selector can ban enum declarations.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONST, TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONS

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Code Organization & ModulesModule structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction../ty2xx-modules.xml

View raw XML source

Code Organization & Modules

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TY200-ES-MODULESUse ES modules (import/export) exclusively.
TY201-EXPLICIT-EXPORTSExport only what consumers need.
TY202-BARREL-FILESUse barrel files (index.ts) sparingly for package boundaries.
TY203-TYPE-ONLY-IMPORTSUse import type for imports used only in type positions.
TY204-DEPENDENCY-DIRECTIONDependencies flow inward: infrastructure depends on domain, never the reverse.
TY205-PATH-ALIASESUse path aliases in tsconfig.json for cross-cutting imports instead of deep relative paths.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TY200-ES-MODULES

Section titled “TY200-ES-MODULES”

Use ES modules (import/export) exclusively. Avoid CommonJS require/module.exports.

  • Rationale: ES modules are the standard, enable tree-shaking, provide static analysis, and are supported everywhere TypeScript targets.
  • Tool Support: ESLint @typescript-eslint/no-require-imports.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Back to Rule Catalog

TY201-EXPLICIT-EXPORTS

Section titled “TY201-EXPLICIT-EXPORTS”

Export only what consumers need. Use named exports over default exports.

  • Rationale: Named exports enable better IDE auto-imports, refactoring support, and tree-shaking. Default exports lose the name binding and complicate re-exports.
  • Tool Support: ESLint import/no-default-export.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY202-BARREL-FILES, TZ200-CIRCULAR-DEPS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY202-BARREL-FILES

Section titled “TY202-BARREL-FILES”

Use barrel files (index.ts) sparingly for package boundaries. Avoid deep barrel nesting.

  • Rationale: Barrel files define clean public APIs at package boundaries but cause bundle bloat and circular dependency risk when overused internally.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TZ200-CIRCULAR-DEPS, TZ201-BARREL-BLOAT

Back to Rule Catalog

TY203-TYPE-ONLY-IMPORTS

Section titled “TY203-TYPE-ONLY-IMPORTS”

Use import type for imports used only in type positions.

  • Rationale: Type-only imports are erased at compile time, reducing bundle size and preventing circular dependency issues at runtime.
  • Tool Support: TSConfig verbatimModuleSyntax: true. ESLint @typescript-eslint/consistent-type-imports.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TY204-DEPENDENCY-DIRECTION

Section titled “TY204-DEPENDENCY-DIRECTION”

Dependencies flow inward: infrastructure depends on domain, never the reverse.

  • Rationale: Inward-pointing dependencies keep domain logic portable and testable, independent of frameworks and I/O.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: Y100-SINGLE-RESPONSIBILITY, Y104-DEPENDENCY-INVERSION

Back to Rule Catalog

TY205-PATH-ALIASES

Section titled “TY205-PATH-ALIASES”

Use path aliases in tsconfig.json for cross-cutting imports instead of deep relative paths.

  • Rationale: Path aliases improve readability and make refactoring (moving files) safer. Deep relative paths (../../../) are fragile and hard to read.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Immutability & Functional PatternsReadonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns../ty3xx-immutability.xml

View raw XML source

Immutability & Functional Patterns

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TY300-READONLY-BY-DEFAULTMark properties, parameters, and arrays as readonly by default.
TY301-CONST-ASSERTIONSUse as const for literal objects and arrays that should not change.
TY302-PURE-FUNCTIONSPrefer pure functions: same input produces same output, no side effects.
TY303-RETURN-NEW-VALUESReturn new objects or arrays instead of mutating inputs.
TY304-ARRAY-METHODS-OVER-LOOPSPrefer map, filter, reduce, and other array methods over imperative loops for data transformations.
TY305-AVOID-MUTABLE-GLOBALSAvoid module-level mutable state.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TY300-READONLY-BY-DEFAULT

Section titled “TY300-READONLY-BY-DEFAULT”

Mark properties, parameters, and arrays as readonly by default. Remove readonly only when mutation is intentional.

  • Rationale: Readonly annotations catch accidental mutation at compile time and communicate intent clearly.
  • Tool Support: ESLint @typescript-eslint/prefer-readonly, @typescript-eslint/prefer-readonly-parameter-types.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONST, TY301-CONST-ASSERTIONS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY301-CONST-ASSERTIONS

Section titled “TY301-CONST-ASSERTIONS”

Use as const for literal objects and arrays that should not change.

  • Rationale: Const assertions make all properties readonly and narrow types to their literal values, enabling type-level computation.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONST, TY300-READONLY-BY-DEFAULT

Back to Rule Catalog

TY302-PURE-FUNCTIONS

Section titled “TY302-PURE-FUNCTIONS”

Prefer pure functions: same input produces same output, no side effects.

  • Rationale: Pure functions are easier to test, reason about, compose, and parallelize.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY303-RETURN-NEW-VALUES, Y100-SINGLE-RESPONSIBILITY

Back to Rule Catalog

TY303-RETURN-NEW-VALUES

Section titled “TY303-RETURN-NEW-VALUES”

Return new objects or arrays instead of mutating inputs. Use spread syntax or utility methods.

  • Rationale: Returning new values prevents shared-state bugs, makes data flow explicit, and works well with React, Redux, and other immutable-data architectures.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY300-READONLY-BY-DEFAULT, TZ303-SHARED-MUTABLE-STATE

Back to Rule Catalog

TY304-ARRAY-METHODS-OVER-LOOPS

Section titled “TY304-ARRAY-METHODS-OVER-LOOPS”

Prefer map, filter, reduce, and other array methods over imperative loops for data transformations.

  • Rationale: Array methods express intent clearly, chain naturally, and avoid off-by-one errors. Use for...of when performance matters in hot paths or when early termination is needed.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Back to Rule Catalog

TY305-AVOID-MUTABLE-GLOBALS

Section titled “TY305-AVOID-MUTABLE-GLOBALS”

Avoid module-level mutable state. Use dependency injection or factory functions instead.

  • Rationale: Module-level mutable state creates hidden coupling, makes testing difficult, and causes surprising behavior in concurrent environments.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TZ303-SHARED-MUTABLE-STATE, Y104-DEPENDENCY-INVERSION

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Error HandlingCustom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards../ty4xx-error-handling.xml

View raw XML source

Error Handling

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TY400-EXHAUSTIVE-CHECKSUse exhaustive checks with never to ensure all union members are handled.
TY401-CUSTOM-ERROR-CLASSESExtend Error for domain-specific errors.
TY402-TYPE-SAFE-CATCHNarrow caught errors with instanceof or type guards.
TY403-RESULT-TYPEConsider Result/Either types for operations where failure is expected and should be handled by callers.
TY404-TYPE-GUARDSUse type guard functions (is return type) and assertion functions (asserts return type) for complex type narrowing.
TY405-VALIDATE-BOUNDARIESValidate and parse external data at system boundaries using runtime validation (e.g., Zod, io-ts, Valibot).

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TY400-EXHAUSTIVE-CHECKS

Section titled “TY400-EXHAUSTIVE-CHECKS”

Use exhaustive checks with never to ensure all union members are handled.

  • Rationale: The never type causes a compile error when a union variant is unhandled, preventing forgotten cases when the union grows.
  • Tool Support: ESLint @typescript-eslint/switch-exhaustiveness-check. Use a final never/assertNever default branch so that TypeScript’s control-flow analysis can surface unhandled union members as type errors.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY401-CUSTOM-ERROR-CLASSES

Section titled “TY401-CUSTOM-ERROR-CLASSES”

Extend Error for domain-specific errors. Include structured context, not just message strings.

  • Rationale: Custom error classes enable instanceof checks, carry typed context, and produce meaningful stack traces.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY402-TYPE-SAFE-CATCH

Back to Rule Catalog

TY402-TYPE-SAFE-CATCH

Section titled “TY402-TYPE-SAFE-CATCH”

Narrow caught errors with instanceof or type guards. Never assume catch (e) is an Error.

  • Rationale: TypeScript types catch parameter as unknown. Anything can be thrown, so narrowing is required for safe access.
  • Tool Support: TSConfig useUnknownInCatchVariables: true (included in strict).

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY401-CUSTOM-ERROR-CLASSES, TZ102-NON-NULL-ASSERTION

Back to Rule Catalog

TY403-RESULT-TYPE

Section titled “TY403-RESULT-TYPE”

Consider Result/Either types for operations where failure is expected and should be handled by callers.

  • Rationale: Result types make failure explicit in the type signature, forcing callers to handle both paths. Use exceptions for truly unexpected errors.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONS, TY400-EXHAUSTIVE-CHECKS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY404-TYPE-GUARDS

Section titled “TY404-TYPE-GUARDS”

Use type guard functions (is return type) and assertion functions (asserts return type) for complex type narrowing.

  • Rationale: Type guards encapsulate narrowing logic, making it reusable and testable. Assertion functions narrow types after a throw.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY402-TYPE-SAFE-CATCH, TZ102-NON-NULL-ASSERTION

Back to Rule Catalog

TY405-VALIDATE-BOUNDARIES

Section titled “TY405-VALIDATE-BOUNDARIES”

Validate and parse external data at system boundaries using runtime validation (e.g., Zod, io-ts, Valibot).

  • Rationale: TypeScript types are erased at runtime. Data from APIs, files, environment variables, and user input must be validated before trusting the type.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY402-TYPE-SAFE-CATCH, TZ103-UNSAFE-TYPE-ASSERTION

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Async & PromisesAsync/await, Promise patterns, concurrency, cancellation../ty5xx-async.xml

View raw XML source

Async & Promises

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TY500-ASYNC-AWAITUse async/await over raw Promise chains (.then/.catch).
TY501-PROMISE-CONCURRENCYUse Promise.all for independent concurrent operations; use Promise.allSettled when partial failure is acceptable.
TY502-ABORT-SIGNALSAccept AbortSignal for cancellable async operations.
TY503-TYPED-EVENT-EMITTERSUse typed event emitters or observable patterns instead of untyped string-based events.
TY504-ASYNC-ITERATORSUse for await...of with async iterables for streaming data.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TY500-ASYNC-AWAIT

Section titled “TY500-ASYNC-AWAIT”

Use async/await over raw Promise chains (.then/.catch).

  • Rationale: Async/await produces linear, readable code with proper stack traces and straightforward error handling via try/catch.
  • Tool Support: ESLint @typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises, @typescript-eslint/promise-function-async.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TZ300-UNHANDLED-PROMISES

Back to Rule Catalog

TY501-PROMISE-CONCURRENCY

Section titled “TY501-PROMISE-CONCURRENCY”

Use Promise.all for independent concurrent operations; use Promise.allSettled when partial failure is acceptable.

  • Rationale: Sequential awaits for independent work waste time. Promise.all fails fast; Promise.allSettled reports all results.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY500-ASYNC-AWAIT, TZ305-SEQUENTIAL-INDEPENDENT-AWAITS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY502-ABORT-SIGNALS

Section titled “TY502-ABORT-SIGNALS”

Accept AbortSignal for cancellable async operations. Propagate signals through the call chain.

  • Rationale: AbortSignal is the standard cancellation mechanism for fetch, streams, and long-running operations. Without it, abandoned work wastes resources.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TY503-TYPED-EVENT-EMITTERS

Section titled “TY503-TYPED-EVENT-EMITTERS”

Use typed event emitters or observable patterns instead of untyped string-based events.

  • Rationale: Typed events catch misspelled event names, wrong payload types, and missing handlers at compile time.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: TY103-GENERICS

Back to Rule Catalog

TY504-ASYNC-ITERATORS

Section titled “TY504-ASYNC-ITERATORS”

Use for await...of with async iterables for streaming data. Prefer async generators over manual iterator implementation.

  • Rationale: Async iterators handle backpressure naturally, compose well, and are the standard pattern for streaming data in Node.js and web streams.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Testing & ToolingType testing, mocking, assertions, test structure../ty6xx-testing.xml

View raw XML source

Testing & Tooling

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TY600-TYPE-LEVEL-TESTSWrite type-level tests for complex generics and utility types using Expect/Equal helpers or tsd.
TY601-TYPED-MOCKSUse typed mocking utilities that preserve interface contracts.
TY602-AAA-STRUCTUREStructure tests with Arrange/Act/Assert (or Given/When/Then).
TY603-DESCRIPTIVE-TEST-NAMESUse descriptive test names that state the behavior and context, not the implementation.
TY604-MOCK-BOUNDARIESMock external systems (HTTP, DB, clock, filesystem).
TY605-PARAMETERIZED-TESTSUse it.each or describe.each for behavior with multiple inputs.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TY600-TYPE-LEVEL-TESTS

Section titled “TY600-TYPE-LEVEL-TESTS”

Write type-level tests for complex generics and utility types using Expect/Equal helpers or tsd.

  • Rationale: Type-level tests verify that utility types, generics, and overloads produce the expected types, catching regressions that runtime tests cannot detect.
  • Tool Support: tsd, @type-challenges style assertions, vitest expectTypeOf.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TY601-TYPED-MOCKS

Section titled “TY601-TYPED-MOCKS”

Use typed mocking utilities that preserve interface contracts. Avoid as any in test doubles.

  • Rationale: Typed mocks catch interface changes at compile time. Untyped mocks silently diverge from the real implementation.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TZ100-OVERUSE-ANY

Back to Rule Catalog

TY602-AAA-STRUCTURE

Section titled “TY602-AAA-STRUCTURE”

Structure tests with Arrange/Act/Assert (or Given/When/Then). One logical assertion group per test.

  • Rationale: Clear structure makes tests readable, debuggable, and maintainable. A single focus per test pinpoints failures.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”
  • Related: Y401-AAA-PATTERN

Back to Rule Catalog

TY603-DESCRIPTIVE-TEST-NAMES

Section titled “TY603-DESCRIPTIVE-TEST-NAMES”

Use descriptive test names that state the behavior and context, not the implementation.

  • Rationale: Good test names serve as documentation. When a test fails, the name should explain what broke without reading the test body.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”

Back to Rule Catalog

TY604-MOCK-BOUNDARIES

Section titled “TY604-MOCK-BOUNDARIES”

Mock external systems (HTTP, DB, clock, filesystem). Do not mock value objects, utility functions, or the module under test.

  • Rationale: Over-mocking couples tests to implementation and reduces confidence. Mock at I/O boundaries to keep tests fast and deterministic.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
  • Related: TY601-TYPED-MOCKS, Y405-MOCKING

Back to Rule Catalog

TY605-PARAMETERIZED-TESTS

Section titled “TY605-PARAMETERIZED-TESTS”

Use it.each or describe.each for behavior with multiple inputs. Avoid copy-pasted test bodies.

  • Rationale: Parameterized tests reduce duplication and make it easy to add new cases. The table format documents the input/output contract.

Good Example

Section titled “Good Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Type Safety ViolationsAny abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore../tz1xx-type-violations.xml

View raw XML source

Type Safety Violations

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TZ100-OVERUSE-ANYUsing any to bypass type checking instead of proper types.
TZ101-TS-IGNOREUsing @ts-ignore or @ts-expect-error to suppress type errors instead of fixing them.
TZ102-NON-NULL-ASSERTIONUsing the non-null assertion operator (!) instead of proper null checks.
TZ103-UNSAFE-TYPE-ASSERTIONUsing as Type to force a type without runtime validation.
TZ104-OBJECT-AS-MAPUsing plain objects (Record&lt;string, T&gt;) as maps when Map&lt;K, V&gt; is more appropriate.
TZ105-IMPLICIT-ANY-RETURNFunctions with inferred any return type due to missing annotation or unsafe operations.
TZ106-STRING-TYPED-PARAMETERSUsing bare string for parameters that accept only specific values.
TZ107-INTERFACE-FOR-UNIONUsing a single interface with optional fields to model variants instead of a discriminated union.
TZ108-NAMESPACE-ABUSEUsing TypeScript namespace for code organization instead of ES modules.
TZ109-FUNCTION-OVERLOADS-ABUSEExcessive function overloads when a union parameter or generic would be clearer.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TZ100-OVERUSE-ANY

Section titled “TZ100-OVERUSE-ANY”

Using any to bypass type checking instead of proper types.

  • Better: TY103-GENERICS. Use unknown for truly unknown values, generics for reusable code, or specific union types.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Defeats the entire type system. Errors propagate silently through any-typed code, discovered only at runtime.

Notes

Section titled “Notes”
  • Common Causes: - Lazily typing external data (use TY405-VALIDATE-BOUNDARIES) - Third-party lib without types (use @types or declaration file) - Complex generic expressions (simplify or use type aliases)

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ101-TS-IGNORE

Section titled “TZ101-TS-IGNORE”

Using @ts-ignore or @ts-expect-error to suppress type errors instead of fixing them.

  • Better: Fix the underlying type issue. If suppression is truly needed, use @ts-expect-error with an explanation (it fails when the error is fixed, unlike @ts-ignore).
  • Why This Is Problematic: Hides real type errors. @ts-ignore suppresses future errors too, masking regressions. Back to Rule Catalog

TZ102-NON-NULL-ASSERTION

Section titled “TZ102-NON-NULL-ASSERTION”

Using the non-null assertion operator (!) instead of proper null checks.

  • Better: TY404-TYPE-GUARDS. Use optional chaining (?.), nullish coalescing (??), or explicit guards.
  • Why This Is Problematic: The ! operator lies to the compiler. If the value is actually null/undefined, it crashes at runtime.

Bad Example

Section titled “Bad Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ103-UNSAFE-TYPE-ASSERTION

Section titled “TZ103-UNSAFE-TYPE-ASSERTION”

Using as Type to force a type without runtime validation.

  • Better: TY405-VALIDATE-BOUNDARIES. Parse with Zod/io-ts at boundaries; use type guards for narrowing.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Type assertions override the compiler without runtime checks. If the data doesn’t match, errors appear far from the assertion.

Bad Example

Section titled “Bad Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ104-OBJECT-AS-MAP

Section titled “TZ104-OBJECT-AS-MAP”

Using plain objects (Record&lt;string, T&gt;) as maps when Map&lt;K, V&gt; is more appropriate.

  • Better: Use Map&lt;K, V&gt; for dynamic key/value stores. Use objects/interfaces for known shapes.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Object keys are always strings, prototype pollution is possible, and for...in iterates inherited properties. Map has proper size tracking, iteration order, and supports non-string keys. Back to Rule Catalog

TZ105-IMPLICIT-ANY-RETURN

Section titled “TZ105-IMPLICIT-ANY-RETURN”

Functions with inferred any return type due to missing annotation or unsafe operations.

  • Better: TY101-EXPLICIT-RETURN-TYPES. Annotate return types on exported functions.
  • Why This Is Problematic: any return types infect callers, spreading unsafe types silently through the codebase.

Bad Example

Section titled “Bad Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ106-STRING-TYPED-PARAMETERS

Section titled “TZ106-STRING-TYPED-PARAMETERS”

Using bare string for parameters that accept only specific values.

  • Better: TY105-LITERAL-AND-CONST. Use literal unions, branded types, or template literal types.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Accepts any string at compile time, deferring validation to runtime.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ107-INTERFACE-FOR-UNION

Section titled “TZ107-INTERFACE-FOR-UNION”

Using a single interface with optional fields to model variants instead of a discriminated union.

  • Better: TY106-DISCRIMINATED-UNIONS. Model variants as separate types with a shared discriminant property.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Optional fields make every variant look the same. Callers must check multiple fields to determine the variant, and the compiler cannot enforce completeness.

Bad Example

Section titled “Bad Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ108-NAMESPACE-ABUSE

Section titled “TZ108-NAMESPACE-ABUSE”

Using TypeScript namespace for code organization instead of ES modules.

  • Better: TY200-ES-MODULES. Use ES modules and files for code organization.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Namespaces are a legacy feature. They don’t tree-shake, conflict with ES modules, and are unnecessary in modern TypeScript.
  • Tool Support: ESLint @typescript-eslint/no-namespace. Back to Rule Catalog

TZ109-FUNCTION-OVERLOADS-ABUSE

Section titled “TZ109-FUNCTION-OVERLOADS-ABUSE”

Excessive function overloads when a union parameter or generic would be clearer.

  • Better: Use union types for simple cases. Reserve overloads for when the return type varies based on the input type.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Overloads add complexity. The implementation signature must handle all cases anyway, and each overload is an additional surface to maintain. Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Module & Import ProblemsCircular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems../tz2xx-module-antipatterns.xml

View raw XML source

Module & Import Problems

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TZ200-CIRCULAR-DEPSCircular module dependencies that cause runtime initialization errors or undefined imports.
TZ201-BARREL-BLOATBarrel files (index.ts) that re-export everything from every module, causing bundle bloat and slow IDE performance.
TZ202-SIDE-EFFECT-IMPORTSModules that execute side effects on import (register globals, modify prototypes, start servers).
TZ203-WILDCARD-REEXPORTSUsing export * from which leaks implementation details and creates name collisions.
TZ204-DEFAULT-EXPORT-MISMATCHDefault exports that lose their name binding, causing inconsistent names across imports.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TZ200-CIRCULAR-DEPS

Section titled “TZ200-CIRCULAR-DEPS”

Circular module dependencies that cause runtime initialization errors or undefined imports.

  • Better: TY204-DEPENDENCY-DIRECTION. Extract shared types into a separate module. Use import type for type-only cycles. Restructure to break the loop.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Circular dependencies can produce undefined at runtime depending on module resolution order. They indicate poor separation of concerns. Back to Rule Catalog

TZ201-BARREL-BLOAT

Section titled “TZ201-BARREL-BLOAT”

Barrel files (index.ts) that re-export everything from every module, causing bundle bloat and slow IDE performance.

  • Better: TY202-BARREL-FILES. Use barrels only at package boundaries. Import directly from specific modules for internal code.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Deep barrels force bundlers to load entire dependency trees. They slow down IDE auto-completion and type checking. Back to Rule Catalog

TZ202-SIDE-EFFECT-IMPORTS

Section titled “TZ202-SIDE-EFFECT-IMPORTS”

Modules that execute side effects on import (register globals, modify prototypes, start servers).

  • Better: Export initialization functions that consumers call explicitly. Keep module top-level code pure.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Side-effect imports create hidden coupling, break tree-shaking, make testing difficult, and cause order-dependent bugs.

Bad Example

Section titled “Bad Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ203-WILDCARD-REEXPORTS

Section titled “TZ203-WILDCARD-REEXPORTS”

Using export * from which leaks implementation details and creates name collisions.

  • Better: TY201-EXPLICIT-EXPORTS. Use explicit named re-exports to control the public API.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Wildcard re-exports expose internal types, cause ambiguous exports when names collide, and make API surface unpredictable. Back to Rule Catalog

TZ204-DEFAULT-EXPORT-MISMATCH

Section titled “TZ204-DEFAULT-EXPORT-MISMATCH”

Default exports that lose their name binding, causing inconsistent names across imports.

  • Better: TY201-EXPLICIT-EXPORTS. Use named exports so the name is consistent everywhere.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Default exports can be imported under any name, leading to inconsistent naming across the codebase and harder searching/refactoring. Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

index

Groups

Section titled “Groups”

Group-level groupings within a Categories bucket.

ItemSummaryOpen
Async & Runtime HazardsUnhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state../tz3xx-async-hazards.xml

View raw XML source

Async & Runtime Hazards

Rule Catalog

Section titled “Rule Catalog”
RuleSummary
TZ300-UNHANDLED-PROMISESCalling an async function without await or an attached rejection handler, leaving the Promise to reject unobserved.
TZ301-CALLBACK-PROMISE-MIXMixing callback-style and Promise-style APIs in the same flow.
TZ302-ASYNC-VOID-EVENTSPassing async functions to event handlers or callbacks that expect synchronous void return.
TZ303-SHARED-MUTABLE-STATESharing mutable state between async operations without coordination.
TZ304-PROMISE-CONSTRUCTOR-ANTIPATTERNWrapping an existing Promise in new Promise() unnecessarily.
TZ305-SEQUENTIAL-INDEPENDENT-AWAITSSequentially awaiting independent Promises instead of running them concurrently.

Rule Details

Section titled “Rule Details”

TZ300-UNHANDLED-PROMISES

Section titled “TZ300-UNHANDLED-PROMISES”

Calling an async function without await or an attached rejection handler, leaving the Promise to reject unobserved.

  • Better: TY500-ASYNC-AWAIT. Always await promises or attach a .catch() (or equivalent) handler. Use the void operator only for intentional fire-and-forget calls where the Promise implementation handles errors internally or you rely on separate/global error handling.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Unhandled promise rejections can terminate or crash Node.js processes (depending on runtime version and configuration) and are typically only logged and then ignored in browsers.
  • Tool Support: ESLint @typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises, @typescript-eslint/no-misused-promises.

Bad Example

Section titled “Bad Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ301-CALLBACK-PROMISE-MIX

Section titled “TZ301-CALLBACK-PROMISE-MIX”

Mixing callback-style and Promise-style APIs in the same flow.

  • Better: Wrap callback APIs with util.promisify or new Promise() at the boundary, then use async/await throughout.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Mixed styles produce confusing error handling, nested callbacks inside async functions, and easy-to-miss unhandled errors. Back to Rule Catalog

TZ302-ASYNC-VOID-EVENTS

Section titled “TZ302-ASYNC-VOID-EVENTS”

Passing async functions to event handlers or callbacks that expect synchronous void return.

  • Better: Wrap async logic in a non-async handler that catches and handles errors explicitly.
  • Why This Is Problematic: The caller ignores the returned Promise, so rejections go unhandled. DOM event handlers (for example, addEventListener) and some framework callbacks ignore returned Promises, depending on version.
  • Tool Support: ESLint @typescript-eslint/no-misused-promises.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”
Bad Example 1
Section titled “Bad Example 1”
Good Example 1
Section titled “Good Example 1”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ303-SHARED-MUTABLE-STATE

Section titled “TZ303-SHARED-MUTABLE-STATE”

Sharing mutable state between async operations without coordination.

  • Better: TY300-READONLY-BY-DEFAULT. Use immutable data, message passing, or explicit synchronization (mutex/semaphore).
  • Why This Is Problematic: Concurrent async operations can interleave, producing race conditions even in single-threaded JavaScript when they share mutable variables.

Bad Example

Section titled “Bad Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ304-PROMISE-CONSTRUCTOR-ANTIPATTERN

Section titled “TZ304-PROMISE-CONSTRUCTOR-ANTIPATTERN”

Wrapping an existing Promise in new Promise() unnecessarily.

  • Better: Return the existing Promise directly or use async/await.
  • Why This Is Problematic: The extra wrapper adds complexity and can swallow errors if reject is not called properly.

Bad Example

Section titled “Bad Example”

Back to Rule Catalog

TZ305-SEQUENTIAL-INDEPENDENT-AWAITS

Section titled “TZ305-SEQUENTIAL-INDEPENDENT-AWAITS”

Sequentially awaiting independent Promises instead of running them concurrently.

  • Better: TY501-PROMISE-CONCURRENCY. Use Promise.all for independent operations.
  • Why This Is Problematic: Sequential awaits add up latency linearly. Independent fetches that take 200ms each take 400ms sequentially vs 200ms concurrently. Back to Rule Catalog

View raw XML source

Context index

Entry Points

Section titled “Entry Points”

Primary context entry points.

ItemSummaryOpen

Java standards catalogFull Java rule catalog. — doc./coding-standards/java/index-catalog.xml
Python standards catalogFull Python rule catalog. — doc./coding-standards/python/index-catalog.xml
TypeScript standards catalogFull TypeScript rule catalog. — doc./coding-standards/typescript/index-catalog.xml

View raw XML source

Contributions

This section contains additional contributions that complement the core repository. These are resources contributed by BCG X practitioners to share perspective, patterns, and lessons learned from real engagements.

Contributions are a great place to:

  • share new patterns, playbooks, or examples
  • capture practical guidance from delivery work
  • publish evolving viewpoints without needing to restructure core docs

If you have something that would help other teams adopt GenAI effectively, add it here.

Available contributions

Section titled “Available contributions”
  • Prompt Playbook — prompting patterns and practices for AI-assisted development.
  • Pattern Library — the Agentic Coding patterns, obstacles, and anti-patterns library.

Prompt Playbook

A Developer’s Guide to Effective GenAI Prompting

Introduction

Section titled “Introduction”

This playbook contains battle-tested prompt patterns for developers using GenAI tools. It’s organized into three fundamental sections: the core development workflow that changes how you build software, paradigm shifts in your approach, and tactical patterns for effective AI collaboration.

Part 1: The Core Development Flow

Section titled “Part 1: The Core Development Flow”

The Foundation: Design → Plan → Implement → Review

These four patterns form the essential workflow that should guide every significant development effort with AI. Together, they create a discipline that prevents false starts, catches issues early, and ensures you’re always building the right thing the right way.

The Flow in Practice:

  1. Design the solution by researching the problem space and creating the technical design
  2. Plan by breaking down the design into actionable tasks with acceptance criteria
  3. Implement by executing tasks step-by-step with test-driven development
  4. Review at each gate—Lead reviews design, plan, and code before proceeding

This sequence transforms AI from a code generator into a strategic partner that helps you think through problems systematically before writing a single line of code. The Review stage ensures human oversight at critical checkpoints.

See Workflows for the complete four-stage SDLC and command reference.

1. Design Before You Build

Section titled “1. Design Before You Build”

Type: Problem analysis and planning (Two-Phase Approach)

The Two-Phase Research Strategy:

Phase 1: Deep Research (10-15 minutes) Use specialized tools to gather comprehensive information:

Phase 2: Distillation & Documentation (5-10 minutes) Synthesize findings into an actionable research document:

Why this changes everything: Traditional development often jumps straight to code. With deep research tools, you can gather insights from hundreds of sources in minutes, then distill that into a focused research document. This prevents false starts and ensures you’re solving the right problem with the right approach.

Claude’s Advanced Research Capabilities:

  • Agentic investigation: Breaks complex questions into parts, researching each deeply
  • Multiple source types: Searches web, Google Workspace (Gmail, Docs, Calendar), and connected Integrations
  • Comprehensive reports: 5-15 minutes for most queries, up to 45 minutes for complex investigations
  • Clear citations: Links directly to original sources for verification
  • Speed advantage: According to Anthropic’s official documentation, Claude delivers faster results than competitors (under a minute for most queries vs. 30+ minutes). [Source: https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-advanced-research]

What makes a good research document:

  • Context first: Clearly describe the problem or scenario that needs addressing, including business goals and technical constraints
  • Explore alternatives: Document multiple potential approaches with their trade-offs, not just the obvious solution
  • Be concise but complete: Include only relevant details; use clear language that both technical and non-technical stakeholders can understand
  • Cite your sources: When referencing industry standards, frameworks, or best practices, document these references (deep research tools do this automatically)
  • Make it actionable: The research should provide enough information to make an informed decision and move to planning
  • Keep it accessible: Store research documents in a central location where all team members can access and reference them
  • Involve stakeholders early: Share research documents with affected teams to gather feedback and ensure alignment

Key insight: Deep research tools can investigate hundreds of sources in the time it used to take to read one article. The bottleneck is no longer gathering information—it’s synthesizing it into actionable insights. This two-phase approach ensures you get both breadth (comprehensive research) and focus (distilled documentation).

2. Plan, Review, Then Execute

Section titled “2. Plan, Review, Then Execute”

Type: Structured implementation with human checkpoint

The Spec-Driven Approach:

Modern planning goes beyond simple task lists—it creates formal specifications that both humans and AI can use as source of truth. Spec-driven development ensures everyone agrees on requirements before code is written.

Core Planning Example:

Why spec-driven development changes everything:

  • Deterministic outputs: AI coding assistants are powerful but unpredictable when requirements live in chat history. Specs lock intent before implementation.
  • Explicit scope: Structured specifications keep what’s being built auditable and reviewable
  • Living documentation: Specs evolve with your system, creating documentation that never gets stale
  • Human-AI alignment: Both stakeholders agree on what to build before any code exists

The human checkpoint is critical: Plans and specs are cheap to change, code is expensive. Always review specifications before implementation begins. This is where you catch scope creep, identify missing requirements, and ensure the approach aligns with your architectural vision.

Key insight: You become the architect while AI becomes your implementation team. This flip means you can review and adjust strategy when it’s just structured text, not tangled dependencies. Spec-driven development makes this process repeatable and scalable across your entire team.

Note: In this section, “spec” refers to industry terminology and external spec-driven development tools. Within this project, we use “design” for ticket-level low-level designs (LLD) and “blueprint” for project-level high-level designs (HLD).

Specification Tools:

Several tools formalize the spec-driven development workflow:

  • OpenSpec - Separates current truth ( openspec/specs/) from proposed changes (openspec/changes/). Excels at brownfield projects and multi-spec updates. Works with Claude Code, Cursor, and other AI tools via slash commands or AGENTS.md.

  • Kiro - Strong for greenfield/0→1 features. Spreads updates across multiple spec folders.

  • spec-kit - Another option for new feature specifications.

Example: OpenSpec Workflow

These tools make spec-driven development repeatable across teams and ensure that requirements don’t get lost in chat history. Choose based on your workflow: OpenSpec for evolving systems with frequent updates, Kiro/spec-kit for net-new feature development.

3. Test-Driven by Default

Section titled “3. Test-Driven by Default”

Type: Quality-first development Example:

Why it changes everything: AI never gets lazy about writing tests. You can actually achieve 100% TDD because writing tests is as fast as writing code. This shifts testing from a chore to the default starting point.

The safety net: Tests serve as executable documentation and prevent regressions. When you implement with TDD, you’re not just writing code—you’re building confidence that your solution works correctly from day one.

Part 2: Additional Development Paradigms

Section titled “Part 2: Additional Development Paradigms”

More Ways AI Changes Your Workflow

These patterns complement the core flow and help you maximize the value of AI collaboration throughout the development lifecycle.

4. Always Consider Alternatives

Section titled “4. Always Consider Alternatives”

Type: Solution space exploration Example:

Why it changes everything: Exploring multiple implementations used to be time-expensive. Now you can see the entire solution space before committing. This prevents technical debt from choosing the first working solution rather than the right solution.

5. Learn While Building

Section titled “5. Learn While Building”

Type: Embedded knowledge transfer Example:

Why it changes everything: Every coding session becomes a learning opportunity. You’re not just getting code; you’re getting mentorship. This compounds over time - you become a better developer while shipping faster.

6. Continuous Prompt Refinement

Section titled “6. Continuous Prompt Refinement”

Type: Meta-learning optimization Example:

Why it changes everything: Your prompts become reusable assets. This builds a library of proven patterns for your team and transforms tribal knowledge into documented workflows.

Part 3: Tactical AI Collaboration

Section titled “Part 3: Tactical AI Collaboration”

In-the-Weeds Patterns for Daily Development

These are your go-to patterns for specific situations. Think of them as your tactical toolkit for getting the most out of your AI pair programmer.

Context Management

Section titled “Context Management”

Sequential Context Loading

Section titled “Sequential Context Loading”

When to use: Starting a complex session with lots of background Example:

Why it works: Prevents premature solutions and ensures full context absorption.

Incremental Building

Section titled “Incremental Building”

When to use: Building complex features Example:

Why it works: Maintains working code at each step, makes debugging trivial.

Quality & Review

Section titled “Quality & Review”

Code Review Assistant

Section titled “Code Review Assistant”

When to use: Before committing code Example:

Why it works: Catches issues a tired developer might miss, teaches best practices.

Refactoring Guide

Section titled “Refactoring Guide”

When to use: Improving existing code Example:

Why it works: Makes refactoring decisions explicit and learnable.

Problem Solving

Section titled “Problem Solving”

Interactive Clarification

Section titled “Interactive Clarification”

When to use: Requirements are unclear Example:

Why it works: AI spots ambiguities humans miss, surfaces hidden requirements.

Error Diagnosis

Section titled “Error Diagnosis”

When to use: Debugging complex issues Example:

Why it works: Teaches debugging methodology while solving the immediate problem.

Constraint-Based Solutions

Section titled “Constraint-Based Solutions”

When to use: Working within limitations Example:

Why it works: Forces creative solutions that fit real-world constraints.

Documentation & Planning

Section titled “Documentation & Planning”

Auto-Documentation

Section titled “Auto-Documentation”

When to use: APIs, complex functions, or public interfaces Example:

Why it works: Consistent, complete documentation with minimal effort.

Integration Planning

Section titled “Integration Planning”

When to use: Connecting systems Example:

Why it works: Surfaces integration issues before they become production problems.

Performance Analysis

Section titled “Performance Analysis”

When to use: Optimizing critical paths Example:

Why it works: Combines theoretical analysis with practical improvements.

The Workflow: Putting It All Together

Section titled “The Workflow: Putting It All Together”

Here’s how the core flow and tactical patterns work together in practice:

Quick Decision Tree

Section titled “Quick Decision Tree”

“I’m starting something new” → Always use Part 1: Design → Plan → Implement → Review (with TDD)

“I’m in the middle of coding” → Use Part 3: Tactical patterns

“I’m stuck” → Error Diagnosis → Alternative Approaches → Interactive Clarification

“I’m learning” → Learn While Building → Continuous Prompt Refinement

“I’m maintaining code” → Code Review → Refactoring Guide → Auto-Documentation

“I’m reviewing work” → Use /pr-review for code review, human review for design/plan

Remember: AI Changes the Economics

Section titled “Remember: AI Changes the Economics”
  • Exploration is now cheap - Try multiple approaches
  • Documentation is now free - Generate it always
  • Research is now fast - Spend 15 minutes researching to save days of rework
  • Planning is now thorough - Get architectural review before writing code
  • Testing is now painless - Write tests first, always
  • Learning is now embedded - Every session can teach you something
  • Review is now instant - Get feedback before committing

Deep Research Tools Comparison

Section titled “Deep Research Tools Comparison”
ToolSpeedDepthBest For
Claude Research5-15 min (up to 45)ComprehensiveInternal + external sources, Google Workspace integration
Perplexity Deep Research3-5 minVery comprehensiveFast, broad web research with citations
ChatGPT Deep Research10-30 minVery comprehensiveThorough investigations, reasoning-heavy tasks
Gemini Deep Research15+ minComprehensiveFree tier access, Google integration
Claude Extended ThinkingVariableDeep reasoningComplex problem-solving, use “ultrathink”

Pro Tip: Use Claude Research for speed and integration with your workspace. Use Perplexity when you need the fastest comprehensive web research. Use ChatGPT Deep Research for the most thorough investigations. All tools provide citations for verification.

Related Documentation

Section titled “Related Documentation”
  • Setup Guide - Prerequisites and environment setup
  • Workflows - Development workflows and commands that implement these patterns
  • Field Guide Overview - What this repository is and how to use it
  • Artifacts Guide - Understanding the artifacts structure
  • Tickets Guide - Guide to working with tickets and artifacts

Pattern Library

This is an additional contribution from BCG X practitioners: a reference library of patterns, obstacles, and anti-patterns for effective AI-assisted development.

How to use it

Section titled “How to use it”
  • Browse the Patterns, Obstacles, and Anti-Patterns sections in the sidebar.
  • Use site search to jump directly to a specific pattern name.

Active Partner

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI defaults to silent compliance, even when instructions don’t make sense.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Explicitly grant permission and encourage AI to:

  • Push back on unclear instructions
  • Challenge assumptions that seem wrong
  • Flag contradictions and impossibilities
  • Say “I don’t understand what you’re seeing”
  • Disagree and propose alternatives
  • Explain its interpretation before acting

Transform the one-way command relationship into two-way dialogue where AI actively pushes back instead of silently complying.

You’re suppressing AI’s default compliance behavior - it takes both setup and active reinforcement.

In ground rules: Set permanent permissions for AI to push back

In conversation: Actively reinforce when you need it:

  • “What do you really think, honestly?”
  • “Do you have any questions?”
  • “Is everything clear?”

Example

Section titled “Example”

Added to ground rules:

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Silent Misalignment, Tell Me a Lie, Flying Blind
  • Counters: Compliance Bias, Obedient Contractor, Keeping Up

Approved Fixtures

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Generating both tests and code with the AI and not checking is risky, but the AI is also prone to generating lots of tests quickly. Reviewing many AI-generated tests quickly becomes impractical, especially when assertions are complex.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Design tests around approval files that combine input and expected output in a domain-specific easy-to-validate format. This is a special case of the Constrained Tests pattern.

Validate the test execution logic once. After that, adding new test cases only requires reviewing fixtures.

Structure each approval file to contain:

  • Input data (context, parameters, state)
  • Expected output (results, side effects, API calls)
  • Format adapted to your problem domain for easy scanning

The test runner reads fixtures, executes code, and regenerates approval files. Validation becomes a simple diff review.

This pattern works best for problems that have an intuitive visual representation that is straightforward to check, but can also be used for checking call sequences.

Example

Section titled “Example”

The pattern adapts to different domains:

Testing a multi-step process with external service calls:

Create fixtures like checkout-with-discount.approved.md:

Single test reads all .approved.md files, executes flows, regenerates files with actual results. Review is scanning markdown diffs, not reading assertion code.

Testing visual algorithms:

Create fixtures like game-of-life-glider.approved.md:

Test reads all game-of-life fixtures, computes next generation, verifies output matches. Adding new test cases is drawing ASCII patterns - trivially easy to validate correctness by eye.

Testing refactorings:

Create fixture pairs like inline-variable.input.ts and inline-variable.approved.ts:

This example uses two separate files. One for the input and one for the expected output. The header contains the command that generates the approved output.

Input file:

Expected output file:

Note

Section titled “Note”

This pattern has similarities to Gherkin but better adapts to the specific domain, making the extra indirection worthwhile.

Approved Logs

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Bridging the gap between observing a bug and documenting it in a test. Reproducing production bugs in test environments is difficult, and writing tests from scratch during debugging is slow. With AI coding, explaining bugs can be harder than showing evidence - logs remove ambiguity between mental models.

This is a special case of the Constrained Tests pattern.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Turn production logs into regression tests immediately.

When a bug appears:

  1. Grab the relevant structured log section from production/staging/development
  2. Fix the incorrect log lines to show expected behavior
  3. Save as a test file (.log, .md, or domain-appropriate extension)
  4. The first log line specifies the entry point invocation and parameters
  5. Test runner parses first line, executes code, captures logs, compares against entire file

Requires structured logging throughout the system. If not present, start adding it as bugs surface.

Minimize entry points - use separate test directories per entry point or design logs to make copy boundaries obvious.

Unlike Approved Fixtures (optimized for validation ease), Approved Logs optimize for bug reproduction speed. They may feel cryptic without system familiarity but turn bug evidence into tests instantly.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Typical: Structured JSON logs

File: payment-tests/discount-calculation-bug.log

Test runner:

  1. Parses first line, extracts payment_processing event
  2. Calls payment entry point with {"user":"premium_member", "amount":1250}
  3. Captures all emitted logs
  4. Compares captured logs against entire file (including first line)

Production bug: discount wasn’t applied. Copy logs, fix discount_calculated and final_amount lines to show correct values, save as test. Bug is now documented.

Atypical: Mixed structured and visual logs

File: hexogram-tests/clue-calculation.md

Custom parser:

  1. Parses HIDDEN SOLUTION and PLAYER STATE as input (hex grids)
  2. Each DEBUG INFO section validates hover calculations for specific cells
  3. Visual format beats arrays of 0s and 1s for comprehension

Bespoke parsers for non-structured logs can enhance readability when domain-specific representations (ASCII grids, trees, graphs) make context obvious.

Background Agent

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Delegate standalone tasks to background agents running in parallel while you stay focused on main work.

Workflow:

  1. Collect: During main work, capture todos/ideas in markdown file (GTD-style)
  2. Identify: Find tasks that are standalone, well-sized, clear enough to delegate
  3. Spawn: Launch background agent per task (separate branch/context)
  4. Continue: Stay focused on main thread while agents work in parallel
  5. Integrate: Review and merge results when ready

What makes a task ready for background agent:

  • Standalone: doesn’t block your current work
  • Well-sized: clear scope, not too big
  • Clear enough: agent can execute without constant guidance

Human stays on main thread. Agent works asynchronously.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Main work: Refactoring authentication flow

While working, you notice:

  • Tests are missing for edge cases
  • Documentation needs updating
  • Dead code should be removed
  • Logging format is inconsistent

Collect these in todo.md. Each is standalone and clear.

Spawn background agents:

  • Agent 1: Add edge case tests (branch: add-edge-tests)
  • Agent 2: Update auth docs (branch: update-auth-docs)
  • Agent 3: Remove dead code (branch: cleanup-dead-code)

You continue refactoring. Agents work in parallel on their branches.

Tools that enable this:

  • Claude Code GitHub app: runs agents in GitHub Actions, creates branches
  • Multiple chat windows/sessions
  • Separate working directories (git worktrees)

Borrow Behaviors

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

The solution already exists, but it’s not in the form we need it, or not where we need it.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

AI can quickly grab and transform from other sources:

  • Styles from designs (colors, spacing, icons)
  • Patterns from other implementations
  • Code concepts across languages

Give AI an example and it adapts it to your context. Show a JavaScript nullable pattern, get the Python version. Point to a Figma design, get matching CSS. Reference another implementation, get your version.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Design to Code: Designer created Figma mockup to improve design of existing app → Use AI to apply it to your app

Cross-language: Show JavaScript nullables → AI creates Python equivalent with proper idioms

Parallel Borrowing: Created 10 implementations → Pick best parts from each → AI combines them. Really powerful

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Counters: Majority Solution Bias, Hallucinations
  • Similar to: Parallel Implementations

Canary in the Code Mine

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

As codebases grow and become more complex, AI agents start to perform worse. This degradation is especially pronounced when the AI is allowed to work independently for longer periods of time.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

When you see the AI struggle with code changes, treat this as a signal that code quality is degrading. Instead of getting frustrated with the AI, ask yourself: is this codebase maintainable?

The AI’s struggle is your canary - an early warning signal that code quality is declining. Use it as feedback to improve the codebase.

When you notice the warning signs:

  1. Ask the AI to review the code, identify code quality issues, and plan a refactor
  2. Review the result and provide further guidance if necessary
  3. If the AI fails to sufficiently clean the code, use IntelliJ or another advanced refactoring browser to manually clean up the code

Key insight: AI performance degradation often indicates duplication and other maintainability issues that also make the code harder for humans to work with.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Warning signs the canary is struggling:

Inconsistencies across pages

  • You ask AI to update user profile display to show new status badge
  • AI updates the main profile page but misses the sidebar and settings page
  • Same user info rendering logic is duplicated across multiple components
  • Signal: Code duplication - things that should change together aren’t co-located

Running out of context

  • AI attempts to implement a feature across multiple files
  • Hits context window limit and reports “I’m done”
  • Tests still fail
  • AI claims failures are “expected for some weird reason”
  • Signal: Code complexity and duplication forcing AI to load too much context

Making excuses

  • Tests fail after AI makes changes
  • AI insists the failures are “expected” or “will resolve themselves”
  • Contradicts itself about what’s working
  • Signal: Code is too complex for AI to reason about correctly; likely needs refactoring

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Signals: Degrades Under Complexity, Context Rot, Excess Verbosity
  • Leads to: Refactoring with SLIM, Chain of Small Steps

Cast Wide

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Don’t settle for your first solution. Actively push AI to show you more alternatives.

Your first solution comes from your limited knowledge and is prone to answer injection.

AI is extremely well-read; it knows approaches you’ve never encountered, entire solution categories you’re either not familiar with or not even thinking about.

Settling for first means missing a lot of possible alternatives outside your bubble.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

After you have an initial approach, deliberately push for different categories of solutions and surface blind spots:

  • “What alternative solutions to the same problem might be possible that we haven’t even considered?”
  • “What are we not even thinking about?”
  • “What should I be thinking about that I’m not considering at all regarding this problem?”
  • “Can we simplify this?..” -> “Could we go simpler than even that?”

Iterating several times and running several parallel explorations with different coding agents makes it even more powerful.

Use this when… you notice you’re already optimizing an approach you haven’t compared against real alternatives.

Example

Section titled “Example”

During a hackathon, needed to process emails from external clinics and extract data. Asked AI: “How do we read Outlook emails?” Standard answer: Microsoft Graph API with OAuth2 and Azure AD registration. A lot of busywork. Pushed for simpler: “What’s the simplest way?” → IMAP with basic auth. Better, but still network protocols and parsing. Kept pushing: “Even simpler?” → File monitoring emerged: configure Outlook to save emails to a folder, process as files. Zero network complexity.

Discovered an entire category we hadn’t considered: file-based processing instead of API solutions. This made a big difference in time spent setting it up. In the end, contributed to getting the project funding because it freed the team up to focus on important parts. The context didn’t warrant any more complexity than that, it needed to work on one person’s computer.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Answer Injection, Majority Solution Bias, Solution Fixation
  • Similar: Take All Paths, Parallel Implementations

Chain of Small Steps

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI degrades under complexity. Complex, multi-step tasks often fail or produce incorrect results when attempted in one shot. AI loses track of requirements and context as complexity increases.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

You have to manage the complexity. Break complex goals into small, focused, verifiable steps. Chain them together:

  1. Identify the complex goal
  2. Break it into small, independent steps
  3. Execute each step with AI, verify it works
  4. Commit or save progress after each step
  5. Move to the next step, building on verified foundation

Small steps are reliable. Each has narrow focus, which AI handles well. Verification catches problems early before they compound.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Hack4Good hackathon - needed version numbering for rapid releases (v1 → v51):

Instead of: “Add automated version bumping to the app”

Small steps:

  1. Step 1: Add version variable to JavaScript (starting at 001) and display it in settings UI

    • Small change to existing code
    • Verify: version shows in UI
    • Commit

  2. Step 2: Write update-version.sh script that reads and increments patch version

    • New focused script, single responsibility
    • Test: run script, verify version increments
    • Commit

  3. Step 3: Create git hook to run script automatically on push

    • New focused piece, single responsibility
    • Test: make commit, verify version bumps automatically
    • Commit

Result: fully automated version bumping. Each step was manageable for AI, verified before moving on. Complex goal achieved through chain of simple steps.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Prompt Overloading
  • Addresses: Degrades Under Complexity, Limited Focus
  • Similar to: Feedback Loop, Offload Deterministic

Check Alignment

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Misalignment between your understanding and AI’s only reveals itself after implementation, wasting time on wrong solutions.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Before letting AI implement, make it show its understanding:

  • “Tell me what you’re going to do before you do it”
  • “Show me your plan” or “Draw an architecture diagram”
  • “What questions do you have?” (not “ask me 3 questions” - let it surface real confusion)
  • “What are you trying to achieve?”

Force it to be very succinct - makes what it tells you scannable.

Why this works: When you see something, you immediately spot what needs adjusting. Catch it early, adjust before implementation starts - not after wasting time going the wrong direction.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Before refactoring: “Show me how you understand the current architecture and what you plan to change, succinctly”

AI draws diagram, reveals it misunderstood service boundaries. Corrected before any code was written - saved an hour of going in wrong direction.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Silent Misalignment
  • Counters: Black Box AI, Compliance Bias
  • Similar to: Approved Fixtures, Active Partner

Chunking

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI agents must keep everything in conscious attention - strategy, tactics, implementation, all file context. This cognitive overload degrades performance.

Humans chunk practiced tasks into automatic execution, freeing attention for higher-level thinking. We can’t retrain production models, but we can simulate this.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Use a main orchestrator agent with focused subagents:

  1. Main agent stays strategic: Plans, designs, breaks down work, integrates results
  2. Subagents handle execution: Read files, implement, test
  3. Delegate in parallel when possible: Multiple subagents on independent chunks

Key insight: Delegate execution to subagents like humans delegate practiced skills to automatic processes. Orchestrator operates strategically while subagents handle details.

How to apply:

  • Use planning mode for mid-size tasks
  • Request plans for parallel subagent execution
  • Include comprehensive test plans
  • Configure agent: “Always delegate coding tasks to subagents”

When this works:

  • Mid-size tasks in clean, modular codebases
  • First prototypes
  • Well-defined interfaces between components

When this fails:

  • Non-modular code
  • Tightly coupled systems
  • Subagents make incompatible tradeoffs

Example

Section titled “Example”

Building this website: Task: “Create Next.js app with unit and E2E tests, simple professional design”

Main agent planned architecture and test strategy. Subagents implemented in parallel: markdown processing, page components, unit tests, E2E tests. Result: fully functional site in one iteration.

Multi-layer system: Main agent designed layer interfaces. Subagents worked on separate layers following contracts. Worked because codebase was modular. In poorly structured code, subagents create integration problems.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Limited Focus, Limited Context Window, Degrades Under Complexity
  • Uses: Focused Agent
  • Similar to: Orchestrator, Background Agent

Coerce to Interface

Context

Section titled “Context”

You’re aiming for either a standardized way of doing something or a verified-to-work approach.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Design MCP tool interfaces that enforce structure through their API definition. Required fields, enums, and typed parameters become constraints the agent cannot bypass.

The interface itself documents usage - well-named fields and clear types reduce the need for separate explanations.

This shifts enforcement from instructions (“please include a title”) to mechanism (the tool requires a title parameter).

Example

Section titled “Example”

Learning MCP with constrained interface:

Every learning has identical anatomy. No variation possible. Results are immediately searchable by topic, filterable by tags, linkable through related fields.

Constrained Tests

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Code coverage is easy to cheat. Tests can execute code without assertions, making coverage an unreliable quality metric. In general, ensuring test validity is impossible, but for specific subsystems in constrained domains, you can design tests that guarantee validity.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Create a domain-specific language for testing that makes it impossible to write tests without sufficient assertions.

Use external DSLs (data files with custom parsers) rather than internal DSLs (fluent APIs). External DSLs make it easier to enforce required components - the parser can reject incomplete test specifications.

Structure the DSL to combine:

  • Input specification
  • Expected outcomes
  • Validation rules

When tests are constrained this way, code coverage becomes a reliable measure of test suite quality.

Implementation typically uses data providers where the external DSL drives a single test function that:

  1. Parses the DSL file
  2. Executes the system under test
  3. Validates outputs match the DSL specification

Example

Section titled “Example”

Two implementations of this pattern optimize for different goals:

Approved Fixtures uses domain-specific formats optimized for easy validation. Even non-experts can review and verify correctness at a glance.

Approved Logs captures production logs and turns them into regression tests immediately. Optimizes for bug reproduction speed over readability.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Flying Blind, Unvalidated Leaps
  • Addresses: Keeping Up, Productivity Illusion, Hallucinations
  • Uses: Approved Fixtures, Approved Logs

Context Management

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI has no persistent memory and context degrades over time. Everything you put in context competes for limited attention. Long conversations lose early instructions. There’s no undo—only reset.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Treat context as a scarce, degrading resource that requires active management.

You have only two operations: append to context (prompt it) and reset it (start a new conversation). Everything you do with AI works within this constraint.

Key techniques:

  • Use Ground Rules for always-loaded essentials
  • Use Reference Docs for on-demand knowledge
  • Use Knowledge Documents to preserve insights across resets
  • Use Extract Knowledge to capture learnings before they’re lost
  • Use Focused Agent to reduce cognitive load per session
  • Use Context Surface to curate what’s loaded
  • Reset frequently—before context degrades, not after

Example

Section titled “Example”

Managing a refactoring session:

  1. Start fresh session with ground rules auto-loaded
  2. Load only the 3-4 files relevant to current task
  3. Work until you notice quality degrading (instructions being ignored, repetition)
  4. Before resetting: “Save the key decisions we made to refactor-notes.md”
  5. Reset, reload ground rules + notes, continue from clean state

The discipline is: extract value before reset, load only what’s needed after.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Cannot Learn, Context Rot, Limited Context Window, Limited Focus
  • Uses: Ground Rules, Reference Docs, Knowledge Document, Extract Knowledge, Focused Agent, Context Surface

Context Markers

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI context is invisible. Can’t tell what rules AI is following, whether it read your ground rules, or if context has degraded.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Use visual markers (emojis) to signal active context:

  • Start every response with a marker showing current mode
  • Different markers for different contexts/roles
  • Stack markers when multiple contexts active
  • Special markers for specific actions (errors, re-reads)
  • Can be impromptu one-offs for crucial instructions (when adding an important instruction mid-conversation, ask it to reply to you with an additional emoji)

Makes the invisible parts of context visible at a glance.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Some example markers:

  • 🍀 = ground rules have been read
  • 🔴/🌱/🌀 = Shows specific TDD (red|green|refactor) phase and that it read tdd.md process file
  • ✅ = committer role active
  • ❗️ = flagging an error
  • ♻️ = rules just re-read
  • ✨📂 = creating new repository

How to set this up

In ground rules (user level):

In specialized contexts (committer):

In process files (TDD):

This way let them stack: ”🍀 ✅” = base rules loaded + committer role active. Easy to see what context AI is operating under.

Context Surface

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Dumping your entire codebase or massive documentation files into the chat window confuses the agent (“Distracted Agent”) and wastes tokens (“Limited Context Window”).

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Curate exactly what the tool “sees” for each task. Treat the context window as a carefully managed surface area, not a dump truck.

Techniques

Section titled “Techniques”
  1. Explicit File Selection: Don’t use @codebase if you can select specific files. Open only the 3-4 files relevant to the immediate task.
  2. Semantic References: Use features like @filename or @symbol (in Cursor) to anchor the agent to specific definitions without dumping the whole file if possible.
  3. Link, Don’t Dump: If a document is too large, provide a link or a summary.
  4. Context Configuration: Use .cursor/rules/*.mdc (Cursor) or AGENTS.md to auto-load high-level context, but keep it brief.

Benefits

Section titled “Benefits”
  • Higher Accuracy: The agent focuses on the relevant signal.
  • Lower Cost: Fewer tokens used.
  • Faster Response: Less data to process.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Distracted Agent
  • Addresses: Limited Context Window, Limited Focus
  • Used by: Context Management

Extract Knowledge

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Valuable insights, corrections, and preferences emerge during conversations, but they’re ephemeral. Without capture, they disappear when you start a new conversation, forcing you to repeat yourself session after session.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Like extract variable for conversations: when you figure something out, explicitly ask AI to save it to a file.

  • Save as you go - don’t wait until the end of the session
  • Review and edit - AI’s first pass may need refinement
  • Modify existing docs - update files when you discover improvements

Example

Section titled “Example”

While integrating the uv package manager, AI repeatedly uses incorrect syntax. You tell it correct commands via search or experimentation, then ask it to create uv.md documenting the correct commands. Next session, you load that file when you need it, and AI uses uv correctly from the start.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Cannot Learn
  • Uses: Knowledge Document
  • Used by: Context Management, Knowledge Checkpoint, Show the Agent Let it Repeat/Automate

Feature-First Organization

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Agents struggle to navigate codebases organized by technical layer (e.g., controllers/, services/, models/). To implement a single feature, the agent must touch files across the entire directory tree, leading to context fragmentation, “Context Rot”, and a higher chance of missing related changes.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Colocate all code related to a specific feature in a single directory.

Instead of:

Do this:

Why It Works for Agents

Section titled “Why It Works for Agents”
  1. Context Locality: When an agent is working on “Users”, it can look at src/features/user/ and see everything relevant. It doesn’t need to hunt through 5 different folders.
