  2. Explicit Boundaries: It’s easier for an agent to understand what not to touch.
  3. Reduced Hallucinations: With all related types and logic in one place, the agent is less likely to hallucinate imports or function signatures.

Feedback Flip

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI focused on implementing is often unable to keep everything you’re asking it to do in parallel in focus and give it equal priority. So code quality and other potential considerations don’t get enough attention and suffer as a result.

The First output is rarely optimal, but you don’t have to limit yourself to the first output.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Flip from producing to evaluating:

  1. AI implements the task
  2. Different AI (or same AI, refocused): Gets task + code diff → “Find problems and suggest improvements”
  3. Feed critique back to original AI to fix

Use this when… you can feel the agent “locking in” on implementation and starting to miss quality, constraints, or review concerns.

Variations:

  • Different models, different windows (most variety)
  • Same model, different windows (very effective)
  • Same model, same window (just shift the focus)

Before human review: Do this before PR/code review - catches obvious issues so humans start from better ground.

Why This Works

Section titled “Why This Works”

When implementing, AI seems to be hyper-focusing on getting the goal completed, and the main goal to it is usually implementing the feature asked of it. Quality takes lower precedence and suffers.

Flip the focus to finding problems; now quality is the main goal. AI sees issues it couldn’t see before because you’ve shifted what it’s focused on. Even the same AI in the next prompt.

We’re shifting its focus to a new goal, and making it what is important to us its main priority for one of the iterations.

Example

Section titled “Example”

AI implements authentication. Focused on making it work.

Flip: Second AI gets task + diff, finds a lot of issues with implementation.

Give feedback to the first AI again, it fixes → code ready for human review.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Flying Blind
  • Uses: Feedback Loop

Feedback Loop

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Set up automated feedback, give AI permission to iterate autonomously until goal is reached.

  1. Identify clear success signal (tests pass, UI matches design, coverage hits X%)
  2. Give AI access to that signal (tests, devtools, linter, logs)
  3. Grant explicit permission: “Keep iterating until tests pass”
  4. Step away - AI checks its own work and keeps refining

Human elevates from tactical executor to strategic director.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Bug fix with DevTools MCP:

  • Goal: Fix layout bug in web app
  • Signal: DevTools screenshots showing the visual issue
  • Setup: Point AI to devtools
  • Permission: “Keep trying until the layout matches the design”
  • AI iterates: change code → check screenshot → adjust → repeat

Debugging with console logs:

  • Goal: Understand why feature behaves incorrectly
  • Signal: Console output showing timing, state, assumptions
  • Setup: “Add console.log to help yourself understand what’s wrong”
  • Permission: “Debug this until you find the root cause”
  • AI iterates: add logs → run → validate assumptions → adjust → repeat

Getting tests to pass:

  • Goal: All tests green
  • Signal: Test runner output
  • Permission: “Fix the failing tests, keep running them until they all pass”
  • AI iterates: read failure → fix code → run tests → repeat

CI monitoring:

  • Goal: Green CI build
  • Signal: Direct access to CI state/logs (e.g., via gh CLI)
  • Permission: “Monitor CI and fix issues until build passes”
  • AI iterates: check status → read logs → fix → push → check again

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Flying Blind, Unvalidated Leaps
  • Addresses: Keeping Up, Productivity Illusion
  • Similar to: Playgrounds, Chain of Small Steps
  • Uses: Approved Fixtures, Constrained Tests

Focused Agent

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

LLMs have limited attention. The more you ask an agent to handle, the worse it performs at everything - even following explicit ground rules.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Prefer single, narrow responsibility on important tasks.

This gives the AI cognitive space to:

  • Actually follow your ground rules
  • Pay attention to details that matter for that specific task
  • Perform at its best instead of spreading thin

Example

Section titled “Example”

Dedicated committer agent - only writes commit messages. Immediately caught naming convention violations and accidental node_modules commits.

Main development agent with identical ground rules and same model never caught these issues. Its attention was diluted across coding, debugging, architecture decisions The focused committer could dedicate all its attention to commit quality.

Small, focused agents > large, scattered agents.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Distracted Agent
  • Addresses: Limited Focus, Context Rot
  • Used by: Chunking, Context Management

Ground Rules

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

You need essential information in every session without re-explaining or remembering to load it.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Knowledge documents that auto-load when you open a session. They’re always in context.

Put only your most important things here - the things you always want the AI to know. Can be scoped hierarchically:

  • User level: Your preferred communication style, tools available in all conversations
  • Project level: Team standards and crucial preferences

Contains: behaviors, tools, context, links - whatever is essential for each scope

Example

Section titled “Example”
  • AGENTS.md (tool-agnostic) or .cursor/rules/*.mdc (Cursor):
    • “Prefer simple solutions”,
    • “Tell me something I need to know even if I don’t want to hear it”,
    • “use ./speak.sh tool to talk to me outloud when you warn me about issues”
  • project/AGENTS.md
    • “Use TypeScript strict mode, all functions must have explicit return types”
    • “always run tests via ./test.sh”
    • links to key places in the repository

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Cannot Learn, Compliance Bias, Obedient Contractor
  • Uses: Knowledge Document
  • Used by: Context Management, Secret-Safe Agent

Habit Hooks

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Enforcing high coding standards without bloating AI’s context with extensive style guides is challenging. Generic reminders either get forgotten as conversations progress or consume too much context when repeated constantly. You want AI to follow your specific coding practices, but explaining them repeatedly is inefficient and unreliable.

As Kent Beck said: “I’m not a great programmer, I’m a good programmer with great habits.” The challenge is giving AI those same good habits.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

A habit is an action taken in response to a trigger. To simulate this, we use deterministic scripts that detect triggers (for example quality violations) and provide actionable prompts.

Optionally habit hooks can be blocking (for example the pre-commit hook can prevent commits until the triggers are resolved) and may have a snooze feature that the human can use to ignore false positives.

This approach reduces context bloat while improving compliance by providing precise, relevant guidance exactly when violations occur, rather than front-loading massive instructions that AI forgets or misapplies.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Common Quality Checks and Their Prompts

Section titled “Common Quality Checks and Their Prompts”

Duplication Detector

  • Trigger: Identical or near-identical code blocks detected
  • Prompt: “Test code duplication detected. Extract common test utilities, fixture handling, or assertion patterns into shared helper functions.”

Feature Envy Check

  • Trigger: Method primarily uses data/methods from another class
  • Prompt: “Consider moving these methods to the class they depend on most, or extract shared behavior.”

File Size

  • Trigger: File exceeds line count threshold
  • Prompt: “Split these files into smaller, focused modules immediately.”

Function Size

  • Trigger: Function exceeds complexity threshold
  • Prompt: “Analyze responsibilities first - what distinct concerns does this function handle? Consider: (1) Are these separate responsibilities that belong in different methods? (2) Should this become a class with multiple methods? (3) Can you group cohesive data into objects to reduce local variables? Avoid mechanical extraction - find true responsibility boundaries. If the code has many misplaced responsibilities you may need to first inline methods to see the whole picture and find a better way of redistributing functionality. Think of this when reducing line count seems particularly hard. Taking a step backwards may open up new, better possibilities.”

Git Diff Size

  • Trigger: Uncommitted changes exceed threshold
  • Prompt: “Commit smaller incremental changes with passing tests to maintain code quality.”

Linter Errors

  • Trigger: Linting violations detected
  • Prompt: “Run npm run lint:fix to automatically fix many of these issues. Manual fixes may be needed for logical errors.”

Dead Code Detector

  • Trigger: Unused methods/functions detected
  • Prompt: “Remove these unused methods to maintain codebase clarity.”

Comments Remover Some behaviors are almost impossible to get AI to stop doing reliably because their whole training contradicts it. For example, reliably getting it to stop commenting code. It’s easier to let it add the comment and have an auto-detecting hook provide it feedback to take the comment out.

  • Trigger: using tools Write|Edit|MultiEdit
  • Prompt: Do not write code comments, prefer expressive self-documenting names and code

Implementation Tips

Section titled “Implementation Tips”

AI can generally create these hooks with minimal prompting. The key is:

  • Specificity: Each prompt should be actionable and specific to the violation
  • Context efficiency: Inject guidance only when needed, not upfront
  • Treat as user prompts: Include guidance in your agent file to treat these prompts as user prompts

A hack that tends to work well is including a 🙂💬 emoji pair in the generated prompts, and mentioning their meaning in the agent file as CRITICAL.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Cannot Learn, Context Rot, Selective Hearing
  • Uses: Hooks, Feedback Loop
  • Used by: Risk-Adjusted Autonomy

Happy to Delete

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Humans are naturally precious about code, especially code they wrote themselves. This attachment makes us reluctant to delete failed attempts and start fresh. With AI-generated code, this instinct is counterproductive—the code is cheap to regenerate, yet we treat it as if it took hours to write by hand. This leads to forcing fixes on fundamentally flawed implementations instead of simply reverting and trying again.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Embrace the disposability of AI-generated code:

  • Treat AI code as cheap exploration: Unlike hand-written code, AI can regenerate solutions in seconds
  • Revert freely: When an attempt goes wrong, don’t force fixes—revert and try again
  • Start fresh with lessons learned: Each failed attempt teaches you what to specify better in the next prompt
  • Use git liberally: Commit before AI changes, making reversion effortless
  • Recognize diminishing returns early: If iteration 2-3 isn’t improving, delete and restart

The willingness to delete removes the pressure to “make it work” and paradoxically leads to better outcomes faster.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Bad: AI generates a refactoring that misses key requirements. You spend 30 minutes across 5 iterations trying to patch it up. The code gets messier with each fix attempt.

Good: AI generates a refactoring that misses key requirements. You immediately revert, refine your prompt with clearer constraints, and try again. The second attempt gets it right in one go because you learned from the first failure.

Example prompt for reverting:

The explicit git reset --hard instruction is important—without it, AI often tries to regenerate the original code from memory instead of properly reverting.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Sunk Cost
  • Leverages: Non-Determinism
  • Similar to: Parallel Implementations, Playgrounds

Hooks

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI has some deeply trained behaviors that might contradict your preferences and are nearly impossible to prevent upfront. Telling AI “don’t comment code” often fails because its training is stronger than your instructions. Fighting these instincts by trying to outprompt them is unreliable, so it is easier to let AI do them, automatically detect and correct it after the fact.

Solution

Section titled “Solution”

Some systems (Claude Code) have hooks that hook into deterministic points in the agent lifecycle. They are deterministic and in combination with custom scripts allow for flexible and reliable correction of behaviors.

Use lifecycle event hooks to intercept the agent’s workflow at specific trigger points and inject targeted prompts, corrections, validations, or add monitoring.

Example of lifecycle events:

Hooks run shell scripts at these events that can allow you to:

  • Validate code quality before commits proceed
  • Collect metrics on tool usage patterns
  • Enforce project-specific conventions automatically
  • Provide just-in-time guidance when specific conditions are detected
  • Inject reminders before the agent forgets your preferences

Example

Section titled “Example”

Create a hook that runs when the agent uses a writing tool (Write|Edit|MultiEdit in Claude Code) and a script that detects code comments. When the agent adds a code comment, the hook immediately gives feedback that the agent needs to address before it can proceed with the next task.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Cannot Learn, Context Rot, Selective Hearing
  • Enables: Habit Hooks, Risk-Adjusted Autonomy
  • Used by: Reminders

JIT Docs

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Models are trained on snapshots of the world. Vendor/library APIs change, internal tooling evolves, and the “most common” examples may be outdated. When an agent relies on training memory, you get stale guidance and hallucinated APIs.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Rely on up-to-date documentation searched in real-time by the agent, rather than AI’s outdated training data.

Use this when…

  • You’re using fast-moving frameworks/libraries (new major versions, recently changed APIs).
  • You see “this API doesn’t exist” errors or suspiciously confident claims without a source.
  • You want answers grounded in a real source right now, not “what the model remembers.”

Workflow:

  1. Point AI to documentation source once
  2. AI searches relevant sections in real-time based on your task
  3. AI fetches and uses current information
  4. One-click access via tools like Context7 MCP

Key shift: From “AI knows from training” → “AI searches current docs”

Steps

Section titled “Steps”
  1. Choose authoritative sources (vendor docs, your internal docs, runbooks).
  2. Ask the agent to retrieve from those sources as part of the task.
  3. Have it quote/link the relevant section before implementing.
  4. If it becomes a repeat need, extract the stable bits into Reference Docs (curated grounding).

Example

Section titled “Example”

Building with Next.js 15: Old way: AI uses outdated training data, suggests deprecated APIs New way: Point to Next.js docs once, AI searches current v15 docs for each feature you need

Using retrieval tooling (MCP):

Internal project patterns:

Using built-in web search (tool-agnostic):

  • “Search the official docs for the current API for X and cite the relevant section before implementing.”

What this solves:

  • Outdated training data → Current, accurate information
  • Hallucinated APIs → Real, version-specific docs
  • Manual doc hunting → Agent searches autonomously

Tools that enable this (examples)

Section titled “Tools that enable this (examples)”
  • Built-in web search in agentic coding tools
  • MCP servers like Context7 (library docs) or organization-specific MCPs (e.g., issue trackers / wikis)
  • Cursor note:
    • Use @Docs for official documentation sources (Cursor’s docs integration).
    • Use @Web when you need to search the wider internet beyond the curated docs sources.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Hallucinations
  • Similar: Reference Docs

Knowledge Checkpoint

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

You spend 30 minutes planning a feature with AI. Then AI implements it and fails. Now you’ve lost both the implementation AND the planning context. You have to explain everything again.

Your planning time is valuable. Implementation attempts are cheap to retry.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Before attempting implementation, checkpoint the plan:

  1. Plan the feature (with AI)
  2. Extract planning knowledge to a document (ask AI: “Save this to feature_todo.md”. Note: forcing it to be more succinct often produces better results here)
  3. Git commit = checkpoint
  4. Now attempt implementation
  5. If fails → git reset, retry without redoing planning

Protect your time, not the code. Code changes are cheap to regenerate. Your explanations and planning are expensive.

  • Protects human time investment in planning
  • Decouples deterministic planning from non-deterministic execution
  • Enables cheap retries

Example

Section titled “Example”

Spend 30 minutes outlining feature architecture with AI. Extract to project.md, commit. AI’s implementation fails. Reset in under a minute, try again - your planning is preserved. You can course correct, but you’re starting from a known good state.

Some models are over eager to start working and want to jump straight into implementation. You’ll have to interrupt it and save it to a file first.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Non-Determinism, Context Rot
  • Uses: Extract Knowledge, Knowledge Document
  • Used by: Context Management

Knowledge Composition

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

When you keep everything in one big file, you lose the ability to load just what you need. It’s all or nothing - either load the whole thing and bloat your context, or skip it entirely.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Split knowledge into focused, composable files. Like avoiding giant functions in code - each file should have a single responsibility.

This lets you load only what’s relevant for the current task instead of polluting context with everything.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Development Practices: Bad: One best-practices.md with git workflow + code review checklist + refactoring process Good: Separate files - git-workflow.md, code-review.md, refactoring-process.md Enables: Pull just refactoring process when cleaning code, or just git-workflow when resolving conflicts

Project Knowledge: Bad: One project-info.md with architecture + tech stack + deployment + API docs Good: Separate files - architecture.md, tech-stack.md, deployment.md, api-docs.md Enables: Pull just architecture when discussing design, or just tech-stack when evaluating libraries

Knowledge Document

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

To keep AI from degrading, you need to hit ‘reset’ often. But important knowledge vanishes when you reset.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Save important information to markdown files. Load them into context when needed.

This makes resetting easier: instead of losing everything, you extract the valuable parts to files first, then load them back into a clean context.

Example

Section titled “Example”
  • AGENTS.md - project-level agent instructions (tool-agnostic)
  • project.md - project-specific context (architecture, key constraints, links)
  • tdd-process.md - how you want to work
  • approval_tests.md - specific techniques to follow

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Cannot Learn, Context Rot
  • Used by: Context Management, Ground Rules, Extract Knowledge, Knowledge Checkpoint

Mind Dump

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Speak unfiltered thoughts directly into AI. Don’t organize, don’t structure - just dump everything.

Modern dictation (Whisper, Wispr Flow) + AI’s natural language understanding = you can think out loud and AI extracts signal from noise.

Speed of voice input enables qualitative shift:

  • From crafted prompts → stream of consciousness
  • From organized thoughts → raw brain dump
  • From typing bottleneck → speaking at thought speed

AI handles the mess. You handle the thinking.

After the dump: “Ask me questions” - turn monologue into dialogue. AI surfaces gaps and clarifies understanding.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Context loading while walking: Ramble entire project context into microphone. AI picks up requirements, constraints, history, concerns - everything you mention while pacing.

Debugging out loud: “So this function is supposed to… wait but it’s actually doing… and I think the issue is around… oh and also we changed that thing last week…” AI extracts the relevant details and problem structure.

Too tired to type: End of day, mentally exhausted. Can still talk through ideas. Voice carries you when fingers won’t.

Requirements gathering: Stakeholder talks, you record. AI extracts user stories, edge cases, constraints from the conversation.

Morning planning: Coffee in hand, speak your todo list, thoughts about priorities, concerns about blockers. AI structures it into actionable plan.

Noise Cancellation

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI is verbose by default, creating poor signal-to-noise ratio. Responses overwhelm you with detail, and documents accumulate bloat and outdated information over time (“document rot”) - making everything hard to scan and work with.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Explicitly ask AI to be more succinct - strip filler, compress to essence. Don’t let bloat accumulate.

For responses:

  • “Much more succinct please” (use this liberally)
  • “shorter”
  • “Higher level of abstraction”
  • “Give me TLDR”

For documents:

  • Regularly compress knowledge documents - ask AI to remove outdated info and noise
  • Delete mercilessly - Git is your friend, you can always get it back
  • Think of files as temporary vs permanent - toss the temporary ones frequently

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Documentation: Force AI to keep documentation extremely succinct. Helps handle document rot, keeps documentation scannable for humans and maintainable.

Debugging: Higher level debugging is often possible. Ask AI to explain the problematic code block in English on a high level. The nonsense becomes obvious fast.

Learning New Tools: Ask “What’s Cargo.toml?” and get a wall of text about Rust’s build system, dependencies, package metadata, workspace configuration… while you are just starting and don’t need all of these details yet.

Say “Much more succinct please” and get: “It’s Rust’s project configuration file - like package.json. Has metadata, dependencies, build settings.”

The level of detail now matches your level of curiosity.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Excess Verbosity
  • Similar to: Semantic Zoom
  • Used by: Context Management

Offload Deterministic

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI is non-deterministic. Using it for deterministic work (counting, parsing, exact operations, repeatable tasks) produces unreliable results.

Every time you ask AI to do something deterministic, there’s a chance it does it wrong.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

AI is bad at determinism. Code is good at it. Use the right tool for the job.

Don’t ask AI to do deterministic work. Ask AI to write code that does it.

Then you run that code every time you need it - reliable and repeatable.

Use AI to explore. Use code to repeat.

  1. Identify the deterministic task (counting, parsing, repeating exact operation)
  2. Ask AI to write a script for it
  3. Test and tweak the script until it works how you want
  4. Run the script whenever you need it

AI figures out the tricky bits (Puppeteer config, parsing logic, edge cases). You get reliable, repeatable execution.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Counting: Don’t ask AI to count Rs in “strawberry” - it’s bad at counting. Instead: Ask it to write bash - you’ll get something like this that you can test and that is deterministic: echo "strawberry" | grep -o "r" | wc -l

Capturing screenshots: Need to update app screenshot frequently. Don’t ask AI each time to “take screenshot using Puppeteer” - unreliable.

Instead: “Make me a shell script to capture screenshot at iPhone dimensions.” AI figures out Puppeteer, dimensions, error handling. Script fails first try - puppeteer not installed. AI adds Safari fallback. Now you have capture_screenshot.sh. Run it anytime - reliable every time.

Converting diagrams: Mermaid → DrawIO conversion needed repeatedly. Gave AI examples, it wrote conversion code. Tweaked colors and formatting. Now every update is quick and reliable.

Making tools makes you more capable. AI helps you create the tools quickly. Code makes them reliable.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Non-Determinism
  • Similar to: Chain of Small Steps

Orchestrator

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Dedicated agent that monitors background work, integrates changes, resolves conflicts, runs tests, updates main trunk.

While background agents work on individual tasks, orchestrator handles integration work autonomously.

Workflow:

  1. Monitor: Track progress of background agents/branches
  2. Integrate: Merge completed branches to main
  3. Resolve: Handle merge conflicts
  4. Verify: Run tests, check CI
  5. Update: Keep main trunk healthy

Orchestrator is a focused agent running in feedback loop - specialized for integration work.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Orchestrator monitors 3 background agents:

Why this works:

  • Focused agent: only handles integration (follows ground rules reliably)
  • Feedback loop: autonomous checking and acting
  • Runs locally: needs more privileges (git merge, push to main)

Collaboration with Background Agent: Background agents produce isolated changes. Orchestrator integrates them. Together they enable parallel work streams without blocking main thread.

Tools:

  • gh CLI for monitoring PR/branch status
  • Git for merging and conflict resolution
  • Test runners for verification

Parallel Implementations

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI is non-deterministic - like rolling dice. You want a three, but probably won’t get it on the first try.

What do you do? Roll five dice.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Run multiple implementations in parallel from the same checkpoint:

  1. Create checkpoint (save plan + git commit)
  2. Fork into parallel working directories (use git worktrees or similar)
  3. Launch multiple AI implementations simultaneously
  4. Review all results
  5. Pick the best or combine elements from multiple attempts

This is trading tokens (relatively cheap) for your time (expensive).

Two complementary modes:

  • Failure Mitigation: Complex feature, uncertain approach. Run 3-5 parallel attempts. Some fail, some succeed. You move forward immediately instead of debugging sequential failures.
  • Exploration of the Solution Space: When quality matters more than speed. Generate multiple working versions, compare approaches, combine the best ideas. Works especially well for creative work: UIs, game mechanics, designs.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Game development (Ricochet Robot): Ran three parallel implementations. First version: no walls, robot didn’t move - total failure. Second: mediocre. Third: movement logic worked great, loved the button styling. Combined the working movement with the better buttons.

UI design: Run several parallel implementations. One has great layout, another has clever responsive breakpoints, third has interesting color scheme. Borrow the best from each, combine into richer final design.

Designer collaboration: Designer creates mockup in Figma separately. Run parallel AI implementations. Combine designer’s vision with AI’s working implementations.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Sunk Cost
  • Leverages: Non-Determinism
  • Similar to: Take All Paths, Happy to Delete

Playgrounds

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Need a safe space for AI to experiment, test assumptions, and explore libraries without affecting production code or committing throwaway experiments.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Allow it to experiment when it gets stuck.

For example, create an isolated playground folder (can make it .gitignored) where AI can:

  • Test library behaviors and discover constraints
  • Validate assumptions about APIs
  • Try different approaches without consequences
  • Build proof-of-concepts before real implementation and gain valuable learning

When AI gets stuck or when working with new libraries or uncommon languages:

  • Pull back from complex debugging
  • Have AI experiment in playground
  • Test assumptions directly
  • Discover library constraints quickly

Example

Section titled “Example”

Building chess app, nothing working. Instead of debugging through UI → components → chess.js integration, stopped and had AI write playground script to test chess.js directly.

Quickly discovered: chess.js requires kings on board. Would have taken much longer debugging from the top down.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Perfect Recall Fallacy
  • Addresses: Hallucinations, Degrades Under Complexity
  • Similar to: Feedback Loop, Happy to Delete

Polyglot AI

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Supplement text with other channels - AI can use voice and images both ways.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Use the right modality for the task.

Voice changes quality of life - speak naturally, get responses while hands are busy. Modern voice recognition is so good it’s like effortless typing. Additionally, you’re speaking into AI which is very good at interpreting typos and misspellings - so it makes you more powerful.

Images work both ways - AI reads screenshots, diagrams, designs. Show AI a mockup, get implementation. Show a bug screenshot, get diagnosis.

Pick the channel that feels most natural for what you’re doing.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Debugging UI: Screenshot → AI spots the alignment issue Design implementation: Figma export → AI writes the CSS Hands busy cooking: Voice → AI reads you the next steps Quick code review: Share screen → AI sees what you see

Reference Docs

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

You have knowledge you need sometimes, but not always. Loading it into ground rules would bloat context and dilute focus.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

On-demand knowledge documents. Load them only when you need them for the current task.

Unlike ground rules (always loaded), you explicitly pull in references when relevant. This keeps context focused and gives you granular control over what’s loaded.

Use this when…

  • You’re about to do work with high “house style” requirements (TDD, logging standards, security constraints, API conventions).
  • You notice the agent “guessing” (hallucinating APIs, inventing conventions, or using the wrong internal abstractions).
  • You need consistency across sessions without stuffing everything into AGENTS.md.

Steps

Section titled “Steps”
  1. Create a small, focused reference doc for one topic (single responsibility).
  2. When starting a task, attach or point the agent to the specific reference doc(s) that apply.
  3. During implementation, treat the doc as the “source of truth” for conventions and decisions.
  4. After the task, update the reference doc with what you learned (so the next run is smoother).

Cursor note (native implementation of this pattern)

Section titled “Cursor note (native implementation of this pattern)”

Cursor has a built-in rules system that can serve as a powerful “reference docs” mechanism:

  • Project rules live in .cursor/rules/*.mdc (version-controlled), and can be scoped via path globs / relevance, with support for nested .cursor/rules/ directories.
  • User rules can be set globally via Cursor Settings → Rules.
  • Cursor also supports AGENTS.md (plain markdown) as a simpler alternative to project rules.

Example

Section titled “Example”
  • bash-standards.md - load only when writing bash scripts
  • tdd.process.md - load only when doing TDD
  • architecture.md - load when you need to quickly explain architecture of your project

Build a library of these. Pull them in as needed.

Notes on “grounding”

Section titled “Notes on “grounding””
  • Reference Docs are “curated grounding”: you choose a trusted doc and load it.
  • If you need fresh vendor/library docs, pair this with JIT Docs (real-time retrieval via web search and retrieval tools).

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Perfect Recall Fallacy, Distracted Agent
  • Addresses: Context Rot, Limited Focus, Hallucinations
  • Enables: Knowledge Composition
  • Used by: Context Management

Refinement Loop

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI’s first attempt at any goal is rarely optimal. One pass isn’t enough, and it’s often hard to see deeper when surface problems obscure the core.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Give AI a specific improvement goal and loop it:

  1. Define your goal (distill, improve quality, simplify, understand deeper - can be many things!)
  2. AI makes one pass toward that goal. Aiming for the simplest one currently visible works really well
  3. Next pass builds on previous, goes deeper
  4. Repeat until there’s nothing noticeable to improve

Each iteration removes a layer of noise, making the next layer visible.

Use external artifacts (files/commits) to make iteration real rather than pretend.

Use this when… you know the direction is right, but the result is still rough (unclear writing, messy code, too much complexity, shaky naming).

Why This Works

Section titled “Why This Works”

One small goal per iteration keeps AI focused. Each pass removes one layer of problems. Looping this (like Feedback Flip, but generalized to any goal) compounds the improvement until there’s nothing visible to improve.

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Llewellyn Falco’s refactoring process consistently produces better code quickly.

What makes it work really well:

  • Focus on the simplest visible refactoring each time.
  • Commit after each change with passing tests.
  • The process file acts like a todo list, keeping AI on track.
  • Each simple fix removes noise, revealing the next improvement hidden before.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Uses: Feedback Loop, Knowledge Checkpoint
  • Similar: Chain of Small Steps, Feedback Flip

Reminders

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI forgets your priorities. Ground rules get ignored in long conversations. Important steps get skipped. Your critical requirements drift out of focus as AI works.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

AI has recency bias - it values what you told it recently more than what you said earlier. Force attention on what matters through repetition and structure. Make compliance structural, not optional.

TODOs

Section titled “TODOs”

Turn complex work into explicit checkboxes. AI checks off each step.

Simple structure → reliable execution. Lightweight way to keep the agent following instructions much more reliably.

Instruction Sandwich

Section titled “Instruction Sandwich”

Builds on TODOs. Repeat critical instructions as explicit steps where they matter.

Don’t say “Remember to test!” and hope it sticks.

Instead, add “Run tests” as individual steps, repeating at all key points:

This is ~95% more reliable than just telling AI once.

User Reminders

Section titled “User Reminders”

Inject critical rules into every message. Trading tokens for compliance.

  • Automated (hooks): Inject automatically via hooks on every user prompt. Don’t recommend more than 5 reminders maximum to avoid context rot and distracting the agent. Example: https://github.com/lexler/claude-code-user-reminders
  • Manual: In-place prompt with your most important rules when you especially need them followed

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Selective Hearing, Context Rot
  • Uses: Hooks

Reverse Direction

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Monologue has inertia. Once you’re telling AI what to do, you keep telling. Once AI is asking you questions, it keeps asking.

That inertia makes you miss chances where switching to dialogue would surface better options.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Break the inertia — flip the direction at key moments:

  • AI asks you to decide → “What do you think would work better?”
  • You’re stuck telling → “What questions do you have?”
  • You’re deciding alone → “Show me a few approaches”

The reversal turns monologue into dialogue. Surfaces options you wouldn’t have considered and makes preferences clearer by comparison.

When you catch yourself about to answer AI’s question, pause. Ask what it thinks or could suggest instead.

Instead of an order taker, you get a collaborator. You’re using AI’s strengths (breadth, fast generation) and yours (quick scanning, clear preferences and more ideas once you see some options).

Example

Section titled “Example”

File renaming:

  • You: “Rename this file”
  • AI: “What should I rename it to?”
  • You reverse: “Can you think of some good names?”
  • AI shows options with reasoning.
  • You see different options and that might give you more ideas or show to you quickly what you don’t like

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Answer Injection
  • Counters: Compliance Bias
  • Similar to: Active Partner, Check Alignment

Risk-Adjusted Autonomy

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

The same autonomy setting gets used for wildly different tasks—“clean up this toy repo” and “touch infra scripts and deployment manifests.” Combined with prompt injection risks and recent IDE/agent security findings, this makes AI-driven workflows fragile and unsafe by default.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Autonomy is a dial bound to risk and environment, not an aesthetic choice.

Define clear autonomy levels and bind them explicitly to task type and environment. Treat this as part of your process and AGENTS.md, not a per-session whim.

Technique

Section titled “Technique”

1. Name your autonomy levels

Section titled “1. Name your autonomy levels”
  • Manual – assistant only suggests; humans apply changes and run commands.
  • Guided – assistant can edit files / run read-only commands; destructive operations and config changes require explicit confirmation.
  • Semi-Auto – assistant can run tests, format, and make small code edits within a branch, but cannot alter infra, CI, or secrets.
  • Headless / Background – assistant runs autonomously in a sandboxed environment (CI job, temporary branch, container) on a tightly scoped task.

2. Bind levels to context

Section titled “2. Bind levels to context”
  • Scratch / throwaway repo → Guided → Semi-Auto.
  • Product feature branches → Guided by default; Semi-Auto only for low-risk chores (lint-fixing, snapshot updates).
  • Infra / deployment / IAM / data-migration scripts → Manual or Guided only.
  • CI sandboxes (“fix lints”, “update generated docs”) → Semi-Auto or Headless with restricted credentials.

3. Encode policy in instruction files and config

Section titled “3. Encode policy in instruction files and config”
  • In AGENTS.md, document per-directory expectations, e.g.:
    • “Treat infra/ and deploy/ as high-risk: never run shell commands or modify files here without human confirmation.”
  • For tools that support project rules (e.g. .cursor/rules/*.mdc), scope rules so sensitive directories receive stricter prompts or outright deny certain actions.
  • Use Hooks / Habit Hooks to block or warn on risky operations outside approved contexts.

When It Helps

Section titled “When It Helps”
  • You want “Turbo” / autofix capabilities for low-risk domains without risking real machines or production pipelines.
  • Security/compliance teams need a straight answer to “What can the agent do where?”

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Builds on: Hooks, Habit Hooks, Background Agent, Orchestrator.
  • Counteracts: Over-Trusted Autopilot.

Secret-Safe Agent

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Agents operate over whatever context they can see: files, buffers, logs, conversation. Without explicit fences, they’ll read .env files, SSH keys, production URLs, and even raw customer data if it’s nearby—and may leak those into prompts or external calls. Incident reports and best-practice guides all warn against pasting secrets into assistants or letting them sweep secret-bearing paths into context.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Treat secrets and sensitive data as “non-context” by default. Use secret stores and ignore lists so agents can use secrets indirectly without ever seeing their raw values.

Key Techniques

Section titled “Key Techniques”

Fence off sensitive files and directories

Section titled “Fence off sensitive files and directories”
  • Add .env*, secrets/, key material, kubeconfigs, and similar files to your agent’s ignore config (and .gitignore if appropriate).
  • Ensure instruction systems (like .cursor/rules/*.mdc) don’t implicitly scope over secret directories.

Use named secrets, not pasted values

Section titled “Use named secrets, not pasted values”
  • Provide secrets via your usual mechanism (cloud secret manager, env vars, local keychain).
  • In conversations, refer to them by name: “Use the DB_PASSWORD secret from the environment” instead of pasting the literal password.

Make “no-go” rules explicit in AGENTS.md

Section titled “Make “no-go” rules explicit in AGENTS.md”

Add a short “Secrets & Data” section, for example:

  • “Never print, log, or echo secret values.”
  • “Never commit .env* or files under secrets/.”
  • “Use fabricated test data for examples; do not use real customer records.”

When It Helps

Section titled “When It Helps”

Any repo that touches real users, proprietary systems, or production credentials—which is most non-toy work.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Works with: Ground Rules, Context Management.
  • Mitigates: Supply-Chain Blindness, Hallucinations that might tempt pasting secrets “just to test.”

Semantic Zoom

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Code, logs, docs, books, and articles are frozen at a single written abstraction level. You can’t zoom out for overview or zoom in for details — you’re constrained to whatever resolution the author chose.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Generative AI makes text elastic. You control the level of detail by how you ask and how you steer it. You can expand, collapse, and shift abstraction levels on demand.

Zoom Out — ask for high-level synthesis

  • “Give me the high-level architecture of this codebase”
  • “Summarize the main components and how they interact”
  • “Make this paragraph much shorter”

Zoom In — interrogate specifics interactively

  • “How does authentication work here?”
  • “Show me the implementation of this flow”
  • “What edge cases does this function handle?”

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Exploring codebases: Opening Kafka repository: First ask for high-level architecture overview with ASCII diagrams. Once oriented, zoom into specific areas: “How does the consumer group rebalancing work?” AI researches and explains the details.

Research papers: Start with chapter-level summary, then zoom into one experiment’s methodology. Ask questions when something is unclear.

Refactoring: Extremely powerful as one of the steps during refactoring:

  • ask a coding agent to explain your code in English
  • force to the right level of abstraction by zooming in or out and tuning the details out until English is extremely clear
  • then ask AI to align code with the English

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Addresses: Excess Verbosity
  • Similar to: Noise Cancellation
  • Used by: Context Management

Shared Canvas

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Domain experts and developers speak different languages. Designers think in visuals, stakeholders in outcomes, SMEs in domain logic. Code creates a wall that excludes non-developers from meaningful contribution.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Text and markdown files work as a shared canvas. All humans—and AI—can collaborate in it.

Use markdown files as:

  • Specs both can refine
  • Documentation both can update
  • Plans both can modify
  • Knowledge both can expand

Version control tracks changes. Both partners contribute. Voice inputs empower contribution further—AI tools with speech-to-text, meeting transcripts converted to markdown, dictation into docs—lowering the barrier for anyone to participate.

Why It Works

Section titled “Why It Works”

Markdown is AI-native: every tool reads and writes it fluently. It’s also human-native: no IDE required, no syntax to learn. This makes it the natural meeting point for cross-functional collaboration.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Feature planning with domain expert: Grab your knowledge expert and talk through the feature together with AI. Have AI sketch the solution into a spec. Expert reviews and adds domain constraints—perhaps by voice, dictating notes that AI transcribes. Developer adds technical considerations. AI refines based on both inputs. Everyone contributes to the same artifact—no wall of code in between.

Task tracking: Create todo.md with checkboxes. AI checks them off as it works. You can see progress at a glance, reorder priorities, or add new items. The file becomes the shared state, not the chat.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Uses: Knowledge Checkpoint, Text-Native
  • Enables: Polyglot AI (voice/transcription as input)
  • Solves: Context Rot (decisions persist in file, not chat)

Show the Agent, Let it Repeat/Automate

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

AI’s non-deterministic nature means it interprets the same task differently each time. Without codified knowledge, you waste time explaining the same mistakes repeatedly, especially for tasks that require multiple iterations (like migrations, refactoring patterns, or complex chores).

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Turn collaborative work into either reusable knowledge for AI-assisted repetition or full automation:

  1. Identify a task that will be repeated multiple times
  2. First iteration: Work through it together with the AI
  3. Document: Ask the AI to “Document how we [task description] as a process file in [filename.md]. Focus on things you wish you had known before starting the task. The audience is future AI agents working on similar tasks.”
  4. Second iteration: AI attempts the task using the documentation while you correct mistakes
  5. Refine: Ask the AI to “Refine the document based on things you wish you’d known when starting”
  6. Repeat refinement cycles until AI can work independently
  7. Automate (optional): For mechanical steps, ask AI to identify and automate them, potentially reducing required context window or eliminating AI involvement entirely

The documentation becomes a living knowledge base that captures effective patterns, common gotchas, and context-specific constraints. Automation can transform AI-assisted processes into self-running systems.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Example 1: AI-Assisted Repetition (Database Migration)

Section titled “Example 1: AI-Assisted Repetition (Database Migration)”

Task: Migrating legacy MongoDB application to SQL in a messy codebase where one-shot prompting consistently failed.

Process:

  • First migration done collaboratively, discovering test writing patterns, preferred migration tools, and typical oversights from poor code quality
  • Created purpose-built testing and migration infrastructure together
  • Documented the complete process including tool usage, test examples, and gotchas
  • Second migration attempted by AI with corrections
  • Documentation refined based on lessons learned
  • Result: AI completed all subsequent migrations independently except the first two

Example 2: Full Automation (Tax Processing)

Section titled “Example 2: Full Automation (Tax Processing)”

Task: Processing invoices for tax reporting (non-coding task).

Process:

  • Showed coding agent the manual process of handling invoices
  • AI identified mechanical steps that could be automated
  • Created automation: send invoices to an email address → AI sorts, parses, and generates necessary reports
  • Result: Eliminated need for AI involvement in routine execution; process runs automatically

Benefits of automation path:

  • Significantly reduces or eliminates required context window
  • Transforms AI-assisted process into self-running system
  • Frees AI for more complex, judgment-requiring tasks

Generic task descriptions for documentation:

  • “migrated this MongoDB collection to a Postgres database”
  • “refactored this part of the system to hexagonal architecture”
  • “extracted this legacy component into a microservice”

Slash-Command Playbook

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

You discover good prompts and workflows (“fix this GitHub issue end-to-end,” “TDD this file,” “explain this refactor plan”) but re-typing or reconstructing them each time is error-prone and inconsistent. Different developers reinvent slightly different prompts for the same task, so agents behave unpredictably.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Turn your most valuable prompts into reusable command templates—slash-commands, snippets, or short “plays” that run a known workflow with parameters.

Key Techniques

Section titled “Key Techniques”

Capture the workflow once, then template it

Section titled “Capture the workflow once, then template it”
  • After guiding an assistant through a complex but repeatable flow (e.g. “diagnose failing CI job and propose a fix”), ask it to turn that dialog into a general recipe with explicit placeholders ($ISSUE_NUMBER, $TARGET_FILE, etc.).
  • Save that recipe into AGENTS.md or a small commands/ directory so it’s versioned with the repo.

Expose as commands / aliases

Section titled “Expose as commands / aliases”

Depending on the tool, this might be:

  • A slash command (/tdd-file path/to/file.ts).
  • A snippet / macro in your editor.
  • A simple shell wrapper that feeds a standard system prompt to a CLI assistant.

Encourage the team to reuse and evolve these plays rather than freestyle each time.

Iterate on the play, not just the prompt

Section titled “Iterate on the play, not just the prompt”
  • When a workflow misbehaves, update the shared template instead of tweaking it ad-hoc for one person.
  • This turns “prompt engineering” into workflow engineering.

Example

Section titled “Example”

A team formalizes three common plays:

  • /tdd-file <path> – enforce a strict TDD loop on a file, guided by your TDD process doc.
  • /bug-triage <issue-url> – read linked issue + relevant code, propose minimal reproduction steps and candidate fixes.
  • /refactor-safe <path> – use the SLIM recipe (seams, learning tests, incremental changes, mocks) to refactor a gnarly module.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Builds on: Show the Agent, Let it Repeat/Automate, Extract Knowledge, Knowledge Document.
  • Supports “Connect your universe” behavior by making well-known workflows callable from Jira/GitHub/CI/etc.

SLIM (Refactoring Recipe)

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Refactoring legacy code with agents is risky. Agents can easily break hidden dependencies or introduce subtle bugs when rewriting large chunks of code (“Unvalidated Leaps”).

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Use the SLIM recipe for safe, agentic refactoring:

  1. Seams: Ask the agent to identify or create “seams” — places where you can isolate the code you want to change (e.g., extract an interface, create a facade).
  2. Learning Tests: Before changing anything, have the agent write “learning tests” (characterization tests) that assert the current behavior of the code, even if it’s buggy. This locks in the baseline.
  3. Incremental Changes: Make small, verified moves. Don’t rewrite the whole module. Replace one method at a time.
  4. Mocking: Use mocks at the seams to isolate the code under test, allowing the agent to work fast without spinning up the whole environment.

Why It Works

Section titled “Why It Works”

It constrains the agent’s creativity to a safe process. Instead of “Refactor this class,” you say “Create a seam for method X,” then “Write a test for method X,” then “Optimize method X.”

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Solves: Unvalidated Leaps
  • Addresses: Degrades Under Complexity, Limited Context Window
  • Uses: Chain of Small Steps

Softest Prototype

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Code constrains too early. You don’t know what you need until you’ve built it. But building takes time, and wrong decisions are expensive to change.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

AI + files is softer than software. Use AI as a flexible agent with markdown instructions instead of code. Discover what you need by using it, not by planning it. Very powerful when you’re not sure what you need.

The “system” is just:

  • Markdown files with instructions
  • AI reading and following them
  • Conversation to adjust as you go

Shape the solution while using it. Pivot instantly. No compile, no refactor. Allows exploring solution space by using it before you write a single line of code.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Zork AI: Instead of coding game integration, used tmux + AI tools. Discovered needs (how to do map, memory) while playing.

Review workflow: Markdown inputs → AI processing → formatted output. System emerged through use.

The power: You’re running before you know what you’re building. Can validate approach and learn about the solution space.

Take All Paths

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Prototyping used to be expensive, so we’d only try one or two approaches. We’d pick the “most likely” solution and hope it worked. Better alternatives stayed unexplored.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Prototyping is now so cheap that you can try all paths. Build 10 variations, not 1. Test them all. Pick the best. Learn as you’re prototyping. With AI, a working prototype costs almost nothing. You can build and test multiple prototypes per day. Actually feel how each one works instead of imagining it.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Claude Code’s todo list: 20 prototypes in 2 days

  • Inline progress vs drawer vs pill vs spinner
  • Each variation fully functional, tested with users
  • Found the best through exploration, not guesswork

UX decisions: Instead of mockups, build 10 working versions. Play with all of them (can also borrow behaviors and combine)

The power: You’re no longer limited to your first idea. Explore the whole solution space.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Leverages: Non-Determinism
  • Counters: Solution Fixation, Majority Solution Bias
  • Similar to: Parallel Implementations, Happy to Delete

Text Native

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

We habitually reach for specialized tools - databases for data, UIs for design, diagrams for architecture. These create barriers: tool switching, format conversion, access friction.

Pattern

Section titled “Pattern”

Text is everything now. Stay in text.

Text is AI’s native medium. When you stay in text:

  • Everything is at your fingertips - directly editable
  • No barriers, no tool switching
  • Instant iteration
  • Version-controlled by default
  • Shared workspace - both human and AI can edit

What can be text:

  • Design mockups (ASCII)
  • Architecture diagrams (ASCII)
  • Process workflows (markdown)
  • Data specifications (markdown tables)
  • Plans and todos (markdown)
  • State descriptions (plain text)

If it can be text, make it text.

Example

Section titled “Example”

UI redesign staying entirely in text:

Designer draws on paper → AI converts to ASCII → edit ASCII together → describe in text → compare old vs new state (both text) → generate todos from diff → implement.

Never left text. No Figma, no drawing tools, no databases. Just editable text files.

Architecture exploration: Ask AI for codebase architecture - get ASCII diagram you can edit and refine together, not a PNG you have to regenerate and open in a different tool.

Process automation: Write processes in markdown. AI reads and follows them. Edit the file, AI adapts immediately.

Text keeps everything lightweight, editable, and accessible.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Enables: Shared Canvas, Knowledge Document
  • Supports: Context Management

Black Box AI

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI’s reasoning is hidden. With code, you can open it up and see what’s there - no mystery. With AI, you only see inputs and outputs, not what’s inside.

AI has something resembling a human mental model, but you can’t inspect it.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Misalignment between your mental model and AI’s goes undetected
  • Hard to even suspect something is wrong

Counter With

Section titled “Counter With”
  • Check Alignment: Explicitly ask “describe the structure you see” or “tell me what you’re about to do”
  • Active Partner: Require interpretation before action to surface the hidden model

Leads To

Section titled “Leads To”
  • Silent Misalignment (when the hidden model diverges from yours)

Cannot Learn

Description

Section titled “Description”

LLMs have two fundamental limitations that prevent further learning:

  • Fixed Weights: The model cannot learn from your interactions. You can’t make it write better code by talking to it, teach it your coding style, or make it remember your preferences through conversation. The only way to change model behavior is through additional training or fine-tuning, which is either unavailable or hard for an individual developer.

  • Statelessness: The model itself has no memory between API calls. What appears as “memory” in AI tools is the tool re-sending all messages in the conversation with each request. The model isn’t remembering, it’s re-reading everything every time.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • You must continually restate context, preferences, and project details. YOU are becoming its memory, and it’s very tiresome
  • Knowledge vanishes when a session resets or starts fresh
  • The model cannot adapt to your codebase or preferences through experience
  • Any lasting memory must be explicitly managed through external means (files, notes, configurations, etc)

Work Within Using

Section titled “Work Within Using”
  • Ground Rules: Encode preferences and standards in project files (.cursor/rules/*.mdc, AGENTS.md) so they’re always present
  • Reference Docs: Create documentation for libraries and patterns you use frequently
  • Knowledge Checkpoint: Save decisions and context to files before they’re lost to session resets

Compliance Bias

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI is deliberately trained to be helpful and compliant above all else. It will say “Sure thing, boss” even when your request makes no sense in its universe.

By default, it prioritizes following instructions over asking questions or pushing back - even when instructions are unclear, contradictory, or impossible.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Accepts nonsensical requests instead of clarifying
  • Attempts impossible tasks rather than explaining limitations
  • Silently misinterprets rather than questioning your assumptions
  • Creates elaborate workarounds instead of suggesting better approaches
  • Combined with Black Box AI, makes misalignment invisible until things break

Counter With

Section titled “Counter With”
  • Active Partner: Explicitly give permission to push back, ask questions, and flag contradictions
  • Reverse Direction: Flip the conversation—“What would you choose?” or “Show me options”—to break the compliance loop
  • Ground Rules: Add instructions like “Ask questions when unclear” and “Tell me if my instructions don’t make sense”

Leads To

Section titled “Leads To”
  • Silent Misalignment, Tell Me a Lie, Answer Injection (when not addressed)

Context Rot

Description

Section titled “Description”

Context degrades as the conversation grows. The model stops following earlier instructions, and performance drops unpredictably This happens long before you hit the context window limit.

Context doesn’t decay evenly; it fades in zones:

  • Focus zone: Instructions followed reliably
  • Effective context: Still usable but weakening. Feels productive, yet earlier guidance is starting to be ignored or de-prioritized
  • Red zone: Past instructions are routinely lost or contradicted.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Earlier instructions lose influence as conversation progresses
  • The same question may yield very different results later in the thread
  • You can’t rely on what the model will remember or follow
  • Forces frequent resets to maintain quality

Work Within Using

Section titled “Work Within Using”
  • Context Surface: Start fresh sessions per phase; reset when thread feels “foggy”
  • Knowledge Checkpoint: Save critical decisions to files before they rot out of context
  • Context Management: Curate what stays in context; remove what’s no longer needed

Degrades Under Complexity

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI struggles with complex, multi-step tasks that require holding many moving pieces in mind simultaneously. Reliability degrades as complexity increases, either through larger steps or larger artifacts. Small errors accumulate, and quality drops when working with large codebases or long documents.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Complex requests often fail or produce incorrect results
  • Effectiveness degrades with the size of the codebase
  • AI loses track of requirements, constraints, or context
  • Multi-step transformations attempted in one shot are especially error-prone
  • Small errors accumulate, code quality and system reliability degrade rapidly

Work Within Using

Section titled “Work Within Using”
  • Chain of Small Steps: Break complex tasks into small, verifiable pieces
  • Chunking: Divide large artifacts into manageable chunks
  • Orchestrator: Coordinate multiple focused agents rather than one overloaded agent
  • Playgrounds: Isolate complex experiments; spike small, delete freely

Excess Verbosity

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI is a token machine - verbose by default, often overwhelming you with unnecessary detail, filler, and redundancy.

The level of detail you get by default when talking to it rarely matches your actual level of curiosity.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Overwhelming - hard to scan responses and find what matters
  • Low signal-to-noise ratio makes everything harder to maintain and trust
  • Makes it hard for a human to follow and makes a human likely to tune out
  • Knowledge documents accumulate bloat over time (document rot)

Counter With

Section titled “Counter With”
  • Ground Rules: Set explicit verbosity expectations (“be terse”, “bullet points only”, “no preamble”)
  • SLIM: Favor concise artifacts that respect the reader’s time

Hallucinations

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI makes up APIs, methods, or syntax that don’t exist. It will confidently reference functions, parameters, or library features that were never real.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”

A minor inconvenience because code is self-verifiable. Unlike factual hallucinations, code hallucinations are self-revealing—the code won’t compile or run.

Work Within Using

Section titled “Work Within Using”
  • Feedback Loop: Let tests and compilation catch hallucinations automatically
  • Playgrounds: Experiment with unfamiliar libraries in isolation where hallucinations are quickly exposed
  • Reference Docs: Provide accurate, curated documentation so the AI doesn’t have to guess
  • JIT Docs: Ground the agent in current vendor/library docs via real-time retrieval (web search / retrieval tools)

Keeping up

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI coding agents generate code and take actions faster than humans can review and understand it. The code is often verbose, and attempting to thoroughly understand every change becomes a bottleneck. The AI isn’t optimized for bringing humans along - it focuses on speed and quantity of generation over comprehensibility.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”

Engineers face a choice between speed and understanding. Since AI-generated code is typically lower quality than human-written code, the time saved on creation is lost on verification. As a result the potential productivity gain is not realized - or worse, the engineer would have been faster without AI. The temptation is to skip review entirely, leading to codebases where nobody understands what the AI built.

Counter With

Section titled “Counter With”
  • Active Partner: Stay engaged; require explanation before accepting changes
  • Feedback Loop: Automate verification so you don’t have to manually review everything
  • Constrained Tests: Let tests catch issues without requiring line-by-line review

Leads To

Section titled “Leads To”
  • Flying Blind (when the temptation to skip review wins)

Limited Context Window

Description

Section titled “Description”

Context has a fixed size limit. Once the limit is reached, older content has to be dropped or summarized by coding assistant to make room for new input.

Everything you put in context competes for this limited space.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Long conversations eventually lose early messages
  • Large files can’t be loaded whole
  • Everything loaded (code, docs, ground rules, conversation history) fights for the same limited space
  • Forces choices about what to keep in context and what to leave out

Work Within Using

Section titled “Work Within Using”
  • Context Surface: Curate what the agent sees; treat context as a managed surface, not a dump
  • SLIM: Keep artifacts concise; less text means more room for what matters
  • Focused Agent: Narrow scope per session so less needs to be loaded

Limited Focus

Description

Section titled “Description”

LLMs have limited attention. Everything you load into context competes for that attention.

When too much is loaded at once, the model either:

  • Dilutes attention across everything (stays shallow)
  • Fixates on the wrong parts (misses what matters)

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Worse performance on all tasks when context is too broad
  • Even explicit ground rules get ignored
  • A longer, focused context outperforms a shorter, scattered one

Work Within Using

Section titled “Work Within Using”
  • Context Surface: Keep 3-5 files loaded at once; close when done
  • Focused Agent: Single, narrow responsibility per session
  • Chain of Small Steps: One task at a time, not many tasks competing for attention

Majority Solution Bias

Description

Section titled “Description”

Models emit the most common pattern from training data, not the best one for your context. The first answer looks plausible but mediocre—a probabilistic echo of what’s most frequent on GitHub.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Insecure defaults: unsanitized inputs, naive auth, weak error handling
  • Over-use of generic libraries when your codebase has better abstractions
  • Design drift toward “what’s common” rather than your architecture

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Feeds into: Solution Fixation
  • Counter with: Borrow Behaviors, Ground Rules, Check Alignment, Take All Paths
  • Catch with: Feedback Loop (e.g., static / security scans after feature work)

Non-Determinism

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI outputs are non-deterministic. The same input may yield different results across runs. Responses can vary in quality: sometimes worse, sometimes better.

This isn’t a bug—it’s how AI works.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”

Unlike deterministic systems where same input always produces same output:

  • Results are not guaranteed to be repeatable
  • Reliability is unpredictable—retries may diverge significantly

Don’t Fight It

Section titled “Don’t Fight It”

Embrace variation as an exploration tool, not an obstacle to overcome.

Leverage With

Section titled “Leverage With”
  • Parallel Implementations: Run multiple attempts from the same checkpoint; compare and select the best
  • Take All Paths: Use variation to surface ideas you wouldn’t have thought of
  • Happy to Delete: When quality stalls, delete and retry—you might get a better result

Obedient Contractor

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI behaves like an obedient contractor hired for a single day—focused on completing the immediate task and leaving as quickly as possible. This manifests in two key ways:

Short-term mindset: AI prioritizes getting the job done right now over long-term maintainability. It works with existing code and finds the quickest solution rather than considering how small improvements could benefit both immediate and future work.

Excessive politeness: AI treats contradicting the original request as very impolite. It won’t push back even when it should, instead working within given constraints rather than questioning whether those constraints make sense.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”

This contractor mentality produces several harmful effects:

  • Technical debt accumulation: Quick fixes chosen over sustainable solutions that require slightly more upfront effort
  • Missed opportunities: AI doesn’t speak up about better approaches it could see, assuming you want exactly what you asked for
  • Literal minimum effort: AI may change test assertions to match incorrect results rather than fixing the actual source of the problem
  • Happy path focus: Edge cases and subtle bugs get left behind in the rush to satisfy the main request
  • Ignoring implicit needs: AI addresses only what’s explicitly spelled out in the prompt, missing context and unstated requirements
  • Suboptimal solutions: The delivered code satisfies the literal request but misses the underlying need
  • Maintenance burden: Code works now but becomes progressively harder to maintain over time

The AI acts as if it will never see this code again, making decisions that create problems for whoever (including itself in future conversations) has to work with the code later.

Counter With

Section titled “Counter With”
  • Active Partner: Require the agent to consider long-term implications, not just immediate completion
  • Ground Rules: Encode standards for maintainability, edge cases, and when to push back on quick fixes

Productivity Illusion

Also known as: Velocity Trap

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI coding assistants increase visible activity: more code, more suggestions, more PRs. Teams often interpret this as pure productivity gain. But controlled studies on experienced developers show the opposite can happen: in a 2025 METR RCT, seasoned open-source maintainers using AI tools took about 19% longer on familiar tasks than without AI, even though they felt ~20% faster.

The gap comes from time spent reviewing, correcting, and integrating AI output, plus context-switching and over-experimentation. Developers and managers end up celebrating throughput metrics (LOC, number of PRs, “assistant activity”) even as end-to-end delivery and defect rates get worse.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Teams overestimate the benefits of AI based on activity metrics (PR count, diff size, time “typing”) instead of outcome metrics (lead time, reliability, incidents).
  • Experienced engineers especially may lose time to reviewing mediocre suggestions, debugging subtle regressions, and fighting the assistant on code they could have written faster themselves.
  • Organizations adopt “AI everywhere” policies, pushing assistants into high-expertise inner loops where they add overhead rather than leverage.
  • Creates pressure to skip review and testing to “keep up” with perceived AI-accelerated throughput, reinforcing Flying Blind.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Exacerbates: Keeping Up, Flying Blind.
  • Mitigated by: Feedback Loop, Constrained Tests, Happy to Delete, Parallel Implementations, and by tracking outcome metrics rather than raw velocity.

Selective Hearing (Obstacle)

Description

Section titled “Description”

Even after narrowing scope and pruning ground rules to essentials, AI still ignores certain instructions unpredictably.

AI filters based on what it deems important (learned from training), not what you mark important. Your instructions compete against billions of examples in its training data and its attention span.

This is unfixable via just prompting. No amount of CAPS, bold, or “IMPORTANT!!!” will override AI’s attention filters.

Root Causes

Section titled “Root Causes”

1. Contradicting the majority of its training data

Section titled “1. Contradicting the majority of its training data”

When your instructions clash with deeply ingrained patterns (like “don’t comment code” vs millions of commented examples), the training wins. The AI cannot hear you over the roar of its training data.

2. Attention overload (distracted agent)

Section titled “2. Attention overload (distracted agent)”

As tasks grow complex, AI’s attention narrows to the main goal. Your ground rules and constraints get filtered out as “less important” - the AI is so focused on solving the problem that it has no attention for your additional guidance. Your constraints get filtered out as “noise” as all available attention flows to the main task.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Random compliance — instructions work unpredictably
  • Critical constraints vanish mid-task without warning
  • Forces constant vigilance and re-prompting of “simple” rules

Examples

Section titled “Examples”

Fighting training: Tell AI “no comments in code” → it complies briefly → silently reverts because comments are everywhere in its training data.

Attention overload: Asking it to run tests after every refactoring. It initially complies, then as refactoring continues, AI gets absorbed in the code transformation and starts skipping tests - its attention is consumed by the main task.

Work Within Using

Section titled “Work Within Using”

Primary: automated nudges (make compliance structural)

Section titled “Primary: automated nudges (make compliance structural)”
  • Habit Hooks: detect violations (like code comments) and inject targeted “fix this now” prompts at the moment the agent drifts.
  • Hooks: intercept deterministic lifecycle points to inject reminders/validations when the agent is most likely to “filter out” constraints.
  • Reminders: repeat critical constraints as explicit steps (TODOs / instruction sandwiches) so they stay in the agent’s active focus.
  • Feedback Loop: add an external check (tests/linters/logs) and require “keep iterating until green.” This doesn’t fix attention filters, but it does provide a ground-truth correction mechanism that catches drift.

Secondary: reduce the attention burden (make hearing easier — helpful but not reliable in this case)

Section titled “Secondary: reduce the attention burden (make hearing easier — helpful but not reliable in this case)”
  • Noise Cancellation: remove non-essential instructions so the “must-follow” constraints can win attention.
  • Semantic Zoom: restate constraints at the right level for the current step (high-level vs. line-level).
  • Focused Agent: give the agent one narrow job so secondary rules don’t get dropped.
  • Context Surface: keep only the few files/instructions relevant to the current change.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Related: Limited Focus, Limited Context Window, Context Rot

Solution Fixation

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI latches onto the first plausible solution and loses critical thinking. When it finds something that might be the problem, it declares “found it!” and stops exploring alternatives.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Jumps to conclusions without validating root cause
  • Treats hypotheses as facts
  • Misses the actual problem while fixating on red herrings
  • Declares victory prematurely: “This must be it!”
  • Stops being critical or rational once a pattern matches

Counter With

Section titled “Counter With”
  • Take All Paths: Force exploration of multiple hypotheses before committing
  • Parallel Implementations: Try different approaches simultaneously to avoid premature convergence
  • Feedback Loop: Validate solutions against tests before declaring victory

Supply-Chain Blindness

Description

Section titled “Description”

AI coding assistants treat the open ecosystem as a big suggestion pool. They will happily recommend:

  • Arbitrary packages (npm install some-random-lib)
  • Copy-pasted snippets of unknown provenance
  • Outdated, vulnerable, or unmaintained dependencies

They have no built-in notion of your approved dependency set or secure supply chain. Emerging attacks like “slopsquatting” exploit this: models hallucinate plausible but non-existent package names; attackers register those names and ship malware.

Security analyses of AI-generated code also show a high rate of common vulnerabilities (e.g. missing input validation, insecure defaults, hard-coded secrets) baked into suggestions.

Impact

Section titled “Impact”
  • Malicious or risky dependencies can enter the codebase through casual “just install what the assistant suggested” behavior.
  • Inconsistent or unapproved libraries increase maintenance burden and complicate SBOM / SCA enforcement.
  • Insecure boilerplate—SQL built via string concatenation, weak crypto, unsafe defaults—quietly spreads, especially in greenfield vibe-coded projects.

Related

Section titled “Related”
  • Reinforces: Hallucinations, Keeping Up (security reviewers can’t keep pace with AI-driven package churn).
  • Mitigated by: Ground Rules (encode approved dependencies), Secret-Safe Agent, Constrained Tests, external SCA / SBOM tooling.

AI Slop (Anti-pattern)

Problem

Section titled “Problem”

Using AI output without adding human judgment or value. The formula: prompt AI → lightly edit → present as yours.

Two flavors degrading information quality everywhere:

  1. Exploitation slop: Mass-produced content gaming systems for profit
  2. Lazy slop: Zero-effort AI output passed off as original thought

What Goes Wrong

Section titled “What Goes Wrong”
  • Real insights become needles in haystacks
  • The bar for “acceptable” keeps dropping
  • Common spaces become useless, people tune out or leave

Diagnostic

Section titled “Diagnostic”

If someone else could paste your prompt into a model and get essentially the same output, and you can’t point to what you added (judgment, constraints, evidence, experience, verification), you’re probably about to ship slop.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Exploitation: AI-generated foraging guides flood bookstores, confidently mixing deadly mushrooms with edible ones.

Lazy: Having AI write your LinkedIn article, or presenting AI’s analysis as your own insights in a meeting.

Solution

Section titled “Solution”

You can’t stop the flood, but you can choose not to add to it.

The slop test: Could anyone with your prompt get the same result? That’s probably slop.

Add value instead:

  • Use AI as thought partner, not ghost writer
